SCF ID: CFG-02, NET-01, NET-04, PRM-04, PRM-05, SEA-01.1
Description: 1.1 Processes and mechanisms for installing and maintaining network security controls are defined and understood.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive capabilities for defining, implementing, and maintaining network security controls across the infrastructure stack.

VCF develops, documents, and maintains secure baseline configurations consistent with industry-accepted hardening standards, including the VCF Security Baseline, component hardening guides, and DISA Security Technical Implementation Guides (STIGs) with network-specific settings.

NSX, as the networking component, delivers centralized network policy management with role-based access control, allowing for the definition and enforcement of network segmentation, routing, and connectivity rules from a single management plane.

vSphere Distributed Switches provide centralized management of virtual networking, ensuring consistent VLAN configurations and security policies.

VCF Operations includes compliance scanning capabilities that evaluate infrastructure against baseline configurations, helping maintain consistent configurations and detect drift.

vSphere Lifecycle Manager enforces desired-state configuration for hosts, automatically remediating deviations that could weaken network security posture.

VCF Automation maintains Access Control Lists (ACLs) for object-level access control, complementing role-based access controls.

Audit logging captures all policy changes, providing a clear record of modifications to network security controls.


VMware Live Recovery: Contributes | VMware Live Recovery (VLR) provides configurable security baseline settings and documentation for its replication infrastructure, contributing to the definition and maintenance of network security controls within its specific domain. The product's TLS configuration is controlled through parameters that administrators can adjust to enforce stricter TLS requirements, and VLR documents specific network encryption requirements for replication traffic. This provides the technical specifications needed to develop and maintain secure baseline configurations for disaster recovery infrastructure.

The broader organizational processes for defining and understanding comprehensive network security controls, beyond the scope of replication infrastructure, remain a customer responsibility.


VMware vDefend: Contributes | VMware vDefend provides robust technical mechanisms for installing and maintaining network security controls within its domain, including structured configuration management capabilities, predefined security profiles, and configuration integrity controls. The Distributed Firewall supports draft configuration management with locking and comment requirements, while the Gateway Firewall offers predefined TLS Decryption Action Profiles representing vendor-defined baseline configurations.

The Security Services Platform validates configuration changes before implementation and supports backup and restore of configuration data.

vDefend provides the technical enforcement layer for Network Security Control (NSC) implementation, including Distributed Firewall policy management, security policy generation, and publishing to NSX Manager.

Security Intelligence observes traffic flows to generate granular, application-level policy recommendations, supporting the identification of authorized traffic and the move toward least-privilege rule sets.

While vDefend provides the technical capabilities and enforcement, the procedural and governance aspects of developing, updating, and managing NSC procedures, such as change management processes, periodic ACL reviews, and documentation of authorized flows, are organizational responsibilities that vDefend's technical capabilities support but do not fulfill on their own.

Description:
1.1.1 All security policies and operational procedures that are identified in Requirement 1 are: 
 • Documented.
 • Kept up to date.
 • In use.
 • Known to all affected parties.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 1.1.2 Roles and responsibilities for performing activities in Requirement 1 are documented, assigned, and understood.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 1.2 Network security controls (NSCs) are configured and maintained.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation delivers comprehensive capabilities for configuring and maintaining network security controls across the infrastructure stack. NSX, the networking component, provides centralized network policy management with robust role-based access control, enabling delegation of security responsibilities through defined administrative roles such as VPC Admin, Security Admin, and Network Admin. Security policies are logically organized into categories like Infrastructure and Application to prevent conflicts and ensure governance across multiple administrators.

vSphere Distributed Switches centralize virtual networking management, ensuring consistent VLAN configurations and security policies across all hosts. Network segmentation is architecturally embedded, isolating management, vMotion (on non-routable networks), and storage traffic on dedicated networks.

VMware Cloud Foundation publishes security baselines through the Security Configuration Guide and DISA STIGs, which include specific network hardening settings, and vSphere Lifecycle Manager enforces desired-state configuration, automatically detecting and remediating any configuration drift that could compromise network security posture.

NSX VPC extends these controls to multi-tenant environments, allowing tenant administrators to configure their own network and security policies within isolated VPC boundaries, with all policy changes meticulously captured in audit logs detailing the user, module, operation, and result.

VMware vDefend provides the technical enforcement layer underpinning these network security controls, implementing zero-trust microsegmentation via the Distributed Firewall and defense-in-depth through layered L2–L7 controls, including Gateway Firewall, IDS/IPS, malware prevention, URL filtering, and TLS inspection.

The Security Services Platform enables users to generate and centrally publish associated security policies for infrastructure services and environments, supporting the governance of NSC deployment.

The Security Engineer role within the Security Services Platform is formally defined to build, implement, and maintain security policies, supporting role-based separation of duties in security engineering activities.

While VMware Cloud Foundation and vDefend provide the robust technical framework and enforcement mechanisms for network security controls, the organization remains responsible for establishing and maintaining the overarching procedural and governance aspects. This includes developing and updating formal NSC procedures, integrating secure systems engineering governance into the broader organizational SDLC, and defining the specific operational processes for ongoing policy management and incident response.

SCF ID: CLD-01, CFG-02, CFG-02.5, NET-06, NET-08.1, SEA-03
Description:
1.2.1 Configuration standards for NSC rulesets are:
 • Defined.
 • Implemented.
 • Maintained.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) and VMware vDefend provide comprehensive capabilities to ensure configuration standards for network security controller (NSC) rulesets are defined, implemented, and maintained. VCF develops, documents, and maintains secure baseline configurations, including the VCF Security Baseline, component-specific hardening guides, and DISA STIGs, which serve as defined standards. VCF Operations includes compliance scanning capabilities that continuously evaluate infrastructure against these baselines and custom policies, identifying non-compliant objects and providing actionable remediation recommendations. Automated remediation actions can be configured to bring objects back into the expected configuration state, ensuring consistent implementation and maintenance.

VMware vDefend further contributes within its network security domain by providing structured configuration management, predefined security profiles, and configuration integrity controls. The vDefend Gateway Firewall offers vendor-defined baseline configurations through predefined TLS Decryption Action Profiles. The Distributed Firewall enforces microsegmentation policies at the vNIC level, implementing defined standards across workloads. Security Services Platform validates configuration changes before implementation, supports backup and restore of configuration data, and provides a Security Assessment capability to evaluate whether configurations meet expected security standards. Segmentation Monitoring continuously watches traffic for adherence to defined policies, ensuring ongoing maintenance of the security posture.

While VCF and vDefend provide robust technical mechanisms and pre-defined baselines, organizations are responsible for formally adopting, tailoring, and documenting these configuration standards to align with their specific risk posture and compliance requirements. Establishing internal policies and operational procedures for the lifecycle management of these standards, including review cycles and change management processes, is also a customer responsibility.

Description:
1.2.2 All changes to network connections and to configurations of NSCs are approved and managed in accordance with the change control process defined at Requirement 6.5.1.
 Applicability Notes
 Changes to network connections include the addition, removal, or modification of a connection. 
 Changes to NSC configurations include those related to the component itself as well as those affecting how it performs its security function.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust technical controls to ensure changes to network connections and security component configurations are approved and managed.

VCF Automation includes workflow capabilities with approval policies that require project administrators to review changes to deployed catalog instances before they take effect, and integrates with IT Service Management (ITSM) platforms for comprehensive change tracking and approval. Role-based access control across VCF components, NSX Manager, and Kubernetes environments restricts who can initiate configuration changes, enforcing authorization boundaries.

VMware vDefend further enhances this by offering policy locking for Distributed Firewall and Gateway Firewall TLS Inspection policies, preventing unauthorized concurrent modifications, and employs a deployment wizard that conducts thorough validations before implementing any configuration changes. Security Services Platform enforces immutability on published security policies, requiring defined workflows rather than direct edits, and supports staged policy enforcement for controlled transitions.

The platform actively manages configuration integrity through vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) and VCF Operations, which utilize desired-state configuration to maintain consistency, detect configuration drift from approved baselines, and offer automated remediation. VCF Operations tracks each configuration change to the authenticated user or scheduled job, and integrates configuration templates with source control repositories for versioning, auditing, and review-based change control.

VMware Live Recovery defines granular privilege levels that separate change authority across disaster recovery functions, generates audit log events for permission and configuration changes, and provides structured orchestrator workflows and scripting APIs for repeatable and auditable changes. vSphere Replication generates detailed configuration change events, and configuration modifications are performed through structured tools like hms-configtool and the DR IP Customizer, ensuring controlled and documented processes.

Audit logging across all VCF components captures change activity for accountability and review.

While the platform provides extensive technical mechanisms for enforcing approval, managing changes, detecting drift, and maintaining an audit trail, the organization is responsible for defining the overarching change control process (as referenced in Requirement 6.5.1), establishing specific approval workflows, integrating with their chosen ITSM platforms, and assigning human accountability for review and authorization.

Description:
1.2.3 An accurate network diagram(s) is maintained that shows all connections between the CDE and other networks, including any wireless networks.
 Applicability Notes
 A current network diagram(s) or other technical or topological solution that identifies network connections and devices can be used to meet this requirement.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides an end-to-end interactive network map within VCF Operations for Networks, which dynamically displays a detailed model of both physical and virtual devices, including connections between them. This map updates based on collected data, ensuring it reflects the current network topology. Flow Insights further visualizes and analyzes data flows using 5-tuple values, presenting application topology maps that illustrate traffic movement between application tiers, internet endpoints, and shared services. NSX network connectivity workflows and the vSphere Client also offer built-in topology visualizations, correlating configurations with resulting network architectures. The PCI Compliance page in VCF Operations for Networks specifically generates network flow diagrams showing data flows, firewall placement, and connections relevant to security documentation.

VMware vDefend Security Intelligence complements this with graphical visualizations of NSX components, virtual machines, and network traffic flows, which automatically refresh to reflect the current security posture and network state. It provides detailed flow information, including security group relationships and color-coded indicators for protected, unprotected, and blocked traffic. Network Detection and Response (NDR) extends this by visualizing correlated security events and threat activity across the network.

VMware Live Recovery contributes by maintaining detailed network mappings between protected and recovery sites, and provides tools to export networking configurations of protected virtual machines, ensuring that disaster recovery topologies and replication traffic paths are documented.

While these platforms provide robust, dynamic, and accurate technical and topological solutions for identifying network connections and data flows, the organization remains responsible for formally defining the boundaries of the Cardholder Data Environment (CDE) and other networks. The customer must also establish processes to integrate these dynamic technical representations into their formal compliance documentation and governance frameworks, ensuring that the scope of control applicability aligns with organizational policies.

Description:
1.2.4 An accurate data-flow diagram(s) is maintained that meets the following: 
 • Shows all account data flows across systems and networks.
 • Updated as needed upon changes to the environment.
 Applicability Notes
 A data-flow diagram(s) or other technical or topological solution that identifies flows of account data across systems and networks can be used to meet this requirement.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation, VMware Live Recovery, and VMware vDefend collectively provide a robust technical solution for visualizing and documenting data flows across systems and networks, ensuring accuracy and currency.

VCF Operations for Networks offers an interactive network map that dynamically updates to reflect the current physical and virtual topology, alongside Flow Insights for visualizing and analyzing 5-tuple flow data and application topology maps. NSX and vSphere Client provide built-in topology visualizations, and the PCI Compliance page within VCF Operations for Networks generates network flow diagrams.

VMware Live Recovery contributes by maintaining detailed network mappings between protected and recovery sites, including test network configurations, and offers tools to export these networking configurations for virtual machines.

VMware vDefend Security Intelligence generates live, interactive visualizations of network traffic patterns, security group relationships, and firewall rule coverage, automatically refreshing to reflect changes in the environment. It provides detailed flow information, including Layer 7 application IDs and FQDNs, and visually distinguishes between protected, unprotected, and blocked traffic. Network Detection and Response further enhances this with Campaign Blueprint, an interactive graphical representation of correlated security events and threat activity.

These capabilities directly address the requirement for a technical or topological solution that identifies data flows and is updated as needed.

The ultimate responsibility for formally maintaining these data flow diagrams or technical solutions, defining what constitutes "account data" within the organization's context, and ensuring the tools are configured to capture and present all relevant account data flows rests with the customer. The platforms provide the authoritative data and visualization mechanisms, but the organizational process of adopting, validating, and utilizing these outputs as the official record for compliance purposes is a customer obligation.

Description: 1.2.5 All services, protocols and ports allowed are identified, approved, and have a defined business need.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust mechanisms to identify and restrict services, protocols, and ports. ESX hosts incorporate a built-in firewall that controls access to services and ports, supporting specification of allowed hosts and individual service management. Lockdown mode further restricts host access, centralizing management through vCenter. At the network layer, VCF leverages distributed switches with port group security policies and VLANs to control traffic flow and establish communication boundaries. VCF Operations includes Service Discovery to monitor connections, identify listening ports, and map application relationships, providing visibility into active usage. It also offers live data-flow diagramming and an API for recommended firewall rules, aiding in documenting communication patterns. VCF publishes its own port and protocol requirements, serving as a reference for necessary network connectivity.

VMware vDefend extends these capabilities by providing the primary enforcement mechanism for least functionality at the network layer. The vDefend Distributed Firewall operates at the vNIC level, enforcing granular rules that specify allowed ports, protocols, and services, including Layer 2 through Layer 7 filtering based on application identity. Rule optimization analysis identifies overly permissive rules, and governance recommendations explicitly warn against broad allowances.

The Gateway Firewall provides similar allow/drop/reject actions at the perimeter of logical network segments, restricting external access. Security Intelligence visualizes flow details, flags uncommonly used ports, and provides policy recommendations based on observed traffic.

Port Profiler and Server Port Profiler detectors identify unusual or undocumented port usage by any entity, including external service providers. vDefend IDS/IPS inspects traffic for attack signatures, and Network Detection and Response (NDR) correlates events to identify multi-stage attack campaigns, providing continuous monitoring and threat detection for allowed communications.

VMware Live Site Recovery documents its specific network port and protocol requirements, including dedicated VMkernel adapters for replication traffic, providing clear guidelines for necessary connectivity.

While the VMware platforms provide comprehensive technical tools for identifying, restricting, and monitoring services, protocols, and ports, the ultimate responsibility for formal approval, defining the business need for each allowed communication, and maintaining associated documentation rests with the customer. The platforms cannot independently establish organizational policies, contractual requirements for external service providers, or the governance processes necessary to formally justify and approve network access. These aspects require customer-defined governance, risk, and compliance processes to ensure all allowed services, protocols, and ports are formally identified, approved, and have a documented business need.

Description: 1.2.6 Security features are defined and implemented for all services, protocols, and ports that are in use and considered to be insecure, such that the risk is mitigated.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation establishes a robust foundation for defining and implementing security features for services, protocols, and ports. It provides secure baseline configurations through the VCF Security Baseline, component hardening guides, and DISA STIGs.

VCF implements least functionality principles by enabling ESX host firewalls, allowing individual service control (e.g., SSH), enforcing lockdown mode, and supporting the disabling of unexposed features. Network-level controls include distributed switch port group security policies, isolation of management traffic on dedicated networks, and automatic firewall rule configuration for services like vSAN.

VCF Operations supports firewall hardening and collects security properties to ensure consistent configuration. Furthermore, VCF enforces TLS for management API connections and provides mechanisms to mitigate risks from insecure ports and protocols through port group security policies and the removal of unused port groups.

VMware vDefend significantly extends these capabilities by providing granular, application-aware enforcement and active identification of insecure communication.

vDefend explicitly identifies insecure protocols such as HTTP, Telnet, and FTP, tracking workloads exposed to them through metrics like "Workloads without Obsolete OS and Risky Protocols." Its Security Intelligence offers protocol and port-based traffic flow analysis, generating policy recommendations to restrict or exclude insecure port and protocol combinations. The Port Profiler and Server Port Profiler detectors identify anomalous port usage, signaling potential insecure configurations or unauthorized activity.

The vDefend Distributed Firewall operates at the vNIC level, enforcing Layer 2 through Layer 7 rules that permit only essential ports and protocols, effectively microsegmenting workloads to block insecure services. The Gateway Firewall provides similar enforcement at network perimeters.

vDefend also includes IDS/IPS, malware prevention, URL filtering, and TLS inspection as direct security features, and its rule optimization analysis helps eliminate overly permissive rules, ensuring policies align with a least-privilege security model. Intelligent Assist further aids in creating targeted remediation policies to stop attacks, functioning as a technical compensating countermeasure.

Description: 1.2.7 Configurations of NSCs are reviewed at least once every six months to confirm they are relevant and effective.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive capabilities for reviewing technology asset configurations and ensuring adherence to cybersecurity and data protection policies. VCF Operations includes continuous compliance management, monitoring infrastructure against industry-standard security benchmarks and custom policies, and calculating compliance scores for managed objects. It identifies non-compliant objects and associated violations, allowing administrators to review and remediate.

At the host layer, vSphere Configuration Profiles and vSphere Lifecycle Manager assess ESX host configurations against approved baselines, detecting drift and offering automatic remediation. NSX provides its own compliance status reporting for network components. VCF also offers a Configuration Drift feature with scheduled detection for vCenter instances and vSphere clusters, comparing current values against desired state templates and supporting integration with source control for versioning and change control.

These mechanisms are instrumental in identifying unnecessary or non-secure functions, ports, protocols, and services by continuously monitoring against defined baselines and security guides.

VMware vDefend extends these capabilities to the network security domain, providing specific tools for reviewing Network Security Control (NSC) configurations for relevance and effectiveness.

Security Intelligence continuously analyzes network traffic flows to generate policy recommendations, identifying rules that may be overly permissive or unnecessary based on actual communication patterns. The Rule Analysis capability enables administrators to evaluate existing firewall rules for efficiency and relevance, with documentation explicitly recommending periodic audits to remove unused or outdated Distributed Firewall policies and rules using flow inactivity data.

The Security Overview Dashboard provides a centralized view of all security policies, including Firewall, IDS/IPS, Malware Prevention, and TLS Inspection, serving as a starting point for reviews. vDefend monitors infrastructure services to identify actively used ports and protocols, and its Plan and Troubleshoot feature allows review of traffic flow trends over configurable intervals to identify services no longer in use.

The platform also provides rule hit counts, audit logging of firewall policy changes, and policy visualization through Security Intelligence, offering the analytical foundation for assessing ACL relevance and necessity.

While VCF and vDefend provide robust technical mechanisms for configuration review, drift detection, compliance assessment, and identification of unnecessary elements, they do not fully automate the organizational processes required for periodic review, nor do they inherently perform functional validation of control effectiveness. The customer is responsible for establishing the formal six-month review schedule, defining the organizational processes for confirming relevance and effectiveness, and conducting any necessary functional validation, security assessments, or controlled test scenarios to ensure controls operate as intended.

Description:
1.2.8 Configuration files for NSCs are: 
 • Secured from unauthorized access. 
 • Kept consistent with active network configurations. 
 Applicability Notes
 Any file or setting used to configure or synchronize NSCs is considered to be a “configuration file.” This includes files, automated and system-based controls, scripts, settings, infrastructure as code, or other parameters that are backed up, archived, or stored remotely.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation, VMware Live Recovery, and VMware vDefend collectively provide robust mechanisms to secure configuration files for Network Security Controls (NSCs) from unauthorized access. Granular role-based access control (RBAC) is enforced across all infrastructure components within VCF, allowing administrators to define custom roles with specific rights for viewing, editing, or full control over configurations. VCF Automation and Operations further restrict changes through project-level access controls, hard enforcement policies, and permission-based access to policy workspaces.

Similarly, VMware Live Recovery (VLR) and vSphere Replication (VSR) utilize an RBAC model to restrict unauthorized changes to disaster recovery configurations, including dual-permission requirements for critical operations and fine-grained permission hierarchies. VMware vDefend enforces configuration restrictions across its firewall policy management lifecycle through RBAC, policy locking mechanisms for draft configurations, and immutability for published baseline security policies, preventing unauthorized modifications to active network security posture.

The platform also ensures consistency with active network configurations for its managed virtual networking components and security policies. VMware Cloud Foundation contributes to network device configuration synchronization for the virtual networking layer, with NSX maintaining centralized configuration state for virtual switches, segments, and transport nodes. Transport Node Profiles consistently apply desired network configurations across hosts, and vSphere Lifecycle Manager and Configuration Profiles detect and remediate configuration drift.

VMware vDefend's draft-based configuration management workflow, policy locking, and immutability features ensure that security policies are consistent with approved states before and after publication to the enforcement plane.

Organizations must implement external network management tools and processes to ensure consistency for physical network equipment configurations and to define and enforce organizational policies for configuration management across all NSC types, as the platform does not manage configuration synchronization for physical network devices such as top-of-rack switches or routers.

Description: 1.3 Network access to and from the cardholder data environment is restricted.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) establishes a robust networking foundation utilizing VLANs, vSphere Distributed Switches, and NSX software-defined networking constructs like segments, Tier-0/Tier-1 gateways, and Virtual Private Clouds. This architecture inherently segments traffic by function, isolating management, vMotion, and storage networks, and provides mechanisms for Layer 2 isolation through Private VLANs. VCF also enforces role-based access control (RBAC) across all its components, restricting administrative access to the platform's configuration and resources based on assigned roles and least privilege principles.

VMware vDefend provides the primary technical enforcement for restricting network access to and from the cardholder data environment. Its Distributed Firewall enforces stateful Layer 2-7 security policies at the virtual network interface (vNIC) level of every workload, implementing microsegmentation with a default-deny posture where only explicitly authorized traffic is permitted. The vDefend Gateway Firewall extends this enforcement to North-South traffic at the network edge, providing perimeter-level access control. Security Intelligence within vDefend offers traffic flow analysis, policy recommendations, and segmentation monitoring to aid in defining and maintaining granular, application-aware access policies.

VMware Live Recovery enforces RBAC for disaster recovery operations, ensuring administrative access to DR functions is restricted.

While the platform provides comprehensive technical capabilities for network access restriction, the customer is responsible for defining the specific boundaries of the cardholder data environment, establishing detailed security policies, and configuring the precise firewall rules and segmentation policies within vDefend to enforce those restrictions. This includes implementing organizational processes for change management, periodic review of access controls, and documenting authorized data flows to ensure ongoing compliance and effective security posture.

Description:
1.3.1 Inbound traffic to the CDE is restricted as follows: 
 • To only traffic that is necessary, 
 • All other traffic is specifically denied.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation, augmented by VMware vDefend, establishes a robust deny-by-default posture for inbound traffic to the CDE. VMware vDefend's Distributed Firewall enforces Layer 2-7 access policies at the virtual network interface of every workload, operating as virtual access control lists that permit only explicitly authorized traffic. This aligns directly with the requirement to restrict traffic to only what is necessary. The Distributed Firewall includes a configurable Default Deny Rule, which, when set to Drop or Reject, ensures all traffic not explicitly permitted by allowlist rules is specifically denied.

Similarly, the vDefend Gateway Firewall provides stateful North-South inspection at Tier-0 and Tier-1 gateways, enforcing perimeter-level deny-all policies with explicit permit exceptions for traffic entering or leaving the environment. Security Intelligence further supports this by observing traffic flows, identifying unprotected communications, and generating granular, application-level policy recommendations to help define and enforce least-privilege rule sets. The Security Services Platform lockdown rule offers an immediate deny-all capability, permitting only essential management and infrastructure traffic.

While the platform provides the comprehensive technical enforcement for restricting inbound traffic, the organization remains responsible for defining what constitutes "necessary" traffic for its specific CDE applications and services. This includes establishing formal change management processes for firewall rule modifications, conducting periodic reviews of access control lists to ensure continued alignment with business requirements, and maintaining documentation of authorized traffic flows.

Description:
1.3.2 Outbound traffic from the CDE is restricted as follows: 
 • To only traffic that is necessary.
 • All other traffic is specifically denied.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides foundational network-level controls and constructs that support restricting outbound traffic. NSX networking establishes segmentation and traffic control, with distributed switches enforcing consistent VLAN configurations and traffic shaping rules. NSX edge gateways control outbound connectivity, and HTTP proxy configurations can filter and monitor outbound HTTP/HTTPS traffic.

The NSX switch_security datapath module provides stateless L2/L3 security by dropping unauthorized packets, and SpoofGuard prevents source address spoofing.

VCF Automation enables network profiles with isolation and security groups to define permitted traffic, while VCF Operations supports firewall hardening and provides visibility into denied flows. NSX routing infrastructure defaults to a deny action for routes, requiring explicit permit entries. NSX Distributed Firewall supports drop and reject rules, and VCF Automation applies consistent policies via security groups at provisioning.

VMware vDefend extends these capabilities with comprehensive firewall infrastructure for a deny-by-default, allow-by-exception traffic posture. The vDefend Distributed Firewall enforces microsegmentation at the vNIC level, restricting lateral movement and controlling external communication with default-deny policies. The vDefend Gateway Firewall provides stateful North-South traffic inspection at the network edge, controlling outbound connectivity and offering TLS Inspection for encrypted traffic and Advanced Threat Prevention for malware detection.

Security Intelligence provides traffic flow analysis, exfiltration correlation, and identifies risky protocols, helping to establish lockdown protection rules. Both Distributed and Gateway Firewalls include a Default Deny Rule, which, when configured to drop or reject, ensures only explicitly authorized traffic flows are permitted. Security Services Platform (SSP) and Security Intelligence assist in transitioning to a deny-by-default posture by identifying unprotected traffic and recommending specific allowlist rules. The SSP lockdown rule provides an immediate deny-all capability.

While VCF and vDefend provide robust technical mechanisms to restrict outbound traffic to only what is necessary and specifically deny all other traffic, a complete data loss prevention (DLP) solution requires additional content-aware tooling. Furthermore, the organizational governance aspects of defining what constitutes "necessary" traffic, managing access control lists (ACLs), implementing change management processes for firewall rules, conducting periodic ACL reviews, and documenting authorized flows require complementary operational procedures and tooling beyond what VCF and vDefend natively provide.

Description:
1.3.3 NSCs are installed between all wireless networks and the CDE, regardless of whether the wireless network is a CDE, such that: 
 • All wireless traffic from wireless networks into the CDE is denied by default.
 • Only wireless traffic with an authorized business purpose is allowed into the CDE.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides the foundational networking infrastructure through NSX, enabling the creation of network segments and edge gateways that serve as boundaries for the CDE. VMware vDefend, integrated with VCF, delivers the Network Security Controls (NSCs) required at these boundaries.

The vDefend Gateway Firewall operates on NSX Edge nodes, which are the natural enforcement points for traffic entering or leaving the CDE, including traffic originating from wireless networks. This firewall enforces a deny-by-default posture, where all traffic from wireless networks into the CDE is blocked unless explicitly permitted. Administrators configure specific allowlist rules based on authorized business purposes, ensuring only necessary traffic is allowed.

Within the CDE, the vDefend Distributed Firewall provides microsegmentation, further isolating workloads and enforcing granular policies at the virtual network interface level, independent of network placement. This ensures that even if wireless traffic is permitted into a segment, its access to specific CDE assets is strictly controlled.

While VCF and vDefend provide robust technical controls for securing the CDE boundary, the management of the physical wireless network infrastructure, including SSID management, access points, and the initial network design that places the NSC *between* the wireless network and the CDE, remains an organizational responsibility. The customer must define the overall network architecture, including DMZ configurations and trust zones, and establish the specific policies governing wireless network connectivity to the CDE. The platform enforces the security policies within the virtualized environment and at its defined edge, but it does not manage the physical wireless components or dictate the architectural decisions for their integration.

Description: 1.4 Network connections between trusted and untrusted networks are controlled.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) establishes the underlying network architecture, providing logical and physical separation through NSX segments, VLANs, vSphere Distributed Switches, and dedicated subnets for management, vMotion, and storage traffic. NSX edge gateways define North-South boundaries, supporting routing, NAT, and VPN, while NSX VPCs offer project-level isolation. VCF also includes host-based firewalls and port group security policies to restrict traffic flow and enforce least functionality.

VMware vDefend extends these capabilities with a comprehensive security enforcement layer. The Distributed Firewall provides stateful Layer 2 through Layer 7 microsegmentation at the vNIC level, enforcing granular policies between workloads regardless of their physical location. The Gateway Firewall applies stateful inspection at the perimeter of logical network segments, controlling North-South traffic. vDefend's Security Services Platform offers rule optimization, identifying overly permissive rules, and enables environment-level boundary enforcement through Protection Rules to block traffic between defined trust zones. IDS/IPS and Network Detection and Response further enhance control by inspecting traffic for threats and anomalies across these boundaries.

While the platforms provide robust technical mechanisms for establishing and enforcing network controls, the organization remains responsible for defining its specific trusted and untrusted network boundaries, establishing the security policies that govern traffic flow between these zones, and implementing the associated architectural decisions for NSX segments, transport zones, and edge node placement. Ongoing operational processes are required to manage firewall rule sets, review traffic logs, and ensure continuous alignment with the defined security posture.

SCF ID: CFG-03, NET-02, NET-03, NET-03.8, NET-06, NET-08.1, NET-09, SEA-03
Description: 1.4.1 NSCs are implemented between trusted and untrusted networks.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation establishes the foundational networking infrastructure for defining and separating trusted and untrusted networks. It leverages NSX to provide a tiered gateway architecture, segments, VLANs, and dedicated subnets for management, vMotion, and storage traffic, creating logical and physical boundaries. VCF's distributed switches and host firewalls provide initial layers of traffic control and least functionality configuration.

VMware vDefend extends these capabilities by providing the primary enforcement mechanism for Network Security Controls at these boundaries. Its Distributed Firewall operates at the vNIC level, enforcing microsegmentation policies with Layer 2 through Layer 7 inspection, independent of physical location. The vDefend Gateway Firewall provides stateful inspection at North-South perimeters, controlling traffic between the virtual environment and external networks. Furthermore, vDefend's IDS/IPS, Network Detection and Response, and Security Intelligence capabilities offer advanced threat detection, traffic analysis, and policy recommendations to continuously monitor and refine these controls across both internal and perimeter boundaries.

While the platform provides robust technical capabilities for implementing and enforcing Network Security Controls, the ultimate responsibility for defining the specific trusted and untrusted network boundaries, establishing the overall network architecture, and formulating the governing security policies rests with the customer. VMware Cloud Foundation and vDefend provide the tools and infrastructure to implement these controls effectively, but the strategic decisions regarding zone definitions, trust levels, and the ongoing management of firewall rule sets are organizational responsibilities.

Description:
1.4.2 Inbound traffic from untrusted networks to trusted networks is restricted to: 
 • Communications with system components that are authorized to provide publicly accessible services, protocols, and ports.
 • Stateful responses to communications initiated by system components in a trusted network.
 • All other traffic is denied.
 Applicability Notes
 The intent of this requirement is to address communication sessions between trusted and untrusted networks, rather than the specifics of protocols.
 This requirement does not limit the use of UDP or other connectionless network protocols if state is maintained by the NSC.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation, leveraging NSX Edge gateways, establishes the foundational network boundaries between untrusted and trusted networks. VMware vDefend provides the comprehensive enforcement mechanism at these perimeters.

The vDefend Gateway Firewall performs stateful inspection of all North-South traffic, ensuring that inbound communications from untrusted networks to trusted networks are strictly limited. This firewall allows administrators to define explicit rules that permit only authorized publicly accessible services, protocols, and ports, directly addressing the requirement for restricted access to specific system components. Furthermore, its stateful nature inherently permits only legitimate responses to communications that were initiated by system components within the trusted network.

The vDefend Gateway Firewall implements a deny-by-default posture for inbound traffic. This means all other traffic not explicitly permitted by an allowlist rule is automatically denied, either by dropping packets silently or rejecting them with a notification to the sender.

Security Services Platform and Security Intelligence further support this by providing traffic flow analysis and policy recommendations, enabling administrators to refine and enforce precise allowlist rules, thereby ensuring that only essential and authorized inbound traffic is ever permitted across the network boundary.

Description: 1.4.3 Anti-spoofing measures are implemented to detect and block forged source IP addresses from entering the trusted network.
Response:
VMware vDefend, leveraging the robust networking foundation provided by VMware Cloud Foundation's NSX, implements comprehensive anti-spoofing measures to detect and block forged source IP addresses from entering the trusted network.

vDefend's Advanced Threat Prevention capabilities include a native Intrusion Detection and Prevention System (IDS/IPS) and Network Detection and Response (NDR) that operate at both distributed and gateway enforcement points. Distributed IDS/IPS runs within the hypervisor on every ESX host, inspecting east-west traffic at the vNIC level, while Gateway IDS/IPS inspects north-south traffic at perimeter choke points. This layered approach ensures coverage across internal segments and network boundaries.

The system utilizes signature-based detection, with automated signature updates, and NDR extends this with behavioral and anomaly-based detection to identify suspicious activities, including those indicative of IP spoofing. When operating in 'Detect and Prevent' mode, vDefend's IDS/IPS actively blocks detected forged source IP addresses, preventing them from traversing the network. Furthermore, the Distributed Firewall enforces Layer 2-7 access policies at the vNIC level, effectively acting as a virtual ACL to restrict traffic based on source and destination, which inherently prevents unauthorized or spoofed source IPs from being processed.

The customer is responsible for enabling and configuring vDefend's Advanced Threat Prevention features, including IDS/IPS and NDR, and for defining the appropriate detection and prevention policies within the NSX management interface to align with their specific security posture and network architecture.

Description:
1.4.4 System components that store cardholder data are not directly accessible from untrusted networks.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement is not intended to apply to storage of account data in volatile memory but does apply where memory is being treated as persistent storage (for example, RAM disk). Account data can only be stored in volatile memory during the time necessary to support the associated business process (for example, until completion of the related payment card transaction).
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation, in conjunction with VMware vDefend, provides comprehensive technical capabilities to ensure system components storing cardholder data are not directly accessible from untrusted networks.

NSX Edge nodes within VCF serve as critical boundary protection devices, handling all north-south traffic and enabling centralized control over external connectivity. Edge gateways can enforce routing through proxies and apply HTTP/HTTPS traffic policies, preventing direct connections to external networks.

VCF's network profiles, including private VLAN transport zones, restrict workloads to internal-only connectivity by design, while VCF Automation extends this with on-demand network provisioning that enforces isolation settings and internal host denylists.

Furthermore, VCF Operations supports firewall hardening for internal management services and FIPS-compliant encryption for external connections, safeguarding management plane components.

VMware vDefend enhances this by providing multiple layers of boundary protection.

The vDefend Gateway Firewall, operating on NSX Edge nodes, enforces stateful firewall rules on all north-south traffic, acting as an organization-defined perimeter control. URL filtering capabilities enable policy-based control over external web content and services.

The vDefend Distributed Firewall extends enforcement to the workload level, applying rules at the vNIC of each virtual machine to microsegment sensitive workloads and ensure communication is restricted to internal resources, regardless of the underlying routing topology.

Security Intelligence within the Security Services Platform identifies and flags workloads with unprotected external connections, recommending corrective actions. The platform also supports configuring block policies between environment pairs and accommodates air-gap deployments, demonstrating its capacity for strict isolation.

The organizational responsibility remains to define which systems are sensitive, identify untrusted networks, and establish the overarching prohibition policy.

While VCF and vDefend provide the robust technical building blocks and enforcement mechanisms at multiple layers, the customer must define these policies and implement them through the platform's configuration, including firewall rule sets, segmentation capabilities, and network profiles.

Additionally, the customer is responsible for ensuring that account data stored in volatile memory adheres to the necessary business process duration and is not treated as persistent storage.

Description: 1.4.5 The disclosure of internal IP addresses and routing information is limited to only authorized parties.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides multiple layers of network architecture that inherently prevent internal network information from being exposed externally. NSX Virtual Private Clouds abstract underlying NSX infrastructure, network topology, and IP address management from application owners, preventing internal network details from leaking through self-service interfaces. Network profiles support isolation using subnets, external subnets, security groups, or VLAN transport zones, containing internal addressing schemes. At the infrastructure layer, VCF separates management, vMotion, vSAN, and VM traffic onto dedicated, often non-routable, networks and VLANs using vSphere Distributed Switches. NSX overlay networking encapsulates internal traffic using Geneve tunneling, ensuring internal IP addresses and network topology are not visible on the physical network. NSX centralized external connections at the Tier-0 level handle routing and NAT, translating internal addresses before traffic leaves the VCF environment. NSX IP Discovery Segment Profiles, with Trust on First Use (TOFU) and Trust on Every Use (TOEU) modes for ARP and ND snooping, limit address discovery and persistence.

VMware vDefend further contributes to preventing the public disclosure of internal network information through robust firewall enforcement, traffic analysis, and protocol-level visibility. The vDefend Distributed Firewall enforces microsegmentation policies at the vNIC level, restricting lateral traffic and reducing the ability of compromised workloads to discover internal network topology or enumerate services. The Gateway Firewall provides stateful North-South traffic inspection, filtering outbound traffic to prevent accidental or malicious disclosure of internal addressing or topology information. Security Intelligence classifies traffic flows as internal or external, identifies risky cleartext application protocols that could leak sensitive data, and flags workloads with external connections. Network Detection and Response (NDR) includes data privacy controls such as anonymizing sensitive customer information in data sharing and configurable data sharing settings, and can be deployed in isolated networks without external access. Security Intelligence also generates microsegmentation recommendations that can be reviewed and published as Distributed Firewall policy, directly reducing the attack surface discoverable by adversaries.

The platform provides the comprehensive technical capabilities to limit the disclosure of internal IP addresses and routing information. Customers are responsible for defining their specific authorization policies, configuring Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) for management plane access, and implementing granular firewall rules within the provided framework to ensure that only authorized parties can access or view this information.

Description: 1.5 Risks to the CDE from computing devices that are able to connect to both untrusted networks and the CDE are mitigated.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides the secure foundation for hosting computing devices that connect to both untrusted networks and the CDE. It ensures the integrity of the underlying hypervisor and hosts through capabilities such as Secure Boot, Trusted Platform Modules (TPM), and host attestation. VCF also provides mechanisms for applying restrictive baseline configurations, including the VCF Security Baseline, DISA Security Technical Implementation Guides (STIGs), and individual component Security Configuration Guides, with continuous configuration drift detection and automated remediation for virtual machines. Workload domains offer infrastructure-level isolation, enabling high-risk devices to reside in dedicated, tightly controlled environments with independent lifecycle management.

VMware vDefend delivers the critical network security layer to mitigate risks associated with these bridging devices. The Distributed Firewall enforces granular microsegmentation at the vNIC level, isolating these devices from other CDE assets and restricting their network access to only explicitly permitted communications. The Gateway Firewall applies stringent north-south traffic controls for all communication between these devices and untrusted networks. Furthermore, vDefend's IDS/IPS, Malware Prevention Service (including cloud sandboxing and fileless malware detection), and Network Detection and Response (NDR) actively detect and prevent exploit attempts, malicious traffic, and advanced threats targeting these critical connection points. The Security Services Platform enables the classification of these devices into high-risk security zones, facilitating the application of differentiated and more restrictive security policies.

While the platform provides comprehensive technical capabilities to secure and isolate computing devices that bridge untrusted networks and the CDE, the customer is responsible for identifying these specific devices and defining the precise security policies, microsegmentation rules, firewall configurations, IDS/IPS profiles, and malware prevention settings. This includes establishing explicit allow-lists, configuring appropriate enforcement levels, and continuously monitoring for non-compliance or security events to ensure risks are effectively mitigated according to their organizational risk tolerance.

Description:
1.5.1 Security controls are implemented on any computing devices, including company- and employee-owned devices, that connect to both untrusted networks (including the Internet) and the CDE as follows. 
 • Specific configuration settings are defined to prevent threats being introduced into the entity’s network. 
 • Security controls are actively running.
 • Security controls are not alterable by users of the computing devices unless specifically documented and authorized by management on a case-by-case basis for a limited period.
 Applicability Notes
 These security controls may be temporarily disabled only if there is legitimate technical need, as authorized by management on a case-by-case basis. If these security controls need to be disabled for a specific purpose, it must be formally authorized. Additional security measures may also need to be implemented for the period during which these security controls are not active.
 This requirement applies to employee-owned and company-owned computing devices. Systems that cannot be managed by corporate policy introduce weaknesses and provide opportunities that malicious individuals may exploit.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) and VMware vDefend provide robust mechanisms for implementing and enforcing security controls on virtualized computing devices that connect to untrusted networks and the CDE. VCF establishes and maintains specific configuration settings through the VCF Security Baseline, DISA STIGs, and component-specific Security Configuration Guides for vSphere, NSX, and vSAN. VCF Operations continuously monitors the infrastructure against these baselines, identifying non-compliant objects and providing automated remediation to ensure security controls are actively running and configured to prevent threats.

Policy-driven controls and workload domains enable the application of stricter operational thresholds and isolated environments for high-risk assets. The ESX host firewall controls traffic to host services, and VCF Operations hardens management appliance firewalls. VMware vDefend extends this by providing comprehensive network security at the vNIC level via the Distributed Firewall, enabling granular microsegmentation and the application of differentiated security policies based on security zones. vDefend's IDS/IPS, Malware Prevention Service, and Network Detection and Response capabilities actively detect and prevent network-borne threats, ensuring the integrity and availability of virtualized workloads. The platform's configuration management, drift detection, and continuous monitoring capabilities ensure that security controls, once defined, remain active and are not easily alterable without detection.

While VCF and vDefend provide the technical foundation for securing the virtualized environment, the control's scope extends to all computing devices, including physical company- and employee-owned endpoints. The platform does not directly manage or enforce security controls on these physical endpoints, nor does it provide traditional software firewall agents within guest operating systems for file-level or process-level controls.

Furthermore, the requirements for specific management authorization and documentation for altering or temporarily disabling security controls on a case-by-case basis are organizational governance responsibilities. Customers must define and implement comprehensive policies, establish formal authorization workflows, and deploy additional endpoint management and security solutions for physical and employee-owned devices to fully meet the control's intent.

Description: 2.1 Processes and mechanisms for applying secure configurations to all system components are defined and understood.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust mechanisms for applying secure configurations, including Configuration Templates for defining desired states, integrating with source control for versioning, auditing, and change control, and Configuration Drifts Templates for detecting and remediating deviations. vSphere Lifecycle Manager enforces consistent host images and configurations through Configuration Profiles. VCF Automation extends these capabilities by integrating with external tools like Ansible and Puppet for automated workload configuration enforcement. VMware Live Recovery offers specific configuration management for disaster recovery operations, with the Configuration Import/Export Tool enabling versioning and backup of DR configurations, supporting automated capture and scheduled exports. The hms-configtool and DR IP Customizer provide structured, auditable methods for modifying replication and network configurations. VMware vDefend contributes through its Distributed Firewall's draft configuration management, allowing for review and validation before publishing, and its federation architecture centralizes global policy while permitting local customization. Security Services Platform ensures deployment validation and configuration synchronization, while Security Intelligence maintains application context in a CMDB for segmentation planning. Across all platforms, extensive role-based access control (RBAC) is implemented, including predefined and customizable roles, least-privilege enforcement, and integration with external identity providers, ensuring segregation of duties for configuration tasks.

While the platforms provide comprehensive technical mechanisms and robust support for enforcing configuration processes, the organization is responsible for formally defining these secure configuration processes, standards, and baselines. This includes documenting the specific steps for configuration application, change control, and drift remediation. Furthermore, the organization must ensure that all personnel involved in configuration management understand these defined processes and are adequately trained on the use of the platform's capabilities to maintain secure configurations across all system components.

Description:
2.1.1 All security policies and operational procedures that are identified in Requirement 2 are: 
 • Documented.
 • Kept up to date.
 • In use.
 • Known to all affected parties.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 2.1.2 Roles and responsibilities for performing activities in Requirement 2 are documented, assigned, and understood.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 2.2 System components are configured and managed securely.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms for secure configuration management. VCF Operations includes Configuration Templates for defining desired states, integrating with source control for versioning, auditing, and change control, and supporting automated testing and validation of configuration changes. Configuration Drifts Templates detect and facilitate remediation of deviations from the defined desired state, with policies assignable through Infrastructure Operations to enforce configuration standards.

vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) ensures consistency through image-based lifecycle management for ESX hosts and Configuration Profiles for desired host configurations and drift detection. VCF Automation extends these capabilities by integrating with tools like Ansible and Puppet for automated configuration enforcement.

VCF also develops and maintains secure baseline configurations consistent with industry standards, documented in the VCF Security Baseline, component hardening guides, and DISA STIGs. Compliance scanning capabilities within VCF Operations evaluate infrastructure against these baselines, including the consolidated VCF 9 Security Baseline.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) contributes specific configuration management for disaster recovery operations. The Configuration Import/Export Tool allows for versioning and backup of DR configurations, supporting manual or automated exports. The hms-configtool provides structured modification of configuration properties, and the DR IP Customizer manages network configurations for protected virtual machines in a repeatable and auditable manner. VLR also provides configurable security baseline settings for its replication infrastructure, such as TLS protocol versions, and documents network encryption requirements.

VMware vDefend provides configuration management for its network security policy infrastructure. This includes Distributed Firewall draft configuration management with optional locking and comment requirements for change control, and a federation architecture for centralized policy management across multi-site deployments.

The Security Services Platform validates configuration changes pre-deployment, provides configuration synchronization metrics, and supports backup and restore. vDefend also offers structured policy organization, predefined TLS Decryption Action Profiles as secure baselines, and Security Assessment capabilities to evaluate workload security posture against expected standards. Security Intelligence maintains application context in a CMDB for segmentation posture assessment and planning.

While the platform provides comprehensive technical tools for establishing, enforcing, and monitoring secure configurations, the organization remains responsible for defining its specific secure baseline configurations, developing and implementing formal configuration management policies and procedures, establishing change control processes, and assigning human accountability for configuration management activities.

Description:
2.2.1 Configuration standards are developed, implemented, and maintained to:
 • Cover all system components.
 • Address all known security vulnerabilities. 
 • Be consistent with industry-accepted system hardening standards or vendor hardening recommendations. 
 • Be updated as new vulnerability issues are identified, as defined in Requirement 6.3.1.
 • Be applied when new systems are configured and verified as in place before or immediately after a system component is connected to a production environment.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) establishes, documents, and maintains secure baseline configurations that align with industry-accepted hardening standards. The VCF Security Baseline provides a consolidated document addressing compliance standards and frameworks, complemented by specific hardening guidance for major components such as vCenter and NSX. Broadcom further provides DISA Security Technical Implementation Guides (STIGs) and STIG Readiness Guides for VCF components, ensuring consistency with robust security benchmarks. VCF Operations includes compliance scanning capabilities to evaluate infrastructure against these baselines, facilitating consistent configuration across the Software-Defined Data Center (SDDC) components and supporting the application and verification of standards.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) provides configurable security baseline settings for its replication infrastructure, including default TLS configurations and documented network encryption requirements, enabling the establishment of secure baselines for disaster recovery.

VMware vDefend contributes within its network security domain through structured configuration management, predefined security profiles, and configuration integrity controls. This includes draft configuration management with locking and comment requirements, predefined TLS Decryption Action Profiles, and a deployment wizard that validates configuration changes prior to implementation.

The Security Services Platform also offers a Security Assessment capability to evaluate workload security posture against configurable baseline requirements and supports backup and restore of configuration data, all contributing to maintaining secure and consistent configurations.

While the platform provides extensive technical capabilities and secure defaults, the customer is responsible for formally developing and documenting their specific organizational configuration standards, tailoring the provided baselines to their unique environment, and establishing the operational processes for their implementation, ongoing maintenance, and verification. This includes defining the specific vulnerability management process, as referenced in Requirement 6.3.1, that drives updates to these standards and ensuring that all system components, including those outside the direct scope of VCF, VLR, and vDefend, are covered by the overarching configuration management program.

Description:
2.2.2 Vendor default accounts are managed as follows:
 • If the vendor default account(s) will be used, the default password is changed per Requirement 8.3.6.
 • If the vendor default account(s) will not be used, the account is removed or disabled.
 Applicability Notes
 This applies to ALL vendor default accounts and passwords, including, but not limited to, those used by operating systems, software that provides security services, application and system accounts, point-of-sale (POS) terminals, payment applications, and Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP) defaults.
 This requirement also applies where a system component is not installed within an entity’s environment, for example, software and applications that are part of the CDE and are accessed via a cloud subscription service.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation ensures that no component is deployed with shared or factory-default credentials. During initial deployment, the VCF Installer Deployment Wizard requires administrators to specify or auto-generate unique passwords for every component, including VCF Operations, SDDC Manager, vCenter, NSX, and ESX hosts, and will not proceed without this configuration. Post-deployment, SDDC Manager provides centralized password management, enabling on-demand or scheduled automatic rotation of randomized, policy-compliant passwords for all VCF components, automatically re-establishing integrations. Provider management accounts utilize API access tokens that rotate after each use. VCF Operations also offers its own credential rotation for system-managed endpoints. Similarly, VMware vDefend's Security Services Platform Installer mandates that default passwords for its admin and audit user accounts are set during deployment, ensuring default authenticators are replaced at installation for its management plane.

While the platform provides robust mechanisms for its own components, the customer retains responsibility for managing default accounts across the broader environment. This includes identifying and changing default passwords for all other vendor operating systems, applications, security services, POS terminals, payment applications, SNMP defaults, and cloud subscription services that are not part of the VCF or vDefend management plane. Furthermore, the customer must establish processes to identify vendor default accounts that will not be used and ensure they are appropriately removed or disabled, as the platform's capabilities focus on securing accounts that are actively configured for use.

Description:
2.2.3 Primary functions requiring different security levels are managed as follows: 
 • Only one primary function exists on a system component,
 OR
 • Primary functions with differing security levels that exist on the same system component are isolated from each other,
 OR
 • Primary functions with differing security levels on the same system component are all secured to the level required by the function with the highest security need.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust mechanisms to manage primary functions with differing security levels on system components. The ESX hypervisor establishes foundational isolation, ensuring each virtual machine operates within its own secure execution environment, preventing direct access to shared physical resources. This isolation extends to containerized workloads through vSphere Pods, which provide VM-level isolation, and Confidential vSphere Pods, which encrypt guest OS memory. Architectural separation further isolates security-critical management components within a dedicated management domain from customer workloads in separate workload domains, each with isolated compute, network, and storage resources. Role-based access control across all platform components enforces least privilege, restricting security functions to authorized individuals and ensuring that administrative access to vCenter, SDDC Manager, and NSX Manager flows through a centralized authentication layer.

VMware vDefend enhances this isolation through network-layer segmentation and security function separation. The Distributed Firewall operates within the ESX hypervisor kernel, enforcing security policies at the virtual network interface level, thereby isolating network traffic between individual workloads and application tiers on the same host. Microsegmentation capabilities enable granular Layer 2 through Layer 7 policy enforcement, creating explicit security boundaries. Furthermore, the Guest Introspection framework isolates endpoint security services from the workloads they protect by operating outside the guest VM, preventing a compromised guest from tampering with its security service. This architecture ensures that security functions are separated from the workloads they inspect, and network communication between functions is explicitly controlled.

The platform's inherent design and capabilities directly support the management of functions with differing security levels by providing strong isolation mechanisms at the hypervisor, network, and architectural layers. While the customer is responsible for defining the security levels of their primary functions and configuring the platform's extensive isolation and access controls to align with those requirements, the technical enforcement of these separation boundaries is fully provided and maintained by VMware Cloud Foundation and VMware vDefend.

Description: 2.2.4 Only necessary services, protocols, daemons, and functions are enabled, and all unnecessary functionality is removed or disabled.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive mechanisms to enforce least functionality across the infrastructure.

ESX hosts incorporate a built-in firewall that controls service and port accessibility, allowing administrators to specify allowed hosts and manage individual services like SSH. ESX lockdown mode restricts host access to vCenter, with an exception list for controlled overrides, and supports disabling unexposed features through isolation configuration parameters.

At the network layer, VCF utilizes distributed switches with port group security policies and VLAN IDs to create communication boundaries and restrict traffic flow.

The platform automatically configures required ports in ESX firewall rules when vSAN is enabled, ensuring alignment with actual service requirements.

VCF Operations collects ESX security properties, including service running status and firewall rulesets, providing visibility into the least-functionality posture when the vSphere Hardening Guide policy is applied.

Management traffic is isolated on dedicated networks using VLAN-backed distributed port groups.

VMware vDefend extends this enforcement to the network layer, ensuring only essential ports, protocols, and services are permitted.

The vDefend Distributed Firewall operates at the vNIC level on every workload, enforcing granular firewall rules that specify allowed, dropped, or rejected traffic based on defined services with specific port and protocol pairings. This includes Layer 2 through Layer 7 filtering, enabling restrictions by application identity.

The Gateway Firewall provides similar allow, drop, and reject actions at the perimeter of logical network segments, restricting north-south traffic.

The Security Services Platform offers rule optimization analysis to identify overly permissive, duplicate, or ineffective rules, directly supporting least functionality by flagging configurations that allow more traffic than necessary.

Security Intelligence and the Security Services Platform also provide traffic flow analysis with protocol and port filtering, allowing verification that only essential ports and protocols carry traffic and identifying unexpected communication patterns.

Segmentation planning capabilities enable the definition of block policies between environment pairs, enforcing broader isolation boundaries.

While VMware Cloud Foundation and vDefend provide the robust technical capabilities and visibility to implement and monitor least functionality, the determination of what constitutes "necessary" services, protocols, daemons, and functions remains a customer responsibility.

Customers must define their specific security policies, configure the platform's features accordingly, and establish processes for ongoing review and management to ensure that only essential functionality is enabled and all unnecessary components are disabled or removed.

Description:
2.2.5 If any insecure services, protocols, or daemons are present: 
 • Business justification is documented.
 • Additional security features are documented and implemented that reduce the risk of using insecure services, protocols, or daemons.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust mechanisms to mitigate risks associated with insecure ports, protocols, and services. Port group security policies on distributed virtual switches control traffic behavior, restricting promiscuous mode, MAC address changes, and forged transmits, while unused port groups can be removed to reduce the attack surface. NSX delivers distributed security across all ports within DVPGs, offering visibility and policy control at the network layer. Firewall hardening in VCF Operations restricts network access to internal services, and VCF components enforce TLS for management API connections in production environments. Virtual serial port connections support secure telnet (telnets) protocol, with the Virtual Serial Port Proxy enforcing security policies.

VMware vDefend extends these capabilities by identifying inherently risky protocols such as HTTP, Telnet, FTP, TFTP, LDAP, POP3, and IMAP, tracking workloads exposed to them through the "Workloads without Obsolete OS and Risky Protocols" metric. Security Intelligence provides protocol and port-based traffic flow analysis, offering policy recommendations to filter and restrict traffic on insecure ports. Anomaly detection is performed by Port Profiler and Server Port Profiler, which identify unusual port usage patterns. The vDefend Distributed Firewall enforces Layer 2 through 7 rules with application-aware microsegmentation, enabling administrators to define policies that block insecure services between workloads. Security Services Platform continuously monitors infrastructure services for deviations from expected port and protocol usage.

While the platform provides comprehensive technical features for implementing additional security controls to reduce risk, the explicit requirement for documenting business justifications for insecure services and formally documenting the implemented security features remains a customer responsibility.

Description: 2.2.6 System security parameters are configured to prevent misuse.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
2.2.7 All non-console administrative access is encrypted using strong cryptography.
 Applicability Notes
 This includes administrative access via browser-based interfaces and application programming interfaces (APIs).
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation encrypts all non-console administrative access using strong cryptography, including TLS and SSH, with no unencrypted remote management paths available by default.

All web-based management interfaces, such as vCenter, SDDC Manager, NSX Manager, VCF Operations, and the vCenter Management Interface (VAMI), are accessed over HTTPS with TLS encryption. The vSphere Web Services API and vSphere Automation REST API communicate exclusively over HTTPS.

SSH access to ESX hosts and vCenter appliances utilizes encrypted channels, with ESX SSH typically disabled by default. Inter-component communication, including NSX Manager to vCenter compute managers and VCF Operations to various endpoints, is similarly encrypted over HTTPS or SSL.

The platform leverages FIPS 140-3 validated cryptographic modules, with VCF Automation running in FIPS-compliant mode by default and NSX cryptographic modules validated to FIPS 140-3 standards.
