TLS 1.3 is the default for internal VCF communications, with TLS 1.2 as a compatibility fallback, and NSX Manager supports configurable strong cipher suites such as AES-256-GCM and CHACHA20-POLY1305 for TLS 1.3, with TLS 1.1 deactivated and TLS 1.0 unsupported.

VMware Live Recovery and vSphere Replication encrypt all non-console administrative access using TLS, with no unencrypted remote management paths available by default.

The VMware Live Recovery Appliance Management Interface (VAMI) and vSphere Replication Management Server operate over HTTPS, and SSH connections to the VLR appliance are deactivated by default.

VMware vDefend protects its own non-console administrative access to management interfaces through TLS encryption and certificate-based authentication.

The Security Services Platform (SSP) is accessed exclusively over HTTPS, deploying with TLS 1.3 by default and supporting TLS 1.2 with strong cipher suites like TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 and TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256, with TLS versions and cipher suites on SSP fixed to prevent accidental weakening.

NSX Manager, serving as the policy configuration interface for vDefend, is also accessed over HTTPS with TLS 1.3 and TLS 1.2 support. Inter-component communication within vDefend and syslog transmission from SSP support HTTPS/TLS encryption.

Customers are responsible for ensuring that any custom integrations or third-party tools interacting with the platform also utilize strong cryptography for administrative access.

While VMware Live Recovery allows configuration of the minimum TLS version, customers must ensure this setting aligns with their organizational cryptographic policy.

Customers must also manage custom TLS certificates, including their lifecycle, to maintain the integrity of the cryptographic trust chain.

Description: 2.3 Wireless environments are configured and managed securely.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
2.3.1 For wireless environments connected to the CDE or transmitting account data, all wireless vendor defaults are changed at installation or are confirmed to be secure, including but not limited to:
 • Default wireless encryption keys.
 • Passwords on wireless access points.
 • SNMP defaults,
 • Any other security-related wireless vendor defaults.
 Applicability Notes
 This includes, but is not limited to, default wireless encryption keys, passwords on wireless access points, SNMP defaults, and any other security-related wireless vendor defaults.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
2.3.2 For wireless environments connected to the CDE or transmitting account data, wireless encryption keys are changed as follows: 
 • Whenever personnel with knowledge of the key leave the company or the role for which the knowledge was necessary.
 • Whenever a key is suspected of or known to be compromised.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 3.1 Processes and mechanisms for performing activities in Requirement 3 are defined and understood.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive mechanisms for managing user accounts, logging authentication events, and detecting anomalies. It supports account deactivation across VCF Automation, NSX Manager, and VCF Operations, ensuring deactivated users cannot log in.

Authentication events, including successful and failed login attempts, are logged by VCF SSO, NSX Manager, and tracked within the VCF Operations Security Operations dashboard, which identifies authentication trends and privilege changes.

VCF Operations also includes robust anomaly detection capabilities, utilizing dynamic and static thresholds to generate alerts for deviations from normal operating behavior, and a Troubleshooting Workbench to identify significant metric changes.

User activity auditing within VCF Operations tracks logins, logouts, administrative actions, password changes, and certificate activations, providing detailed event views for suspicious activity analysis. NSX Manager further captures administrative actions and source IP addresses for login attempts.

These extensive authentication and audit logs can be forwarded to external syslog servers or SIEM platforms for broader correlation and analysis.

VCF Operations for Logs centralizes collection and search across these sources, offering content packs with pre-built dashboards and alerts for security use cases like failed logins.

For advanced network-level behavioral analytics, VMware vDefend Security Intelligence detects anomalies in network traffic profiles and identifies suspicious events, categorizing anomalies by type.

While VMware Cloud Foundation provides the foundational technical mechanisms, the organization is responsible for defining and documenting the specific processes and procedures for monitoring, incident response, and account management. This includes configuring specific monitoring rules, alerting thresholds, and integrating VCF logs with external SIEM platforms to create alerts for known deactivated accounts. Administrators must continuously monitor user security aspects and ensure personnel understand these defined processes.

Furthermore, vDefend, while offering network-observable anomaly detection, is not a full User and Entity Behavior Analytics (UEBA) or User Activity Monitoring (UAM) solution; organizations must implement dedicated UEBA/UAM tooling to fully correlate user activity across identity, endpoint, and application layers to detect account compromise.

Description:
3.1.1 All security policies and operational procedures that are identified in Requirement 3 are: 
 • Documented. 
 • Kept up to date. 
 • In use.
 • Known to all affected parties.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 3.1.2 Roles and responsibilities for performing activities in Requirement 3 are documented, assigned, and understood.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 3.2 Storage of account data is kept to a minimum.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive, configurable data retention capabilities across its operational components.

Administrators can define historical data retention periods for VCF Operations, with options ranging from one month to extended intervals based on data category. VCF Operations for Networks offers granular retention controls for entity, configuration, metrics, and flow data, allowing periods from minutes up to 13 months. VMware Live Recovery, through vSphere Replication, provides configurable retention policies for disaster recovery data, enabling administrators to specify the number of point-in-time recovery instances and their retention intervals, with automated expiration and storage management based on capacity thresholds. VMware vDefend offers configurable data retention for security telemetry and flow data, allowing administrators to set storage durations for flow records and enabling dynamic pruning to optimize storage. Network Detection and Response retains event and campaign data for a fixed 12-month period.

While the platform provides robust technical mechanisms for data retention and minimization, the organization is responsible for establishing and implementing data retention policies that define the minimum necessary storage periods for all account data, aligning with statutory, regulatory, and contractual obligations. Administrators must then configure the platform's retention settings to enforce these defined policies across all relevant data categories and components.

Description:
3.2.1 Account data storage is kept to a minimum through implementation of data retention and disposal policies, procedures, and processes that include at least the following:
 • Coverage for all locations of stored account data.
 • Coverage for any sensitive authentication data (SAD) stored prior to completion of authorization. This bullet is a best practice until its effective date; refer to Applicability Notes below for details.
 • Limiting data storage amount and retention time to that which is required for legal or regulatory, and/or business requirements. 
 • Specific retention requirements for stored account data that defines length of retention period and includes a documented business justification. 
 • Processes for secure deletion or rendering account data unrecoverable when no longer needed per the retention policy.
 • A process for verifying, at least once every three months, that stored account data exceeding the defined retention period has been securely deleted or rendered unrecoverable. 
 Applicability Notes
 Where account data is stored by a TPSP (for example, in a cloud environment), entities are responsible for working with their service providers to understand how the TPSP meets this requirement for the entity. Considerations include ensuring that all geographic instances of a data element are securely deleted.
 The bullet above (for coverage of SAD stored prior to completion of authorization) is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required as part of Requirement 3.2.1 and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides extensive configurable data retention capabilities across its operational components. VCF Operations allows administrators to define historical data retention periods from 1 month to longer intervals, while VCF Operations for Networks offers granular controls for entity, configuration, metrics, and flow data, ranging from 5 minutes for real-time data to 13 months for long-term metrics. VMware Live Recovery (VLR) offers configurable retention policies for disaster recovery data, managing point-in-time recovery instances and optimizing storage consumption through preserve/collapse modes. VMware vDefend provides granular control over security telemetry and flow data retention, including dynamic pruning, fixed retention windows for event and campaign data (12 months), and overwriting of Last Known Value metrics. These platform capabilities enable organizations to align data retention periods with statutory, regulatory, and contractual obligations.

Furthermore, VCF provides robust mechanisms to restrict data processing and storage locations. DRS affinity and anti-affinity rules, Storage Policy Based Management (SPBM) with tag-based placement, workload domains, vSAN storage policies, resource pools, and vSphere Namespaces allow administrators to enforce data locality, isolation, and residency requirements. VMware vDefend leverages VM storage policies to control the placement of its components, supporting infrastructure-level data residency enforcement. These technical controls facilitate the implementation of policies covering all locations of stored account data and limiting storage to required amounts and retention times.

The organization is responsible for defining and implementing comprehensive data retention and disposal policies, procedures, and processes that leverage these platform capabilities. This includes establishing specific retention periods for all stored account data, providing documented business justifications, and defining the specific processes for secure deletion or rendering data unrecoverable. Additionally, the organization must implement a process for verifying, at least quarterly, that stored account data exceeding the defined retention period has been securely deleted or rendered unrecoverable. While the platform provides the technical means to manage data retention and deletion, the overarching policy, procedural definition, and verification activities remain an organizational responsibility. The explicit handling and secure deletion methods for Sensitive Authentication Data (SAD) also require specific organizational policy and process definition, particularly as its mandatory effective date approaches.

Description: 3.3 Sensitive authentication data is not stored after authorization.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
3.3.1 SAD is not stored after authorization, even if encrypted. All sensitive authentication data received is rendered unrecoverable upon completion of the authorization process
 Applicability Notes
 Issuers and companies that support issuing services, where there is a legitimate and documented business need to store SAD, are not required to meet this requirement. A legitimate business need is one that is necessary for the performance of the function being provided by or for the issuer. 
 Refer to Requirement 3.3.3 for additional requirements specifically for these entities. 
 Sensitive authentication data includes the data cited in Requirements 3.3.1.1 through 3.3.1.3.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
3.3.1.1 The full contents of any track are not stored upon completion of the authorization process.
 Applicability Notes
 In the normal course of business, the following data elements from the track may need to be retained: 
 • Cardholder name.
 • Primary account number (PAN).
 • Expiration date.
 • Service code.
 To minimize risk, store securely only these data elements as needed for business.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
3.3.1.2 The card verification code is not stored upon completion of the authorization process.
 Applicability Notes
 The card verification code is the three- or four-digit number printed on the front or back of a payment card used to verify card-not-present transactions.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
3.3.1.3 The personal identification number (PIN) and the PIN block are not stored upon completion of the authorization process.
 Applicability Notes
 PIN blocks are encrypted during the natural course of transaction processes, but even if an entity encrypts the PIN block again, it is still not allowed to be stored after the completion of the authorization process.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
3.3.2 SAD that is stored electronically prior to completion of authorization is encrypted using strong cryptography. 
 Applicability Notes
 Whether SAD is permitted to be stored prior to authorization is determined by the organizations that manage compliance programs (for example, payment brands and acquirers). Contact these organizations for any additional criteria.
 This requirement applies to all storage of SAD, even if no PAN is present in the environment. 
 Refer to Requirement 3.2.1 for an additional requirement that applies if SAD is stored prior to completion of authorization.
 Issuers and companies that support issuing services, where there is a legitimate and documented business need to store SAD, are not required to meet this requirement. A legitimate business need is one that is necessary for the performance of the function being provided by or for the issuer. 
 Refer to Requirement 3.3.3 for requirements specifically for these entities. 
 This requirement does not replace how PIN blocks are required to be managed, nor does it mean that a properly encrypted PIN block needs to be encrypted again. 
 This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive cryptographic protections for data at rest, utilizing FIPS 140-3 validated cryptographic modules across its component stack, including NSX, OpenSSL, BouncyCastle, and BoringCrypto. Data-at-rest encryption is implemented through vSAN cluster-based encryption and VM Encryption, which operates across all datastore types such as vSAN, iSCSI, NFS, and Fibre Channel. These mechanisms employ strong algorithms like AES 256-bit, with vSAN encryption leveraging AES-NI CPU offloading for efficiency. Encryption keys are securely managed via KMIP 1.1-compatible external Key Management Servers (KMS) or the vSphere Native Key Provider (NKP), establishing a three-element domain of trust and supporting a three-tier key hierarchy with automatic key rotation. ESX hosts store configuration data in encrypted form, protected by TPM 2.0, and Memory Tiering encryption secures tiered memory with unique AES-XTS keys. VMware Live Recovery ensures cryptographic continuity by preserving data-at-rest encryption for replicated virtual machines throughout disaster recovery workflows, supporting the replication of encrypted VMs and integrating with shared KMS or NKP. VMware vDefend's Intelligent Assist component further contributes by encrypting its stored data using AES 256-bit.

While the platform delivers the necessary cryptographic tools and infrastructure, the organization is responsible for identifying all instances of Sensitive Authentication Data (SAD) that may be stored electronically prior to authorization. This includes determining the specific locations where such data resides and configuring the appropriate platform-level encryption features, such as vSAN encryption, VM Encryption, or guest-level encryption via vTPM, to protect it. The customer must also establish and enforce policies for key management, including key rotation intervals, and ensure that access to cryptographic operations is appropriately restricted through fine-grained privileges. The decision to store SAD prior to authorization, and any specific criteria for such storage, remains an organizational policy determination based on payment brand and acquirer requirements.

Description:
3.3.3 Additional requirement for issuers and companies that support issuing services and store sensitive authentication data: Any storage of sensitive authentication data is: 
 • Limited to that which is needed for a legitimate issuing business need and is secured.
 • Encrypted using strong cryptography. This bullet is a best practice until its effective date; refer to Applicability Notes below for details.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement applies only to issuers and companies that support issuing services and store sensitive authentication data. 
 Entities that issue payment cards or that perform or support issuing services will often create and control sensitive authentication data as part of the issuing function. It is allowable for companies that perform, facilitate, or support issuing services to store sensitive authentication data ONLY IF they have a legitimate business need to store such data. 
 A legitimate issuing business need is one that is necessary for the performance of the function being provided by or for the issuer.
 The bullet above (for encrypting stored SAD with strong cryptography) is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required as part of Requirement 3.3.3 and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 3.4 Access to displays of full PAN and ability to copy PAN are restricted.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation implements a comprehensive, granular role-based access control (RBAC) model that spans all platform components, including vCenter, ESX, NSX, and SDDC Manager. This model ensures that users are granted only the minimum necessary privileges to perform specific activities on specific objects, effectively scoping access by both function and inventory location. Access to virtual machine consoles, datastores, or network configurations—where full PAN might be displayed or copied—is strictly governed by these assigned roles and privileges. Users must possess the appropriate privilege to view a property or invoke an operation, and custom roles can be defined with only the minimum required privileges. Centralized authentication through VCF SSO consistently enforces these privilege assignments across the entire environment.

VMware Live Recovery extends this least privilege principle to disaster recovery operations through granular roles and custom role creation, ensuring that only authorized personnel can manage replication or perform sensitive DR operations. VMware vDefend further contributes by enforcing least privilege at the network layer through its Distributed Firewall, restricting workload-to-workload communication to only explicitly permitted flows, and by implementing robust RBAC for its administrative interfaces, ensuring that personnel managing vDefend components are granted only the privileges necessary for their assigned responsibilities.

While the platform provides the foundational access controls to restrict who can access the underlying infrastructure where PAN might reside, customers are responsible for identifying where full PAN data is processed, stored, or displayed within their applications and guest operating systems. They must then configure VCF's RBAC model to ensure that only authorized roles have access to those specific virtual machines, storage, or network segments.

Additionally, customers should implement application-level controls, data loss prevention (DLP) solutions, and organizational policies to further prevent unauthorized display or copying of PAN data within the application layer.

Description:
3.4.1 PAN is masked when displayed (the BIN and last four digits are the maximum number of digits to be displayed), such that only personnel with a legitimate business need can see more than the BIN and last four digits of the PAN.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement does not supersede stricter requirements in place for displays of cardholder data—for example, legal or payment brand requirements for point-of-sale (POS) receipts.
 This requirement relates to protection of PAN where it is displayed on screens, paper receipts, printouts, etc., and is not to be confused with Requirement 3.5.1 for protection of PAN when stored, processed, or transmitted.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation applies data masking across its management interfaces and APIs to prevent sensitive information from being displayed in clear text. VCF Automation returns user passwords in masked format, shown as asterisks, in API responses. The writeOnly attribute on form fields hides keystrokes behind asterisks, preventing on-screen exposure of credentials. Custom properties can be explicitly marked as sensitive using a dedicated syntax, ensuring those values are encrypted by default in responses and obscured when entered in request forms. VCF Automation also provides a toggle to display or hide full user names in the UI, supporting data protection. Furthermore, VCF Operations protects sensitive data in its orchestrator plug-ins by treating it as internal to plug-in objects, making it inaccessible through scripting properties, and allows sensitive values to be stored in orchestrator configuration elements or external password vaults. The VCF CLI supports password input via file, environment variable, or standard input to avoid exposing credentials in command-line arguments.

VCF restricts security functions to authorized individuals and enforces least privilege through role-based access control across all platform components. VCF SSO provides centralized authentication and identity management, integrating with enterprise authentication systems and supporting multi-factor authentication for administration tasks. vCenter implements a three-tier authorization model with fine-grained permissions and custom roles to enforce least privilege. NSX implements its own RBAC with project-scoped and custom roles. SDDC Manager provides granular rights, and VCF Operations enforces role-based and scope-based access controls, limiting user actions to assigned permissions and managed objects. For Kubernetes workloads, vSphere Namespaces support permission roles that map to Kubernetes ClusterRoles. These robust access controls ensure that only personnel with a legitimate business need can access or view sensitive information beyond its masked state.

While VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust mechanisms for masking sensitive data and controlling access, the specific requirement for masking Primary Account Numbers (PAN) to the BIN and last four digits is an application-level concern. VCF's native management interfaces do not typically handle or display PAN. Customers are responsible for ensuring that any applications deployed on VCF that process or display PAN implement the required masking format and for configuring VCF's general sensitive data masking capabilities if PAN is inadvertently stored within VCF's management plane (e.g., in custom properties).

Description:
3.4.2 When using remote-access technologies, technical controls prevent copy and/or relocation of PAN for all personnel, except for those with documented, explicit authorization and a legitimate, defined business need.
 Applicability Notes
 Storing or relocating PAN onto local hard drives, removable electronic media, and other storage devices brings these devices into scope for PCI DSS.
 This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation establishes a secure foundation for remote access through centralized identity management via VCF SSO, supporting multi-factor authentication and identity federation. SSH access to VCF components is configurable, with public-private key authentication available for VCF Automation deployments, enhancing the security of remote connections. Granular role-based access control (RBAC) models, including the CloudAdmin role in SDDC Manager and vCenter's authorization model, consistently enforce permissions across all platform components, restricting what authenticated users can perform during remote sessions.

VMware vDefend further enhances remote access governance by enforcing network-level policies via the Distributed Firewall, controlling which sources and protocols can reach management interfaces. Identity Firewall (IDFW) provides user-aware policy enforcement for remote user groups. The Security Services Platform includes a Remote Services detector to identify suspicious behavior during remote connections, offering visibility into remote access activity. Role-based access control within vDefend's administrative interfaces ensures that personnel managing vDefend components are granted only the necessary privileges.

VMware Live Recovery supports least privilege through granular roles and custom role creation, ensuring that administrative access to DR operations is appropriately scoped.

While these platforms provide robust controls for securing remote access to the environment and managing privileges within the environment, they do not inherently include technical mechanisms such as Data Loss Prevention (DLP) to actively prevent the copy and/or relocation of PAN from a remote session to local hard drives, removable media, or other unauthorized storage devices. The organization is responsible for implementing additional technical controls, such as endpoint DLP solutions, virtual desktop infrastructure (VDI) with restricted client-side capabilities, or other content-aware data exfiltration prevention tools, to meet the specific requirement of preventing PAN copy/relocation.

Furthermore, defining approved remote access methods, provisioning VPNs, and establishing centralized remote access governance are organizational processes that must be implemented by the customer.

Description: 3.5 PAN is secured wherever it is stored.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides extensive, layered technical capabilities to secure Personally Identifiable Information (PAN) wherever it is stored. This includes vSAN cluster-based encryption, which encrypts all data written to the vSAN datastore, and VM Encryption, offering an additional layer of protection across all datastore types including vSAN, iSCSI, NFS, and Fibre Channel.

Encryption keys are managed through integration with KMIP 1.1-compatible external Key Management Servers (KMS) or the built-in vSphere Native Key Provider (NKP), establishing a cryptographic domain of trust and supporting a three-tier key hierarchy with automatic key rotation.

VCF also encrypts ESX host configuration data, protects tiered memory, and supports virtual Trusted Platform Module (vTPM) 2.0 for in-guest encryption capabilities.

All cryptographic operations leverage FIPS 140-3 validated modules, ensuring strong cryptographic assurance. For secure disposal, vSAN includes NIST 800-88 aligned secure disk wipe capabilities.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) seamlessly preserves these data-at-rest encryption capabilities during disaster recovery, ensuring encrypted virtual machines remain protected across sites and encrypting replication traffic between sites.

VMware vDefend further contributes by enabling microsegmentation through the Distributed Firewall, restricting unauthorized network access to workloads processing sensitive data and thereby reducing the attack surface around stored PAN.

While VCF provides the foundational technical mechanisms for securing data at rest, the customer is responsible for identifying and classifying Personally Identifiable Information (PAN) within their environment. This includes defining and implementing specific storage policies to enforce encryption for identified PAN, configuring and managing the chosen Key Management Server (KMS) or Native Key Provider (NKP), and establishing organizational policies and procedures for the comprehensive lifecycle management of sensitive data. The customer must also ensure appropriate access controls are configured to prevent unauthorized access to encryption settings and the data itself.

Description:
3.5.1 PAN is rendered unreadable anywhere it is stored by using any of the following approaches: 
 • One-way hashes based on strong cryptography of the entire PAN. 
 • Truncation (hashing cannot be used to replace the truncated segment of PAN).
 • Index tokens.
 • Strong cryptography with associated key-management processes and procedures. 
 • Where hashed and truncated versions of the same PAN, or different truncation formats of the same PAN, are present in an environment, additional controls are in place to ensure that the different versions cannot be correlated to reconstruct the original PAN.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement applies to PANs stored in primary storage (databases, or flat files such as text files spreadsheets) as well as non-primary storage (backup, audit logs, exception, or troubleshooting logs). 
 This requirement does not preclude the use of temporary files containing cleartext PAN while encrypting and decrypting PAN.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive capabilities to render Payment Card Industry (PCI) data unreadable anywhere it is stored through robust data-at-rest encryption. VCF offers vSAN data-at-rest encryption, which encrypts all data written to the vSAN datastore at the cluster level. For non-vSAN storage, VM-level encryption protects all virtual machine files via storage policies that apply the vmwarevmcrypt encryption IO filter. Both encryption approaches integrate with either an external KMIP-compatible Key Management Server (KMS) or the vSphere Native Key Provider (NKP), ensuring secure key management.

All VCF components utilize FIPS 140-3 validated cryptographic modules. Within the VCF management plane, VCF Automation supports marking properties as sensitive in API requests, storing those values in encrypted form, and VCF Operations secures sensitive information such as credentials in orchestrator configuration elements or external password vaults.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) further contributes by encrypting replication traffic between sites, ensuring sensitive data remains protected during transfer to recovery sites, and relies on the underlying VCF platform for data-at-rest encryption at both primary and recovery sites.

While VCF provides the foundational technical mechanisms for strong cryptography, the organization is responsible for ensuring that all systems storing PAN are configured to utilize VCF's encryption capabilities consistently across primary and non-primary storage.

If the organization chooses to implement alternative methods for rendering PAN unreadable, such as one-way hashes, truncation, or index tokens, these are typically application-level functions that must be designed and implemented by the customer within their applications or databases. The organization must also establish additional controls to prevent the correlation and reconstruction of original PAN if different versions (e.g., hashed and truncated) are present within their environment.

VMware vDefend contributes to overall security through microsegmentation and threat intelligence but does not provide data-at-rest encryption capabilities for PAN.

Description:
3.5.1.1 Hashes used to render PAN unreadable (per the first bullet of Requirement 3.5.1), are keyed cryptographic hashes of the entire PAN, with associated key-management processes and procedures in accordance with Requirements 3.6 and 3.7.
 Applicability Notes
 All Applicability Notes for Requirement 3.5.1 also apply to this requirement.
 Key-management processes and procedures (Requirements 3.6 and 3.7) do not apply to system components used to generate individual keyed hashes of a PAN for comparison to another system if:
 • The system components only have access to one hash value at a time (hash values are not stored on the system)
 AND
 • There is no other account data stored on the same system as the hashes.
 This requirement is considered a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment. This requirement will replace the bullet in Requirement 3.5.1 for one-way hashes once its effective date is reached.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides a comprehensive cryptographic key management framework, ensuring the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of keys. The platform integrates with KMIP 1.1-compatible Key Management Servers (KMS) or the vSphere Native Key Provider (NKP, creating a cryptographic domain of trust across vCenter and ESX hosts.

VCF employs a three-tier key hierarchy, where Key Encryption Keys (KEK) wrap Disk Encryption Keys (DEK), with optional wrapping keys protecting KEKs, and supports configurable automatic key rotation from 30 days to 10 years. Fine-grained Cryptographic Operations privileges in vCenter, including the No Cryptography Administrator role, enforce separation of duties for key management actions, while the vSphere API facilitates programmatic key lifecycle management over mutually authenticated TLS channels.

VMware Live Recovery extends these key management practices to disaster recovery scenarios, requiring common KMS or shared encryption keys across protected and recovery sites for encrypted virtual machine protection, enforcing key availability as a prerequisite for successful VM recovery. vSphere Replication mirrors these key coordination requirements, and both products support the vSphere Native Key Provider.

VMware vDefend further contributes by managing certificate lifecycles and service-specific key pairs for its distributed security services, with the Security Services Platform (SSP) providing capabilities for certificate export, CSR generation, and replacement with CA-signed alternatives, leveraging cert-manager for automated lifecycle management.

For Distributed Malware Prevention, vDefend manages public-private key pairs, allowing configuration for cluster-specific or shared key pairs.

While the platform provides robust infrastructure for cryptographic key management, the direct implementation of keyed cryptographic hashes for rendering the entire PAN unreadable is an application-level function. Organizations are responsible for deploying and configuring applications that generate these specific hashes and ensuring they cover the entire PAN as required.

Additionally, customers must define and implement the associated key-management processes and procedures, leveraging the platform's capabilities to meet requirements for key rotation, access control, and disaster recovery.

Description:
3.5.1.2 If disk-level or partition-level encryption (rather than file-, column-, or field-level database encryption) is used to render PAN unreadable, it is implemented only as follows :
 • On removable electronic media
 OR
 • If used for non-removable electronic media, PAN is also rendered unreadable via another mechanism that meets Requirement 3.5.1. 
 Note: Disk or partition encryption implementations must also meet all other PCI DSS encryption and key-management requirements. 
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement applies to any encryption method that provides clear-text PAN automatically when a system runs, even though an authorized user has not specifically requested that data.
 While disk or partition encryption may still be present on these types of devices, it cannot be the only mechanism used to protect PAN stored on those systems. Any stored PAN must also be rendered unreadable per Requirement 3.5.1—for example, through truncation or a data-level encryption mechanism. Full disk encryption helps to protect data in the event of physical loss of a disk and therefore its use is appropriate only for removable electronic media storage devices.
 Media that is part of a data center architecture (for example, hot-swappable drives, bulk tape-backups) is considered non-removable electronic media to which Requirement 3.5.1 applies. 
 Disk or partition encryption implementations must also meet all other PCI DSS encryption and key-management requirements. 
 For issuers and companies that support issuing services: This requirement does not apply to PANs being accessed for real-time transaction processing. However, it does apply to PANs stored for other purposes.
 This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive cryptographic mechanisms for protecting data at rest across its infrastructure stack. vSAN cluster-based encryption secures all data written to the vSAN datastore at the storage layer, occurring after deduplication and compression. VM Encryption offers an additional data-at-rest encryption layer, operating before data reaches the storage, protecting virtual machine files and disks across all datastore types.

Encryption keys are managed through integration with KMIP 1.1-compatible external Key Management Servers (KMS) or the built-in vSphere Native Key Provider (NKP), establishing a cryptographic domain of trust and supporting a three-tier key hierarchy with automatic key rotation.

Crucially, VCF supports virtual Trusted Platform Module (vTPM) 2.0 for virtual machines, enabling in-guest encryption capabilities such as Windows BitLocker and Linux disk encryption. These guest-level encryption features can operate independently or in conjunction with VMware's infrastructure-level encryption, providing the technical foundation for implementing additional data-level protection.

All cryptographic operations leverage FIPS 140-3 validated modules.

VMware Live Recovery preserves and integrates with these data-at-rest encryption capabilities, ensuring that encrypted virtual machines remain protected throughout the disaster recovery lifecycle. vSphere Replication supports the replication of encrypted virtual machines, maintaining cryptographic continuity across sites and providing mechanisms to add encryption post-replication or to encrypt the replication data stream itself.

While VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust disk and VM-level encryption, along with the enabling technology for in-guest encryption via vTPM, the customer is responsible for implementing and configuring the specific data-level encryption or other mechanisms (e.g., truncation) for PAN within the guest operating system or application layer. This ensures that for non-removable electronic media, PAN is rendered unreadable via an additional mechanism that meets Requirement 3.5.1, beyond the platform's disk or partition encryption. The customer must also define and enforce associated policies and procedures for these implementations.

Description:
3.5.1.3 If disk-level or partition-level encryption is used (rather than file-, column-, or field--level database encryption) to render PAN unreadable, it is managed as follows:, 
 • Logical access is managed separately and independently of native operating system authentication and access control mechanisms. 
 • Decryption keys are not associated with user accounts. 
 • Authentication factors (passwords, passphrases, or cryptographic keys) that allow access to unencrypted data are stored securely.
 Applicability Notes
 Disk or partition encryption implementations must also meet all other PCI DSS encryption and key-management requirements.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive disk-level encryption capabilities through vSAN cluster-based encryption and VM Encryption, protecting data at rest across the infrastructure stack. Encryption keys are managed through integration with KMIP 1.1-compatible external Key Management Servers (KMS) or the vSphere Native Key Provider (NKP), establishing a cryptographic domain of trust. This architecture ensures decryption keys are not associated with individual user accounts, as keys are tied to the infrastructure and managed by the KMS/NKP.

VCF maintains separate logical access for encryption configuration, requiring specific cryptographic privileges distinct from native operating system authentication, and offers a "No Cryptography Administrator" role to prevent unauthorized encryption changes while allowing routine operations.

Authentication factors, such as wrapping keys, are stored securely within the KMS or protected by TPM 2.0 on ESX hosts, leveraging a three-tier key hierarchy and FIPS 140-3 validated cryptographic modules.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) preserves these data-at-rest encryption capabilities during disaster recovery, supporting replication of encrypted virtual machines with cryptographic continuity and mandatory network encryption for replication traffic.

While VCF provides the robust technical mechanisms to meet the control's requirements for managing disk-level encryption, the customer is responsible for the proper implementation, configuration, and ongoing operational management.

This includes defining and enforcing organizational policies for key management, establishing access controls for the external KMS, and assigning VCF roles with appropriate cryptographic privileges in alignment with least privilege principles.

The customer must also ensure their overall key management program adheres to all other PCI DSS encryption and key-management requirements.

Description: 3.6 Cryptographic keys used to protect stored account data are secured.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive cryptographic key management controls designed to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of keys through multiple integrated mechanisms.

The platform supports integration with KMIP 1.1-compatible Key Management Servers (KMS) or the vSphere Native Key Provider (NKP), establishing a cryptographic domain of trust between key providers, vCenter, and ESX hosts.

A robust three-tier key hierarchy is employed, where Key Encryption Keys (KEK) wrap Disk Encryption Keys (DEK, with optional wrapping keys protecting KEKs), allowing for efficient key rotation or revocation without requiring re-encryption of underlying data. Automatic key rotation is configurable with intervals ranging from 30 days to 10 years.

Fine-grained Cryptographic Operations privileges in vCenter, including the No Cryptography Administrator role, enforce separation of duties and allow for the creation of custom roles with restricted cryptographic privileges, such as enabling encryption without decryption. The vSphere API exposes a CryptoManager managed object hierarchy, facilitating programmatic key lifecycle management, with all key operations occurring over mutually authenticated TLS channels.

VMware Live Recovery extends these key management practices to disaster recovery scenarios, ensuring key availability, consistency, and coordination across protected and recovery sites.

For encrypted virtual machine protection, VMware Live Site Recovery and vSphere Replication require common KMS or shared encryption keys across sites, with KMS clusters registered under identical names to maintain a consistent key management topology. The absence of the correct encryption key on the recovery site prevents VM power-on, enforcing key availability as a prerequisite for successful failover. Both products also support the vSphere Native Key Provider as an integrated alternative.

VMware Live Site Recovery further supports custom SSL/TLS certificates with specific cryptographic requirements, including x509v3 Extended Key Usage attributes.

VMware vDefend contributes to cryptographic key management through its Security Services Platform (SSP), which manages certificates for client/host identification, trust, and secure inter-component communication using automated cert-manager lifecycle management. For Distributed Malware Prevention, vDefend manages public-private key pairs to secure communication, allowing administrators to configure key pair scope and emphasizing secure private key storage.

The platform provides the complete technical framework and capabilities to ensure cryptographic keys used to protect stored account data are secured throughout their lifecycle, including generation, storage, usage, rotation, and recovery.

Customers are responsible for configuring these robust features, defining specific key management policies, and ensuring the secure operation of any external Key Management Servers integrated with the platform.

Description:
3.6.1 Procedures are defined and implemented to protect cryptographic keys used to protect stored account data against disclosure and misuse that include: 
 • Access to keys is restricted to the fewest number of custodians necessary. 
 • Key-encrypting keys are at least as strong as the data-encrypting keys they protect. 
 • Key-encrypting keys are stored separately from data-encrypting keys. 
 • Keys are stored securely in the fewest possible locations and forms.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement applies to keys used to protect stored account data and to key-encrypting keys used to protect data-encrypting keys.
 The requirement to protect keys used to protect stored account data from disclosure and misuse applies to both data-encrypting keys and key-encrypting keys. Because one key-encrypting key may grant access to many data-encrypting keys, the key-encrypting keys require strong protection measures.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive cryptographic key management capabilities that protect keys against disclosure and misuse. VCF integrates with KMIP 1.1-compatible Key Management Servers (KMS) or offers the vSphere Native Key Provider (NKP) for key storage and management. A cryptographic domain of trust is established between key providers, vCenter, and ESX hosts, ensuring keys are accessible only to authorized components. VCF employs a three-tier key hierarchy where Key Encryption Keys (KEK) wrap Data Encryption Keys (DEK), with optional wrapping keys protecting KEKs, ensuring KEKs are at least as strong as the DEKs they protect and are stored separately. Internal encryption keys are kept in memory only, with only encrypted data stored on disk, minimizing the attack surface. Fine-grained Cryptographic Operations privileges in vCenter, including the "No Cryptography Administrator" role, enforce separation of duties and restrict access to the fewest necessary custodians. All key operations occur over mutually authenticated TLS channels, and ESX hosts support key persistence, caching encryption keys locally, including TPM-backed persistence, to maintain availability and secure local storage. VMware Live Recovery (VLR) extends these capabilities by requiring consistent key management across protected and recovery sites, ensuring key availability and secure distribution in disaster recovery scenarios. VMware vDefend further contributes by securely managing its own service-specific certificates and public-private key pairs, including secure storage of private keys for Distributed Malware Prevention deployments, leveraging X.509 PKI for asymmetric key distribution and automated certificate lifecycle management.

While the platform provides robust technical controls for key protection, the organization is responsible for defining and implementing formal procedures for cryptographic key management. This includes establishing policies for key access restriction, defining the roles and responsibilities of key custodians, and ensuring that these procedures are consistently followed across the environment. The organization must also define the specific locations and forms for secure key storage, leveraging the platform's capabilities to minimize exposure.

Description:
3.6.1.1 Additional requirement for service providers only: A documented description of the cryptographic architecture is maintained that includes: 
 • Details of all algorithms, protocols, and keys used for the protection of stored account data, including key strength and expiry date.
 • Preventing the use of the same cryptographic keys in production and test environments. This bullet is a best practice until its effective date; refer to Applicability Notes below for details.
 • Description of the key usage for each key. 
 • Inventory of any hardware security modules (HSMs), key management systems (KMS), and other secure cryptographic devices (SCDs) used for key management, including type and location of devices, to support meeting Requirement 12.3.4.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement applies only when the entity being assessed is a service provider.
 In cloud HSM implementations, responsibility for the cryptographic architecture according to this Requirement will be shared between the cloud provider and the cloud customer.
 The bullet above (for including, in the cryptographic architecture, that the use of the same cryptographic keys in production and test is prevented) is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required as part of Requirement 3.6.1.1 and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation establishes a robust cryptographic architecture through its integration with KMIP 1.1-compatible Key Management Servers (KMS) and the vSphere Native Key Provider (NKP). This creates a cryptographic domain of trust among key providers, vCenter Server, and ESX or vSAN hosts, facilitating automated authentication and key retrieval via certificates and secure KMIP sessions. The platform employs a three-tier key hierarchy (KEK/DEK) with configurable automatic key rotation intervals from 30 days to 10 years, ensuring key confidentiality, integrity, and availability. NSX Manager and other VCF components utilize FIPS 140-approved and FIPS 140-3 validated cryptographic modules, with TLS 1.3 as the default for internal communications. Fine-grained Cryptographic Operations privileges, including the No Cryptography Administrator role, enforce separation of duties for key management.

VMware Live Recovery extends these capabilities to disaster recovery, establishing authenticated communication channels through token-based and mutual certificate-based authentication. VLR requires KMS clusters to be registered with the same name across protected and recovery sites, ensuring common encryption keys are available for encrypted virtual machine protection and recovery.

VMware vDefend contributes by managing certificates essential for its distributed security services, utilizing cert-manager for automated lifecycle management within the Security Services Platform (SSP). It also manages public-private key pairs for Distributed Malware Prevention, securing communication between malware analysis infrastructure and protected hosts.

While the VMware Cloud Foundation platform provides the foundational cryptographic capabilities, including algorithms, protocols, key management, and the use of secure cryptographic devices, the explicit requirement for a *documented description* of the cryptographic architecture, including details of key strength, expiry, usage, and a comprehensive inventory of all HSMs, KMS, and SCDs with their types and locations, remains a customer responsibility. The customer must also define and enforce policies to prevent the use of the same cryptographic keys in production and test environments, which becomes a mandatory requirement after March 31, 2025. The platform provides the technical mechanisms that form the basis of this architecture, but the aggregation, formal documentation, and policy enforcement are external to the platform's automated functions.

Description:
3.6.1.2 Secret and private keys used to protect stored account data are stored in one (or more) of the following forms at all times: 
 • Encrypted with a key-encrypting key that is at least as strong as the data-encrypting key, and that is stored separately from the data-encrypting key. 
 • Within a secure cryptographic device (SCD), such as a hardware security module (HSM) or PTS-approved point-of-interaction device. 
 • As at least two full-length key components or key shares, in accordance with an industry-accepted method. 
 Applicability Notes
 It is not required that public keys be stored in one of these forms.
 Cryptographic keys stored as part of a key management system (KMS) that employs SCDs are acceptable. 
 A cryptographic key that is split into two parts does not meet this requirement. Secret or private keys stored as key components or key shares must be generated via one of the following: 
 • Using an approved random number generator and within an SCD, 
 OR
 • According to ISO 19592 or equivalent industry standard for generation of secret key shares.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms for storing secret and private keys used to protect stored account data. VCF integrates with KMIP 1.1-compatible Key Management Servers (KMS) and offers the vSphere Native Key Provider (NKP), establishing a cryptographic domain of trust where hosts authenticate to key providers via certificates and keys are exchanged over secure KMIP sessions.

The platform automatically wraps Data Encryption Keys (DEK) with Key Encryption Keys (KEK), and supports optional wrapping keys for KEKs, ensuring keys are stored encrypted with a key-encrypting key that is at least as strong as the data-encrypting key and stored separately. Additionally, ESX hosts with a Trusted Platform Module (TPM) 2.0 store configuration encryption keys within the TPM, which functions as a Secure Cryptographic Device (SCD).

NSX cryptographic modules are validated against the FIPS 140 standard, and VCF Operations for Networks can be configured to restrict connections to FIPS-validated cryptographic modules. The platform further supports a three-tier key hierarchy, configurable automatic key rotation, and fine-grained cryptographic operations privileges, including a No Cryptography Administrator role, to enforce separation of duties.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) extends these capabilities to disaster recovery scenarios by requiring consistent key management server (KMS) configurations or common encryption keys across protected and recovery sites. VLR and vSphere Replication authenticate to these key providers automatically during encryption and decryption operations, ensuring key availability and consistency are maintained for encrypted virtual machines during replication and recovery workflows.

VMware vDefend contributes by managing its own service-specific public-private key pairs for Distributed Malware Prevention and various certificates for its Security Services Platform, securing inter-component communication and establishing trust relationships. While vDefend manages its internal key material, the broader enterprise key management functions, such as integration with KMIP-compliant Key Management Servers and key hierarchy management, are provided by the underlying VCF platform.

Description: 3.6.1.3 Access to cleartext cryptographic key components is restricted to the fewest number of custodians necessary.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive cryptographic key management controls that protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of keys. VCF establishes a cryptographic domain of trust between key providers, vCenter, and ESX hosts, ensuring keys are only accessible to authorized components and operations occur over mutually authenticated TLS channels. The platform supports KMIP 1.1-compatible Key Management Servers or the vSphere Native Key Provider, offering flexibility in key storage. Fine-grained Cryptographic Operations privileges in vCenter allow administrators to precisely control who can perform specific key management actions. The No Cryptography Administrator role enforces separation of duties between key managers and system administrators, and custom roles can be created to further restrict operations, such as allowing encryption but not decryption of virtual machines. This robust framework directly enables the restriction of access to cryptographic key components.

VMware Live Recovery extends these key management practices to disaster recovery scenarios, requiring common KMS configurations or shared encryption keys across protected and recovery sites to ensure key availability and consistency for encrypted virtual machines. VMware vDefend further contributes by managing its own service-specific key pairs and certificates, such as those for Distributed Malware Prevention, where administrators can configure key pair scope and ensure secure storage of private keys.

While the platform provides the technical mechanisms to restrict access and enforce separation of duties, the organization is responsible for defining the specific policies and procedures that determine the "fewest number of custodians necessary" for cleartext cryptographic key components. This includes implementing the fine-grained privileges and roles provided by VCF to align with organizational policy and assigning individuals to these roles.

Description: 3.6.1.4 Cryptographic keys are stored in the fewest possible locations.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides a robust framework for cryptographic key management that inherently supports the principle of storing keys in the fewest possible locations. VCF integrates with KMIP 1.1-compatible Key Management Servers (KMS) or the vSphere Native Key Provider (NKP), establishing these as the primary, centralized storage locations for cryptographic keys. A cryptographic domain of trust is established between key providers, vCenter, and ESX hosts, ensuring keys are only accessible to authorized components and preventing their arbitrary dispersion. The three-tier key hierarchy further organizes and protects keys within these controlled environments, while fine-grained cryptographic operations privileges in vCenter enforce strict access controls and separation of duties for key management actions.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) and vSphere Replication extend these capabilities to disaster recovery scenarios. For encrypted virtual machine protection and recovery, these services require a common Key Management Server (KMS) or common encryption keys across protected and recovery sites. This ensures that while keys are necessarily duplicated for business continuity, this duplication is controlled, consistent, and limited to the minimum required for successful failover, rather than introducing unmanaged key proliferation.

VMware vDefend further contributes by offering direct control over the storage locations for its service-specific key pairs. For Distributed Malware Prevention, administrators can configure whether a new key pair is created for each cluster or whether a single key pair is shared across all service deployments, providing a direct mechanism to minimize the number of locations where these specific private keys are stored.

While the platform provides comprehensive technical capabilities and architectural patterns that facilitate centralized and controlled key storage, the ultimate determination and implementation of the "fewest possible locations" remains a customer responsibility. Organizations must define their key management policies, make architectural decisions regarding KMS deployment, and configure service-specific options within vDefend to align with their specific security posture and operational requirements for minimizing key dispersion.

Description: 3.7 Where cryptography is used to protect stored account data, key management processes and procedures covering all aspects of the key lifecycle are defined and implemented.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive cryptographic key management controls, safeguarding the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of keys through multiple mechanisms. The platform integrates with KMIP 1.1-compatible Key Management Servers (KMS) or the vSphere Native Key Provider (NKP), establishing a cryptographic domain of trust between key providers, vCenter, and ESX hosts.

A three-tier key hierarchy is employed, where Key Encryption Keys (KEK) wrap Disk Encryption Keys (DEK, with optional wrapping keys for KEKs), enabling efficient key rotation or revocation without re-encryption of underlying data. Automatic key rotation is configurable with intervals ranging from 30 days to 10 years.

Fine-grained Cryptographic Operations privileges in vCenter, including a "No Cryptography Administrator" role, enforce separation of duties, allowing for custom roles to restrict specific key management actions. The vSphere API exposes a CryptoManager managed object hierarchy, facilitating programmatic key lifecycle management over mutually authenticated TLS channels.

VMware Live Recovery extends these capabilities to disaster recovery scenarios, ensuring key availability, consistency, and coordination across protected and recovery sites by requiring shared KMS infrastructure or common encryption keys registered with identical names.

VMware vDefend further contributes by managing certificate lifecycle and service-specific key pairs for its distributed security services, including automated certificate management via cert-manager and configurable key pair strategies for malware prevention components, addressing the key material vDefend itself generates, consumes, and rotates.

While the platform provides the robust technical mechanisms and tools for managing cryptographic keys throughout their lifecycle, the organization remains responsible for defining and implementing the overarching key management processes and procedures. This includes establishing specific policies for key generation, storage, distribution, use, rotation, revocation, and destruction, as well as configuring the integrated key management systems, assigning appropriate roles and privileges, and ensuring the secure storage of private keys where applicable. The platform provides the capabilities; the customer must operationalize them through documented processes and assigned accountability.

Description: 3.7.1 Key-management policies and procedures are implemented to include generation of strong cryptographic keys used to protect stored account data.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive cryptographic key management controls, ensuring the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of keys. This includes integration with KMIP 1.1-compatible Key Management Servers or the vSphere Native Key Provider, establishing a cryptographic domain of trust, and employing a three-tier key hierarchy (Key Encryption Keys wrapping Disk Encryption Keys) with configurable automatic key rotation intervals from 30 days to 10 years.

Fine-grained Cryptographic Operations privileges in vCenter, along with the No Cryptography Administrator role, enforce separation of duties for key management actions. The vSphere API further enables programmatic key lifecycle management, with all key operations secured over mutually authenticated TLS channels.

VMware Live Recovery extends these capabilities to disaster recovery scenarios by requiring common KMS infrastructure or shared encryption keys across protected and recovery sites, ensuring key availability and consistency for encrypted virtual machine protection and recovery.

VMware vDefend contributes by managing certificate lifecycle and service-specific key pairs for its internal security services, including automated certificate management via cert-manager and configurable key pair scope for malware prevention components.

While the platform provides robust technical mechanisms for the generation and management of strong cryptographic keys, the organization remains responsible for defining and implementing the overarching key-management policies and procedures. This includes establishing specific key generation standards, defining key rotation schedules within the platform's configurable ranges, formalizing separation of duties, and documenting the operational procedures for key lifecycle management to meet their specific compliance requirements.

Description: 3.7.2 Key-management policies and procedures are implemented to include secure distribution of cryptographic keys used to protect stored account data.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation implements comprehensive cryptographic key management controls that ensure the secure distribution, storage, and lifecycle management of keys.
The platform integrates with KMIP 1.1-compatible Key Management Servers (KMS) or the vSphere Native Key Provider (NKP), establishing a cryptographic domain of trust between key providers, vCenter, and ESX hosts to restrict key access to authorized components.

A three-tier key hierarchy (KEK wrapping DEK) with optional higher-level wrapping keys provides layered protection and simplifies key rotation or revocation without re-encryption of underlying data, with automatic rotation configurable from 30 days to 10 years.

Fine-grained Cryptographic Operations privileges in vCenter, including the "No Cryptography Administrator" role, enforce separation of duties, while the vSphere API enables programmatic key lifecycle management. All key operations are secured over mutually authenticated TLS channels.

VMware Live Recovery extends these capabilities to disaster recovery scenarios by requiring shared KMS or common encryption keys across protected and recovery sites, with consistent KMS registration names. This ensures key availability and consistency, as VM recovery fails if encryption keys are not present at the recovery site, thereby enforcing secure key distribution across the DR environment.
vSphere Replication applies identical key coordination requirements.

VMware vDefend further contributes by managing its own certificate lifecycle and service-specific public-private key pairs within the Security Services Platform and Malware Prevention components, securing inter-component communication and providing control over key deployment scope.

While the platform provides robust technical mechanisms for secure key management and distribution, the organization is responsible for defining and implementing the overarching key-management policies and procedures, including those specifically addressing the secure distribution of cryptographic keys used to protect stored account data.

Description: 3.7.3 Key-management policies and procedures are implemented to include secure storage of cryptographic keys used to protect stored account data.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive cryptographic key management capabilities, integrating with KMIP 1.1-compatible Key Management Servers or the vSphere Native Key Provider to ensure secure storage of cryptographic keys. It establishes a cryptographic domain of trust, employs a three-tier key hierarchy (Key Encryption Keys wrapping Disk Encryption Keys) with configurable automatic key rotation intervals from 30 days to 10 years, and offers fine-grained access controls through vCenter privileges and roles such as the No Cryptography Administrator, enforcing separation of duties. The vSphere API enables programmatic key lifecycle management, with all key operations secured via mutually authenticated TLS channels. VMware Live Recovery extends these capabilities to disaster recovery scenarios, ensuring key availability and consistency across protected and recovery sites by requiring shared KMS infrastructure or common encryption keys, with VM recovery failing if keys are unavailable. VMware vDefend further contributes by managing the lifecycle of certificates and service-specific key pairs essential for its distributed security services, including automated certificate management and secure storage of private keys for malware prevention components.

The organization is responsible for defining, documenting, and implementing comprehensive key-management policies and procedures that leverage these platform capabilities. This includes establishing specific key rotation schedules, defining roles and responsibilities for key administrators, and ensuring that these policies align with the secure storage of cryptographic keys used to protect stored account data across the environment.

Description:
3.7.4 Key management policies and procedures are implemented for cryptographic key changes for keys that have reached the end of their cryptoperiod, as defined by the associated application vendor or key owner, and based on industry best practices and guidelines, including the following:
 • A defined cryptoperiod for each key type in use.
 • A process for key changes at the end of the defined cryptoperiod.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive cryptographic key management capabilities, integrating with KMIP 1.1-compatible Key Management Servers or the vSphere Native Key Provider to establish a secure cryptographic domain of trust. The platform employs a three-tier key hierarchy, where Key Encryption Keys (KEK) wrap Disk Encryption Keys (DEK, with optional wrapping keys for KEKs), allowing for efficient key rotation or revocation without re-encrypting underlying data. VCF supports configurable automatic key rotation with intervals ranging from 30 days to 10 years, directly enabling the enforcement of defined cryptoperiods. Fine-grained cryptographic operation privileges and the No Cryptography Administrator role facilitate separation of duties, while the vSphere API offers programmatic key lifecycle management over mutually authenticated TLS channels. VMware Live Recovery extends these capabilities to disaster recovery, requiring shared or consistent Key Management Servers and common encryption keys across protected and recovery sites to ensure key availability and consistency for encrypted virtual machines during failover and replication. VMware vDefend further contributes by managing its own service-specific certificates and key pairs, including automated certificate lifecycle management via cert-manager for the Security Services Platform and configurable key pair management for Distributed Malware Prevention, ensuring secure internal communications and operations.

While the platform provides the robust technical mechanisms for key rotation and lifecycle management, the organization is responsible for formally defining the cryptoperiod for each key type in use, establishing comprehensive key management policies and procedures, and implementing the overarching process for key changes at the end of the defined cryptoperiod. This includes documenting the chosen rotation intervals, defining roles and responsibilities, and ensuring adherence to industry best practices and guidelines.

Description:
3.7.5 Key management policies procedures are implemented to include the retirement, replacement, or destruction of keys used to protect stored account data, as deemed necessary when: 
 • The key has reached the end of its defined cryptoperiod.
 • The integrity of the key has been weakened, including when personnel with knowledge of a cleartext key component leaves the company, or the role for which the key component was known.
 • The key is suspected of or known to be compromised. 
 Retired or replaced keys are not used for encryption operations.
 Applicability Notes
 If retired or replaced cryptographic keys need to be retained, these keys must be securely archived (for example, by using a key-encryption key).
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust cryptographic key management capabilities that enable the retirement, replacement, and destruction of keys. It integrates with KMIP 1.1-compatible Key Management Servers (KMS) or the vSphere Native Key Provider (NKP) for secure key storage and lifecycle management. Automatic key rotation is configurable, allowing for key replacement at defined cryptoperiods. The vSphere API exposes a CryptoManager managed object hierarchy with methods for adding, listing, and removing keys, facilitating programmatic key lifecycle management. Fine-grained Cryptographic Operations privileges in vCenter, including the No Cryptography Administrator role, enforce separation of duties for key management actions. VMware Live Recovery extends these capabilities by ensuring key availability and consistency across protected and recovery sites for encrypted virtual machines, requiring common KMS or encryption keys. VMware vDefend manages its own internal service-specific key pairs and certificates, providing lifecycle management for these components.

The organization is responsible for defining and implementing comprehensive key management policies and procedures that specify the cryptoperiods for keys, criteria for assessing weakened key integrity (such as personnel changes), and processes for responding to suspected or known key compromises. These policies must govern the retirement, replacement, and destruction of keys, ensuring that retired or replaced keys are not used for new encryption operations. Furthermore, the organization must establish and implement secure archiving procedures for any cryptographic keys that need to be retained after retirement or replacement.

Description:
3.7.6 Where manual cleartext cryptographic key-management operations are performed by personnel, key-management policies and procedures are implemented including managing these operations using split knowledge and dual control. 
 Applicability Notes
 This control is applicable for manual key-management operations. 
 A cryptographic key that is simply split into two parts does not meet this requirement. Secret or private keys stored as key components or key shares must be generated via one of the following: 
 • Using an approved random number generator and within a secure cryptographic device (SCD), such as a hardware security module (HSM) or PTS-approved point-of-interaction device, 
 OR
 • According to ISO 19592 or equivalent industry standard for generation of secret key shares.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust, automated cryptographic key management capabilities designed to minimize or eliminate the need for manual cleartext key operations. VCF integrates with KMIP 1.1-compatible Key Management Servers (KMS) or the vSphere Native Key Provider, establishing a cryptographic domain of trust.

Keys are managed through a three-tier hierarchy, with automatic rotation configurable at defined intervals. All key operations are performed programmatically via the vSphere API over mutually authenticated TLS channels. The platform enforces separation of duties for cryptographic operations privileges within vCenter through fine-grained roles, including a "No Cryptography Administrator" role, which restricts who can perform key management actions. Integration with KMS, often implemented as Hardware Security Modules (HSMs), ensures that keys, including any components or shares, are generated using approved random number generators within secure cryptographic devices, aligning with industry standards.

VMware Live Recovery extends these secure key management practices to disaster recovery scenarios. It requires that protected and recovery sites share a common Key Management Server or common encryption keys, with KMS clusters registered consistently across sites. This ensures key availability and consistency for encrypted virtual machines during replication and recovery, without introducing manual cleartext operations.

VMware vDefend further contributes by managing its own certificate lifecycle and service-specific public-private key pairs within the Security Services Platform and Malware Prevention components. It utilizes automated certificate lifecycle management and provides controls for secure storage of private keys, relying on the underlying VCF platform for broader enterprise key management functions.

While the platform's architecture and capabilities significantly reduce the necessity for manual cleartext cryptographic key-management operations, the organization remains responsible for implementing comprehensive key-management policies and procedures.

These policies must explicitly address any manual cleartext operations that may occur within their broader environment, ensuring they are managed using split knowledge and dual control principles. The customer must also define processes for personnel involved in such operations and ensure that any secret or private key components or shares are generated according to ISO 19592 or within secure cryptographic devices.

Description: 3.7.7 Key management policies and procedures are implemented to include the prevention of unauthorized substitution of cryptographic keys.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive cryptographic key management controls that protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of keys. The platform integrates with KMIP 1.1-compatible Key Management Servers or the vSphere Native Key Provider, establishing a cryptographic domain of trust between key providers, vCenter, and ESX hosts. This architecture ensures keys are only accessible to authorized components, directly preventing unauthorized substitution.

VCF employs a three-tier key hierarchy with configurable automatic key rotation intervals ranging from 30 days to 10 years. Fine-grained Cryptographic Operations privileges in vCenter, including the "No Cryptography Administrator" role, enforce separation of duties and control who can perform specific key management actions, further mitigating risks of unauthorized key manipulation. All key operations are conducted over mutually authenticated TLS channels.

VMware Live Recovery extends these capabilities to disaster recovery scenarios by requiring consistent key management server registration and common encryption keys across protected and recovery sites, ensuring the integrity and availability of cryptographic keys are maintained during failover and failback operations.
