Description: 8.2 User identification and related accounts for users and administrators are strictly managed throughout an account’s lifecycle.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides a robust, centralized identity and access management architecture through VCF Single Sign-On (SSO), which acts as the identity broker for all VCF components including vCenter, SDDC Manager, NSX Manager, VCF Operations, and VCF Automation. VCF SSO supports identity federation with enterprise identity providers such as Microsoft Entra ID, ADFS, Okta, LDAP, OIDC, and SAML, enabling multifactor authentication and automated user synchronization and lifecycle management (provisioning and deprovisioning) with the organization's directory. The Identity Broker appliance manages the mapping between external identity providers and internal directory services, ensuring consistent user identity.

Role-based access control (RBAC) is enforced across all VCF components, with predefined and custom roles available in vCenter, VCF Operations, SDDC Manager, NSX, and VCF Automation to control access to specific functions and inventory objects. VCF Automation supports disabling users and groups at the organization level to revoke access without deleting accounts. For Kubernetes workloads, VMware Kubernetes Service supports namespace permission roles that map to Kubernetes ClusterRoles. NSX Manager provides capabilities for local user creation, deletion, activation, deactivation, password reset, username changes, role assignment, and password expiration configuration.

Service-to-service authentication utilizes certificate-based Principal Identity users, eliminating the need for password credentials between VCF components. VMware vDefend further contributes by providing identity-aware network enforcement through Identity Firewall, which integrates with Active Directory to apply firewall policy based on authenticated user identity. Within its own management plane, vDefend's Security Services Platform and Security Intelligence implement RBAC with defined roles to control access to security functions, supporting local user accounts or directory-managed accounts.

While the platform provides comprehensive technical mechanisms for identity and access management, the effective and strict management of user and administrator accounts throughout their lifecycle requires the customer to define and implement organizational processes. This includes establishing identity governance policies, managing the underlying identity provider infrastructure, and defining role assignment procedures. Centralized identity and access management for organizational users, including single sign-on and multifactor authentication across the broader VCF environment, is primarily managed by vCenter/VCF SSO, with vDefend's direct scope limited to its own management plane.

Description:
8.2.1 All users are assigned a unique ID before access to system components or cardholder data is allowed.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement is not intended to apply to user accounts within point-of-sale terminals that have access to only one card number at a time to facilitate a single transaction.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation establishes a robust identity management framework, leveraging centralized user identity management for platform administrators and non-consumer users. VCF Single Sign-On (SSO) acts as the identity management hub, facilitating federation with enterprise identity providers via OIDC, SAML, and AD/LDAP protocols. The Identity Broker appliance is crucial, managing the mapping between external identity providers and internal directory services, explicitly utilizing unique identifiers to ensure consistent user identity across all integrated systems. This architecture inherently supports the assignment of unique IDs.

Beyond initial assignment, VCF components provide comprehensive user lifecycle management. NSX Manager, VCF Automation, and VCF Operations support local user creation, deletion, activation, deactivation, password reset, username changes, and role assignment. Federating vCenter with an enterprise identity provider automates user registration and termination, further reinforcing the unique ID principle. VCF also supports dynamic group assignment based on SAML and OIDC token assertions, ensuring appropriate role assignments linked to unique user identities. For automated processes, Principal Identity management in NSX provides dedicated service account identities, each uniquely identifiable.

While VCF provides the technical foundation for unique identity management, the customer is responsible for configuring and maintaining their enterprise identity providers to ensure unique IDs are consistently issued and managed according to organizational policy. This includes establishing and enforcing internal processes for user onboarding, offboarding, and identity lifecycle management within their chosen identity provider, which then integrates with VCF.

Description:
8.2.2 Group, shared, or generic IDs, or other shared authentication credentials are only used when necessary on an exception basis, and are managed as follows:
 • ID use is prevented unless needed for an exceptional circumstance.
 • Use is limited to the time needed for the exceptional circumstance.
 • Business justification for use is documented.
 • Use is explicitly approved by management. 
 • Individual user identity is confirmed before access to an account is granted.
 • Every action taken is attributable to an individual user. 
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement is not intended to apply to user accounts within point-of-sale terminals that have access to only one card number at a time to facilitate a single transaction.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms that significantly reduce the necessity for shared or generic credentials and enhance accountability when their use is unavoidable. VCF SSO centralizes authentication and supports identity provider federation, ensuring users authenticate with their individual identity provider credentials, which inherently discourages generic shared accounts and allows for enforcement of individual-tied controls like MFA.

VCF Operations enforces credential ownership, preventing informal sharing by restricting users to credentials they created or were explicitly assigned. Each VCF component supports role-based access control (RBAC), enabling administrators to provision individual user accounts with precise permissions, thereby eliminating the motivation for shared accounts.

Service accounts are specifically designated for API-based access with restricted capabilities. The platform's audit event framework meticulously records each user action, including logins, logouts, and configuration changes, making every action attributable to an individual user.

VCF also employs OAuth 2.0 token-based authentication and certificate-based authentication for service-to-service communication, further reducing scenarios where credentials might be shared.

While VCF provides the technical foundation for individual accountability and discourages shared credentials, the organization remains responsible for establishing and enforcing the policy that prohibits credential sharing. This includes defining the exceptional circumstances under which shared IDs are permitted, documenting business justifications, obtaining explicit management approval for their use, and limiting their use to the time needed.

Furthermore, the confirmation of individual user identity before access to a shared account is granted, and the detection analysis required to identify when a single account is being used by multiple individuals, necessitate external processes, tooling, or human review beyond the platform's inherent capabilities.

Description:
8.2.3 Additional requirement for service providers only: Service providers with remote access to customer premises use unique authentication factors for each customer premises.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement applies only when the entity being assessed is a service provider.
 This requirement is not intended to apply to service providers accessing their own shared services environments, where multiple customer environments are hosted.
 If service provider employees use shared authentication factors to remotely access customer premises, these factors must be unique per customer and managed in accordance with Requirement 8.2.2.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides a comprehensive identity and access management framework that enables service providers to securely manage remote access to customer premises. VCF SSO facilitates identity federation with external identity providers using industry-standard protocols such as SAML 2.0 and OpenID Connect (OIDC). This federation allows VCF to leverage the external IdP's authentication capabilities, including multi-factor authentication (MFA), which can be configured to provide distinct authentication factors. VCF Automation Organizations establish tenant isolation, providing separate quotas, permissions, and network boundaries that create distinct operational environments for each customer. Within these isolated customer environments, VCF enforces granular role-based access control across all management interfaces, including vCenter, NSX, and SDDC Manager, and supports secure remote access methods such as SSH with public-private key authentication. VMware vDefend further enhances security by enforcing network-level access controls through microsegmentation and providing advanced threat detection for third-party access.

While VCF provides the foundational technical capabilities and secure environment, it does not directly manage or enforce the uniqueness of authentication factors across different customer premises from the service provider's perspective. The service provider is responsible for configuring their external identity provider and establishing organizational policies and processes to ensure that the authentication factors used by their employees for remote access are distinct and unique for each customer premises. This includes managing shared authentication factors to ensure they are unique per customer and align with the service provider's broader identity governance program.

Description:
8.2.4 Addition, deletion, and modification of user IDs, authentication factors, and other identifier objects are managed as follows:
 • Authorized with the appropriate approval.
 • Implemented with only the privileges specified on the documented approval.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement applies to all user accounts, including employees, contractors, consultants, temporary workers and third-party vendors.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust technical capabilities for the addition, deletion, and modification of user IDs, authentication factors, and other identifier objects across its components. VCF Single Sign-On (SSO) serves as the centralized identity and authentication service, supporting user and group provisioning through integration with Active Directory, LDAP, and federated identity providers, including Just-In-Time (JIT) provisioning. VCF Operations and VCF Automation offer interfaces for importing user accounts, assigning role-based access, and managing user rights. De-provisioning is effectively handled through identity provider integration, where disabling or removing an account in the federated source automatically revokes VCF access. Explicit user account deactivation and deletion are also supported within VCF Automation and component-specific management interfaces. VCF enforces role-based access control (RBAC) across all components, allowing administrators to adjust or revoke user access rights by modifying role assignments or deactivating accounts. Granular entity access control with defined permission levels further supports the principle of least privilege.

For authenticators, VCF SSO facilitates token-based authentication and supports multifactor authentication (MFA) workflows when federated with enterprise identity providers. Individual VCF components enforce strong password complexity, expiration, and account lockout policies for local accounts. Service-to-service authentication utilizes certificate-based credentials and rotating API access tokens, with SDDC Manager providing certificate lifecycle management. VMware vDefend contributes by supporting RBAC within its management plane, allowing for the update or removal of role assignments for its specific users.

While VCF provides the essential technical mechanisms for managing the lifecycle of user IDs, authentication factors, and access privileges, the platform does not inherently provide standalone registration, formal approval workflows, or periodic entitlement review processes. The organizational responsibility remains with the customer to define and implement the policies, documented approvals, and human processes necessary to authorize additions, deletions, and modifications, ensuring that privileges are granted and adjusted strictly according to documented approval and current job function across all enterprise systems.

Description: 8.2.5 Access for terminated users is immediately revoked
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust technical mechanisms to ensure immediate revocation of logical access for terminated users. Through its centralized identity management, VCF SSO integrates with enterprise identity providers such as Active Directory and LDAP. Disabling or removing a user account in the connected identity source immediately prevents authentication across all VCF components, including vCenter, SDDC Manager, and NSX Manager. This single administrative action at the directory level effectively revokes access across the entire platform.

VCF also offers direct administrative controls within its components, allowing system administrators to deactivate or delete user accounts, revoke individual access tokens, and modify role-based access control (RBAC) assignments. Changes to roles or group memberships are reflected immediately, ensuring that privileges align with current authorization.

Furthermore, VCF Automation enforces deployment lease policies that automatically terminate resource access after expiration, providing an additional safeguard.

VMware vDefend complements these capabilities by enforcing immediate network-level access revocation. Its Distributed Firewall and Gateway Firewall policies can be updated instantly to deny network connectivity for specific users or workloads. The vDefend Identity Firewall integrates with Active Directory, dynamically adjusting network access based on changes to AD group memberships. Microsegmentation policies offer fine-grained control, allowing immediate isolation of workloads or denial of traffic flows upon revocation.

While VCF and vDefend provide the technical enforcement for immediate access revocation, the customer is responsible for establishing and executing the organizational processes that identify terminated users and trigger these technical actions. This includes managing the lifecycle of user accounts within the enterprise identity provider and initiating the necessary administrative steps.

Physical access revocation remains outside the scope of the VCF platform and requires separate facility management processes.

Description: 8.2.6 Inactive user accounts are removed or disabled within 90 days of inactivity.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust capabilities for managing user account states across its components. Within VCF Automation, administrators can disable user accounts and groups through the Access Control interface, preventing login while retaining the account for potential reactivation. Accounts can also be permanently deleted when no longer required. VCF Operations offers similar controls, allowing administrators to deactivate user accounts via an 'Activated' field or disable checkbox, thereby restricting access to VCF Operations features. NSX Manager further supports the activation and deactivation of local user accounts, with Enterprise Admins able to manage these states, including default inactive guest accounts.

While VCF offers comprehensive manual controls for account state management, it does not include a native, built-in automated timer to disable or remove accounts based on a defined period of inactivity. Organizations are responsible for implementing time-based inactivity policies, typically through integration with an external directory service such as Active Directory, LDAP, or an OIDC provider. These external directory services can enforce automated inactivity policies that flow through to VCF access, complementing the platform's direct account management capabilities.

Description:
8.2.7 Accounts used by third parties to access, support, or maintain system components via remote access are managed as follows:
 • Enabled only during the time period needed and disabled when not in use.
 • Use is monitored for unexpected activity.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms for managing and monitoring third-party accounts used for remote access. VCF integrates with external identity providers through VCF SSO, supporting identity federation with enterprise authentication systems like Active Directory or LDAP. User accounts can be imported and assigned granular roles via VCF Access Control, which enforces role-based access control (RBAC) across all VCF components, including vCenter, NSX, and SDDC Manager. VCF Automation further supports service accounts for automated third-party application access. VMware Live Site Recovery (VLR) leverages VCF SSO for authenticating remote maintenance access to its appliance management interface and vSphere Client plug-in. VMware vDefend contributes by enforcing network-level controls through the Distributed Firewall, restricting external or third-party access to internal systems based on least-privilege segmentation rules.

VCF, VLR, and vDefend collectively provide comprehensive monitoring capabilities for third-party account usage. VCF Operations captures platform interactions through its audit events framework, logging user logins, logouts, configuration modifications, and capability checks across vSphere, vSAN, and NSX resources. These audit logs, including NSX CLI logs and VCF Automation audit logs, are tied to authenticated user identities via centralized RBAC and VCF SSO, providing a complete trail of actions. VCF Operations can forward these logs to external syslog servers for centralized security auditing. VLR audits remote maintenance activities through its event logging system, recording configuration changes and DR operations with timestamps and user attribution, and provides session management logging. vDefend supports log forwarding to a remote syslog server and includes a Remote Services detector within its IDS/IPS capabilities to identify suspicious behavior associated with remote connection protocols, while Security Intelligence monitors for anomalous remote access behavior.

While VCF provides the identity integration, RBAC framework, and extensive audit trails, the proactive lifecycle management of third-party accounts, including their time-bound enablement and disablement when not in use, requires organizational processes and potentially a dedicated Privileged Access Management (PAM) solution. The continuous monitoring of user security aspects to detect risks and verify appropriate access levels also remains an operational responsibility of the customer.

Description:
8.2.8 If a user session has been idle for more than 15 minutes, the user is required to re-authenticate to re-activate the terminal or session.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement is not intended to apply to user accounts on point-of-sale terminals that have access to only one card number at a time to facilitate a single transaction.
 This requirement is not meant to prevent legitimate activities from being performed while the console/PC is unattended.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust mechanisms for managing user session inactivity and enforcing re-authentication across its management interfaces.

VCF components, including vCenter, NSX Manager, VCF Automation, and VCF Operations for Networks, support configurable session timeouts that automatically terminate idle user sessions. For instance, VCF Operations for Networks defaults to a 15-minute user session timeout, and NSX Manager sessions expire after 30 minutes of inactivity by default, both of which are adjustable.

When a session terminates due to inactivity, the associated authentication token is invalidated, requiring the user to re-authenticate to regain access. The vSphere Web Services API and PowerCLI sessions also offer configurable timeout durations and explicit logout methods, ensuring that API-based access sessions can be managed and terminated programmatically. VCF's Single Sign-On (SSO) integration issues time-limited authentication tokens that necessitate re-authentication upon expiration, further contributing to session security.

VMware vDefend complements these management plane controls by providing configurable session timers and connection state management for workload network traffic.

The vDefend Distributed Firewall maintains session state for all connections and applies configurable timers that terminate network connections after periods of inactivity. These timers operate at the vNIC level on every ESXi host, governing workload-to-workload connections.

When a session timer expires, the firewall removes the connection entry, effectively terminating the session and logging the reason. Granular TCP timer controls, including Closing and FIN timeouts, are configurable through session timer profiles, allowing organizations to align timeout behavior with specific workload requirements and security policies.

While VCF and vDefend provide the essential technical capabilities for session termination and re-authentication, the specific policy decisions, including the exact 15-minute inactivity threshold and the overall re-authentication strategy, remain an organizational responsibility. Customers must configure the session timeout values across VCF components to align with their defined security policies. Furthermore, VCF terminates idle sessions rather than locking them; therefore, organizations must implement workstation-level screen lock policies to address the "terminal" aspect of the control and ensure comprehensive protection when consoles or PCs are unattended.

Description: 8.3 Strong authentication for users and administrators is established and managed.
Response:
The platform provides a robust, centralized authentication, authorization, and auditing (AAA) model through VCF SSO, which acts as the identity broker for all VCF components including vCenter, ESX, NSX, SDDC Manager, VCF Operations, and VCF Automation. All user and service authentication flows through VCF SSO, issuing SAML tokens and supporting identity federation with enterprise identity providers via SAML or OIDC. This federation enables multifactor authentication (MFA) and automated user lifecycle management, with the Authorization Code grant type in VMware Identity Broker specifically supporting MFA workflows.

For local accounts, VCF components enforce stringent password complexity, length, and lifespan requirements. NSX Manager mandates a minimum of 12 characters with diverse character types and a configurable 90-day expiration, while VCF Operations enforces a minimum of 15 characters with similar complexity and configurable account lockout. VCF Automation also supports account lockout.

Service-to-service authentication utilizes certificate-based Principal Identity users and API access tokens, eliminating password credentials. SDDC Manager provides centralized certificate lifecycle management, integrating with external CAs, and supports non-disruptive certificate rotation. SDDC Manager also offers centralized password lifecycle management for VCF components, enabling on-demand or scheduled rotation with randomized, policy-compliant passwords and automatic re-establishment of integrations.

VMware vDefend enforces strong authentication for its management plane, supporting local user accounts and integration with directory services like Active Directory over LDAPS. The Security Services Platform (SSP) mandates a minimum of 12 characters with diverse character types for local user passwords, valid for 180 days, with notifications prior to expiration. NSX Manager and NSX Edge, which manage vDefend firewall policies, enforce similar stringent password complexity, length, and expiration policies, including restrictions against dictionary words and monotonic sequences. Integration with enterprise directory services allows organizations to extend their existing password policies to vDefend administrative access.

While the platform provides comprehensive technical controls for strong authentication, authenticator strength, complexity, expiration, and rotation within its components, the broader organizational policies governing identity governance, identity provider infrastructure management, role assignment procedures, and authenticator management across all enterprise systems remain the responsibility of the customer.

Description:
8.3.1 All user access to system components for users and administrators is authenticated via at least one of the following authentication factors:
 • Something you know, such as a password or passphrase.
 • Something you have, such as a token device or smart card.
 • Something you are, such as a biometric element.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement is not intended to apply to user accounts on point-of-sale terminals that have access to only one card number at a time to facilitate a single transaction.
 This requirement does not supersede multi-factor authentication (MFA) requirements but applies to those in-scope systems not otherwise subject to MFA requirements.
 A digital certificate is a valid option for “something you have” if it is unique for a particular user.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) ensures that all user and administrator access to its system components is authenticated using at least one robust factor. VCF enforces stringent password complexity, length, and lifespan requirements across all platform components, including NSX Manager, NSX Edge, VCF Operations, and VCF Automation. For instance, NSX Manager mandates passwords of at least 12 characters with diverse character types and a 90-day expiration, while VCF Operations requires a minimum of 15 characters with similar complexity and account lockout policies. SDDC Manager centralizes password lifecycle management, enabling on-demand or scheduled rotation of randomized passwords that adhere to component-specific policies. VCF SSO further supports federation with enterprise identity providers, allowing the application of organizational password policies and enabling token-based authentication.

Beyond passwords, VCF extensively utilizes digital certificates as a "something you have" authentication factor. Solution users, facilitating service-to-service communication between VCF components, authenticate using certificates, with the platform validating the certificate chain for each connection. VCF components validate certificates by constructing and verifying certification paths to trusted anchors during TLS communication and authentication. VCF includes the VMware Certificate Authority (VMCA), which can operate as a subordinate CA to an enterprise PKI, establishing verifiable certification paths. VMware vDefend and VMware Live Recovery similarly leverage PKI-based authentication. vDefend's Security Services Platform (SSP) maintains its own certificate hierarchy for component authentication and secures connections to external identity providers, while its NDR Sensor uses PKIX certificate path validation. VMware Live Recovery uses PKI-based authentication for site pairing and appliance management, verifying certificates during configuration and securing replication channels with certificate-based authentication.

While VCF, vDefend, and VLR provide the technical controls for enforcing strong password policies and utilizing certificate-based authentication for various access types, the broader organizational policies governing authenticator lifecycle management, including specific password policy parameters, certificate revocation checking (CRL/OCSP), and integration with external certificate authorities, remain the responsibility of the customer. The customer must define and implement these overarching policies and processes to ensure comprehensive authenticator management across their entire environment.

Description: 8.3.2 Strong cryptography is used to render all authentication factors unreadable during transmission and storage on all system components.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) inherently employs strong cryptography to protect authentication factors during transmission and storage across its core components. All VCF components utilize FIPS 140-3 validated cryptographic modules. Internal VCF communications, including those involving authentication, default to TLS 1.3, with TLS 1.2 as a compatibility fallback, and all system management network communications are encrypted by default using standardized cipher suites. ESX hosts store configuration data, which includes authentication-related information, in encrypted form on boot devices, with the encryption key protected by TPM 2.0 when enabled. VCF Operations provides centralized certificate lifecycle management, supporting automated renewal and replacement for secure trust establishment.

This cryptographic protection extends to authentication for specific services and workloads. Secure LDAPS connections for platform authentication are supported through certificate validation. VMware Live Recovery (VLR) ensures all communications with vCenter instances occur over TLS connections, and site pairing uses certificate-based trust establishment. VMware vDefend secures inter-component communications using TLS 1.2 with approved cipher suites (e.g., AES-GCM, ECDHE) and encrypts its stored data using AES 256-bit. For virtual machines, VCF's vSAN cluster-based encryption and VM Encryption protect data at rest, including any authentication factors stored within VMs, across all datastore types. Encrypted vSphere vMotion further secures authentication-related data during live migrations by using one-time keys for each session.

While the platform provides robust cryptographic mechanisms, customers are responsible for configuring and enforcing these capabilities according to their organizational security policies. This includes managing external Key Management Servers (KMS) or configuring the Native Key Provider, ensuring appropriate VM encryption policies are applied, and maintaining the security of any guest-level authentication factors through in-guest encryption or other operating system-native features. Customers must also ensure that any third-party applications or custom solutions deployed on VCF adhere to strong cryptographic practices for their authentication factors.

Description: 8.3.3 User identity is verified before modifying any authentication factor.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) verifies user identity before issuing authenticators or modifying access permissions through its centralized VCF SSO service. All user authentication flows through VCF SSO, which validates identity against configured identity sources, ensuring identity verification occurs through trusted sources before authentication tokens or permissions are provisioned. Permissions on managed objects can only be set or updated through the AuthorizationManager API after the calling user has been authenticated. VCF supports federation with external identity providers using SAML or OIDC, enabling multifactor authentication and automatic account synchronization, which strengthens identity verification before credentials are issued. VCF Operations audit reports capture authentication events, providing traceability of identity validation actions. VCF also provides built-in capabilities for managing authenticators, including centralized password lifecycle management through SDDC Manager for VCF components and certificate lifecycle management for service-to-service authentication.

VMware vDefend's Security Services Platform supports authentication for its own management plane using local user accounts or directory-managed accounts, enforcing password complexity, length, and lifespan requirements for administrative access to its security functions.

While VCF provides the technical mechanisms for identity verification prior to authenticator modification, the broader organizational processes for defining identity governance policies, managing identity provider infrastructure, and establishing role assignment procedures remain customer responsibilities. For vDefend, while it enforces password policies and integrates with directory services for its management plane, the explicit identity verification step *before* modifying an authentication factor, beyond the initial authentication to access the management plane, relies on the broader VCF SSO or federated identity provider for centralized organizational user management.

Description:
8.3.4 Invalid authentication attempts are limited by: 
 • Locking out the user ID after not more than 10 attempts.
 • Setting the lockout duration to a minimum of 30 minutes or until the user’s identity is confirmed. 
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement is not intended to apply to user accounts on point-of-sale terminals that have access to only one card number at a time to facilitate a single transaction.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation enforces robust account lockout policies across its management components to limit invalid authentication attempts. VCF Operations locks accounts after a configurable number of failed login attempts, with a default threshold of seven attempts. For VCF Operations for Networks, accounts are locked after five consecutive failed attempts, with an initial lockout duration of 15 minutes that increases with subsequent failures up to a maximum of one day. The super administrator account lockout policy is activated by default and cannot be deactivated. VCF Automation supports account lockout after a configurable number of invalid login attempts when enabled through password policy system settings, allowing administrators to manually unlock accounts if necessary. NSX Manager provides configurable lockout periods and maximum authentication failure thresholds via CLI commands, with default lockout and reset periods set to 900 seconds.

While the platform provides the necessary technical controls for limiting invalid authentication attempts, administrators are responsible for configuring these settings across all VCF components to align with organizational security policies. This includes ensuring that the maximum number of invalid attempts does not exceed 10 and that the lockout duration is set to a minimum of 30 minutes or until the user's identity is confirmed, as some default settings may be lower than the specified minimum duration.

Description:
8.3.5 If passwords/passphrases are used as authentication factors to meet Requirement 8.3.1, they are set and reset for each user as follows: 
 • Set to a unique value for first-time use and upon reset.
 • Forced to be changed immediately after the first use.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust technical capabilities for managing authenticators and enforcing password policies across its components. VCF SSO serves as the centralized authentication service, supporting integration with enterprise identity providers like Active Directory or LDAP for user provisioning and authentication.

For local accounts, VCF components such as NSX Manager, VCF Operations, and VCF Automation enforce stringent password complexity, length, and lifespan requirements. NSX Manager mandates a minimum of 12 characters with diverse character types and a configurable expiration, while VCF Operations requires a minimum of 15 characters with similar complexity rules. SDDC Manager offers centralized password lifecycle management, enabling administrators to rotate passwords on demand or on a scheduled basis, generating randomized passwords that conform to component policies. For service-to-service authentication, VCF utilizes certificate-based credentials and API access tokens that rotate after each use.

While VCF provides the technical enforcement layer for password policies and lifecycle management, the specific requirements for setting unique initial passwords for first-time user accounts and forcing an immediate password change after the first login are typically managed by the integrated enterprise identity provider or require specific organizational processes and configurations beyond the direct scope of VCF's built-in capabilities.

PAIF: Not Assessed | VLR: Not Applicable | vDefend: Contributes | VMware vDefend enforces strong password complexity, length, and lifespan requirements across its management interfaces, contributing to the organization's overall password-based authentication posture.

The Security Services Platform (SSP), vDefend's centralized management console, enforces password policies for local user accounts, requiring a minimum of 12 characters with diverse character types and enforcing validity periods of 180 days for admin/audit users and 60 days for installer users. NSX Manager, which manages vDefend firewall policies, enforces similar password complexity through Linux PAM modules, including a minimum of 12 characters and a default 90-day expiration. SSP also supports integration with directory services such as Active Directory over LDAPS, allowing organizations to apply their enterprise-wide password policies to vDefend administrative access through federated authentication.

vDefend contributes to secure password management by enforcing complexity and lifespan, but the explicit mechanisms for setting unique initial passwords for first-time local user accounts and mandating an immediate password change after the first use are not detailed as being directly enforced by vDefend itself. These aspects often rely on the integrated directory service or organizational procedures.

Description:
8.3.6 If passwords/passphrases are used as authentication factors to meet Requirement 8.3.1, they meet the following minimum level of complexity: 
 • A minimum length of 12 characters (or IF the system does not support 12 characters, a minimum length of eight characters).
 • Contain both numeric and alphabetic characters.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement is not intended to apply to: 
 • User accounts on point-of-sale terminals that have access to only one card number at a time to facilitate a single transaction.
 • Application or system accounts, which are governed by requirements in section 8.6.
 This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
 Until 31 March 2025, passwords must be a minimum length of seven characters in accordance with PCI DSS v3.2.1 Requirement 8.2.3.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) and VMware vDefend components enforce robust password complexity, length, and lifespan requirements for administrative access to their management interfaces.

NSX Manager and NSX Edge, integral to both VCF and vDefend, enforce password complexity via Linux PAM modules, requiring a minimum of 12 characters (up to 128), including at least one lowercase, one uppercase, one numeric, and one special character, along with a minimum of five different characters. NSX Edge further restricts dictionary words, palindromes, and monotonic sequences. VCF Operations enforces an even stronger minimum password length of 15 characters, requiring uppercase, lowercase, numeric, and special characters. The Security Services Platform (SSP), vDefend's centralized management console, mandates a minimum of 12 characters (up to 128) for Admin and Audit user passwords, requiring at least one uppercase, one lowercase, one numeric digit, and one special character.

These platforms also implement account lockout mechanisms, such as VCF Operations locking accounts after five failed attempts for 15 minutes, and NSX Manager enforcing a retry limit of three attempts when setting new passwords. Password expiration policies are also enforced, with NSX Manager defaulting to 90 days and SSP local user passwords valid for 180 days.

SDDC Manager provides centralized password lifecycle management for VCF components, enabling on-demand or scheduled automatic rotation of randomized passwords that conform to component policies.

While the platform components provide and enforce these strong default password policies, customers are responsible for configuring and managing these settings within the provided administrative interfaces to align with their organizational security policies and to ensure compliance with specific regulatory requirements. This includes leveraging the configurable parameters for password length, character types, and expiration periods, as well as integrating with enterprise directory services like Active Directory over LDAPS or OpenLDAP via SSP for federated authentication where applicable.

Description:
8.3.7 Individuals are not allowed to submit a new password/passphrase that is the same as any of the last four passwords/passphrases used.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement is not intended to apply to user accounts on point-of-sale terminals that have access to only one card number at a time to facilitate a single transaction.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust capabilities for managing authenticator strength, complexity, and lifecycle across its components. NSX Manager, NSX Edge, VCF Operations, and vDefend management interfaces enforce stringent password complexity rules, including minimum length, character class requirements (uppercase, lowercase, numeric, special), and restrictions against dictionary words or monotonic sequences. Password expiration policies are configurable, with default validity periods ranging from 60 to 180 days, and account lockout mechanisms are in place for repeated failed login attempts. SDDC Manager centralizes password lifecycle management, supporting on-demand or scheduled automatic rotation of randomized passwords for VCF components, ensuring adherence to their respective policies.

While VCF components enforce comprehensive password policies for strength, length, and expiration, the platform does not include a native mechanism to prevent individuals from reusing any of their last four passwords or passphrases. Organizations must implement this specific password history enforcement through an integrated enterprise identity provider or other external authentication services that manage user accounts federated with VCF SSO, or through policies applied to local accounts where applicable.

SCF ID: GOV-02, HRS-04.2, IAC-01, OPS-01, OPS-01.1, SAT-01, SAT-02, SAT-03
Description:
8.3.8 Authentication policies and procedures are documented and communicated to all users including: 
 • Guidance on selecting strong authentication factors.
 • Guidance for how users should protect their authentication factors.
 • Instructions not to reuse previously used passwords/passphrases.
 • Instructions to change passwords/passphrases if there is any suspicion or knowledge that the password/passphrases have been compromised and how to report the incident.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides a robust, layered identity and access management architecture centered on VCF SSO, which serves as the centralized authentication service. This architecture supports identity federation with enterprise identity providers such as Microsoft Entra ID, ADFS, and Okta, enabling multifactor authentication and automatic user synchronization with organizational directories.

Role-based access control is enforced across all VCF components, including vCenter, SDDC Manager, NSX Manager, VCF Operations, VCF Automation, and Kubernetes workloads, ensuring that users and groups are assigned only the minimum required privileges.

Solution users authenticate between VCF components using certificates for secure service-to-service communications.

VMware vDefend further contributes by providing identity-aware network enforcement through Identity Firewall, which integrates with Active Directory to apply firewall policies based on authenticated user identity. Within its own management plane, vDefend's Security Services Platform and Security Intelligence implement RBAC with defined roles to control access to specific security functions.

While the platform provides the technical foundation for strong authentication and access control, the organization is responsible for defining, documenting, and communicating specific authentication policies and procedures to all users. This includes establishing guidance for selecting and protecting authentication factors, mandating non-reuse of passwords/passphrases, and outlining procedures for reporting and responding to compromised credentials.

The technical capabilities of VCF enable the enforcement of these policies, but the policy content, documentation, and communication to end-users remain an organizational responsibility.

Description:
8.3.9 If passwords/passphrases are used as the only authentication factor for user access (i.e., in any single-factor authentication implementation) then either:
 • Passwords/passphrases are changed at least once every 90 days, 
 OR
 • The security posture of accounts is dynamically analyzed, and real-time access to resources is automatically determined accordingly. 
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement does not apply to in-scope system components where MFA is used.
 This requirement is not intended to apply to user accounts on point-of-sale terminals that have access to only one card number at a time to facilitate a single transaction.
 This requirement does not apply to service providers’ customer accounts but does apply to accounts for service provider personnel.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust capabilities for managing user authentication, including centralized authentication via VCF SSO, which acts as the identity broker for all VCF components. VCF SSO supports identity federation with enterprise identity providers through SAML or OIDC, enabling multifactor authentication (MFA) workflows via the Authorization Code grant type. When MFA is utilized, this control's requirements for password changes or dynamic analysis do not apply.

For single-factor authentication implementations, VCF components enforce stringent password policies. NSX Manager, NSX Edge, and VCF Operations enforce minimum password lengths (e.g., 12 characters for NSX, 15 for VCF Operations) and complexity requirements, including uppercase, lowercase, numeric, and special characters. NSX Manager defaults to a 90-day password expiration, which is configurable. SDDC Manager offers centralized password lifecycle management, allowing administrators to rotate passwords on demand or schedule automatic rotations, generating randomized passwords that conform to component policies.

VMware vDefend's management interfaces, including the Security Services Platform (SSP) and integrated NSX components, also enforce password complexity, length, and lifespan policies for administrative access. SSP local accounts require a minimum of 12 characters with complexity rules and have a 180-day validity period, while NSX Manager enforces a 90-day default expiration.

While VCF and vDefend provide the technical mechanisms to enforce strong password policies and support MFA, the organization is responsible for defining and implementing the overarching identity governance policies. This includes establishing the policy for single-factor authentication usage, ensuring the 90-day password change requirement is enforced for applicable accounts, or implementing a system for dynamic security posture analysis and real-time access determination as an alternative. The management of the enterprise identity provider infrastructure and the establishment of role assignment procedures also remain organizational responsibilities.

Description:
8.3.10 Additional requirement for service providers only: If passwords/passphrases are used as the only authentication factor for customer user access to cardholder data (i.e., in any single-factor authentication implementation), then guidance is provided to customer users including:
 • Guidance for customers to change their user passwords/passphrases periodically.
 • Guidance as to when, and under what circumstances, passwords/passphrases are to be changed.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement applies only when the entity being assessed is a service provider.
 This requirement does not apply to accounts of consumer users accessing their own payment card information.
 This requirement for service providers will be superseded by Requirement 8.3.10.1 once 8.3.10.1 becomes effective.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) and vDefend Security Services Platform provide robust account management and identity services for their respective management planes and infrastructure components. VCF Single Sign-On (SSO) serves as a centralized identity service, enabling administrators to manage individual and group accounts and integrate with external identity sources such as Active Directory, LDAP, and OIDC. This allows for the enforcement of enterprise account lifecycle processes and multi-factor authentication for administrative access to VCF management consoles like vCenter and SDDC Manager.

Similarly, vDefend Security Services Platform manages its own built-in local user accounts and supports directory-managed accounts via external identity providers, ensuring defined account types and privileges within its management plane.

While these platforms offer foundational identity and access management capabilities for their operational integrity, they do not directly provide guidance to customer users regarding password changes for customer-facing applications that access cardholder data. The control specifically requires the provision of guidance on periodic password changes and circumstances for such changes to customer users when single-factor authentication is employed. This responsibility, including the definition of organizational policies, processes, and the communication of guidance to end-users of applications hosted on the platform, remains with the customer.

Description:
8.3.10.1 Additional requirement for service providers only: If passwords/passphrases are used as the only authentication factor for customer user access (i.e., in any single-factor authentication implementation) then either:
 • Passwords/passphrases are changed at least once every 90 days, 
 OR
 • The security posture of accounts is dynamically analyzed, and real-time access to resources is automatically determined accordingly.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement applies only when the entity being assessed is a service provider.
 This requirement does not apply to accounts of consumer users accessing their own payment card information.
 This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
 Until this requirement is effective on 31 March 2025, service providers may meet either Requirement 8.3.10 or 8.3.10.1.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust technical controls for managing authenticators across its components. VCF SSO facilitates centralized authentication and can federate with enterprise identity providers, enabling multifactor authentication (MFA) and token-based authentication, which enhances security posture.

For local accounts, VCF components such as NSX Manager, VCF Operations, and vDefend's Security Services Platform (SSP) enforce strong password complexity, length, and lifecycle policies. NSX Manager defaults to a 90-day password expiration, configurable between 1 and 9,999 days, and enforces a minimum of 12 characters with diverse character requirements. VCF Operations mandates a minimum of 15 characters with similar complexity rules and configurable account lockout policies. SDDC Manager offers centralized password lifecycle management, allowing administrators to rotate passwords on demand or schedule automatic rotation, generating randomized passwords that conform to component policies. VCF also utilizes certificate-based credentials for service-to-service authentication and provides certificate lifecycle management through SDDC Manager.

While VCF provides the foundational technical capabilities for strong authenticator management, including password complexity, expiration, and rotation, the service provider is responsible for defining and enforcing the overarching organizational policies that govern authenticator lifecycle management across all systems, particularly for customer user access. The control specifically addresses scenarios where passwords are the *only* authentication factor for customer users. The service provider must ensure that the 90-day password change requirement is consistently applied and configured across all relevant customer-facing authentication mechanisms, or implement dynamic analysis of account security posture and real-time access determination, leveraging VCF's capabilities where applicable. The platform provides the tools, but the specific implementation and policy enforcement for customer access remain the service provider's responsibility.

Description:
8.3.11 Where authentication factors such as physical or logical security tokens, smart cards, or certificates are used: 
 • Factors are assigned to an individual user and not shared among multiple users. 
 • Physical and/or logical controls ensure only the intended user can use that factor to gain access.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) implements robust mechanisms for managing authentication factors such as tokens and certificates. VCF SSO serves as the centralized authentication service, issuing time-limited bearer and holder-of-key tokens for single sign-on and delegation, which inherently limits the window of exposure for credentials. Federation with an enterprise identity provider enables multifactor authentication (MFA) and automatic user provisioning and deprovisioning, ensuring that logical authentication factors are associated with individual users. For service-to-service communication, VCF components authenticate using X.509 certificates, with SDDC Manager providing comprehensive certificate lifecycle management, including integration with external Certificate Authorities. VCF validates certificate chains during authentication, verifying the authenticity of these factors.

While VCF provides the technical controls for the use and validation of tokens and certificates within its domain, the overarching enforcement of individual assignment and exclusive use for *all* authentication factors, including physical security tokens or smart cards, relies on broader organizational identity and access management policies. The customer is responsible for establishing and enforcing policies that explicitly prohibit the sharing of authentication factors and for implementing physical and logical controls, often managed by the enterprise identity provider or external systems, to ensure that only the intended user can utilize their assigned factor to gain access to VCF and integrated systems.

Description: 8.4 Multi-factor authentication (MFA) systems are configured to prevent misuse.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) supports multi-factor authentication (MFA) by integrating with external enterprise identity providers through federation. VCF's Identity Broker facilitates this integration, allowing VCF Single Sign-On (SSO) to federate with providers using industry-standard protocols such as SAML 2.0 and OpenID Connect (OIDC). This capability enables administrators to enforce MFA for all interactive access to VCF management interfaces, including vCenter and SDDC Manager. The recommended authentication flow for MFA-capable scenarios is the OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code grant type, which is fully supported by both vCenter and the VCF Identity Broker. Non-interactive access for solution user accounts relies on certificate-based authentication, providing a distinct trust model.

VCF does not include a native, built-in MFA mechanism. The organization is responsible for deploying and configuring an external identity provider that supports MFA policies and then federating VCF SSO to that provider. To ensure MFA enforcement, the identity provider federation must be configured to utilize the Authorization Code grant type, as the Password grant type is a single-factor method incompatible with MFA requirements. The customer is solely accountable for the deployment, configuration, and ongoing management of the external identity provider and its associated MFA policies.

Description:
8.4.1 MFA is implemented for all non-console access into the CDE for personnel with administrative access. 
 Applicability Notes
 The requirement for MFA for non-console administrative access applies to all personnel with elevated or increased privileges accessing the CDE via a non-console connection—that is, via logical access occurring over a network interface rather than via a direct, physical connection.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation facilitates multi-factor authentication for privileged personnel accessing the CDE through its management interfaces. All administrative access to VCF components, including vCenter, SDDC Manager, NSX Manager, and VCF Operations, is routed through VCF SSO. This SSO mechanism supports federation with enterprise identity providers using industry-standard protocols such as SAML 2.0 or OIDC. By configuring federation with the OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code grant type, VCF ensures that users holding privileged roles—such as the Administrator system role in vCenter, CloudAdmin, Assembler Administrator, or Cloud Administrator for VCF Operations for Networks—are directed to the external identity provider for authentication. This architecture allows the organization's identity provider to enforce its established MFA policies before granting access to VCF components.

While VCF provides the necessary integration points and architectural support, it does not include a native multi-factor authentication mechanism. The organization is responsible for deploying and configuring an external identity provider that supports MFA, defining and enforcing appropriate MFA policies within that provider, and subsequently federating VCF SSO to this external identity source. Service-to-service authentication between VCF components utilizes certificate-based solution user accounts, which operate independently of this MFA flow for personnel.

Description:
8.4.2 MFA is implemented for all non-console access into the CDE.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement does not apply to: 
 • Application or system accounts performing automated functions.
 • User accounts on point-of-sale terminals that have access to only one card number at a time to facilitate a single transaction.
 • User accounts that are only authenticated with phishing-resistant authentication factors.
 MFA is required for both types of access specified in Requirements 8.4.2 and 8.4.3. Therefore, applying MFA to one type of access does not replace the need to apply another instance of MFA to the other type of access. If an individual first connects to the entity’s network via remote access, and then later initiates a connection into the CDE from within the network, per this requirement the individual would authenticate using MFA twice, once when connecting via remote access to the entity’s network and once when connecting from the entity’s network into the CDE.
 The MFA requirements apply for all types of system components, including cloud, hosted systems, and on-premises applications, network security devices, workstations, servers, and endpoints, and includes access directly to an entity’s networks or systems as well as web-based access to an application or function. 
 MFA for access into the CDE can be implemented at the network or system/application level; it does not have to be applied at both levels. For example, if MFA is used when a user connects to the CDE network, it does not have to be used when the user logs into each system or application within the CDE.
 This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) supports multi-factor authentication (MFA) for all non-console, interactive access to its management interfaces, including vCenter, SDDC Manager, NSX Manager, VCF Operations, and PowerCLI. This capability is realized through identity provider federation, where VCF SSO integrates with external enterprise identity providers (IdPs) such as Okta, Ping Identity, or Microsoft AD FS using standard protocols like SAML 2.0 or OpenID Connect (OIDC). The VCF Identity Broker mediates this integration, ensuring that all interactive authentication flows through the federated IdP.

The OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code grant type is the compatible and recommended authentication flow for MFA, supporting both human user access via web interfaces and API/PowerCLI interactions. Non-interactive access, such as that performed by VCF solution user accounts, utilizes certificate-based authentication, which operates outside the MFA flow.

While VCF provides the necessary federation infrastructure and compatible authentication protocols, it does not include a built-in MFA mechanism. Organizations must deploy and configure an external identity provider that supports MFA policies and then federate VCF SSO to that provider. The enforcement of MFA, including the selection and delivery of out-of-band factors (e.g., push notifications, SMS, hardware tokens), is solely the responsibility of the external identity provider. Additionally, organizations must ensure that the correct OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code grant type is configured for federation, as the Password grant type is incompatible with MFA. For any local VCF accounts that are not federated, MFA is not available, requiring the organization to rely on other compensating controls such as strong password policies, account lockout, and session timeouts.

Description:
8.4.3 MFA is implemented for all remote network access originating from outside the entity’s network that could access or impact the CDE.
 Applicability Notes
 The requirement for MFA for remote access originating from outside the entity’s network applies to all user accounts that can access the network remotely, where that remote access leads to or could lead to access into the CDE. This includes all remote access by personnel (users and administrators), and third parties (including, but not limited to, vendors, suppliers, service providers, and customers).
 If remote access is to a part of the entity’s network that is properly segmented from the CDE, such that remote users cannot access or impact the CDE, MFA for remote access to that part of the network is not required. However, MFA is required for any remote access to networks with access to the CDE and is recommended for all remote access to the entity’s networks.
 The MFA requirements apply for all types of system components, including cloud, hosted systems, and on-premises applications, network security devices, workstations, servers, and endpoints, and includes access directly to an entity’s networks or systems as well as web-based access to an application or function.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) supports multi-factor authentication (MFA) for remote network access to its management interfaces, including vCenter, SDDC Manager, NSX Manager, and VCF Operations. This capability is realized through identity provider federation, where VCF SSO integrates with an enterprise identity provider using standards-based protocols such as SAML 2.0 or OpenID Connect (OIDC).

The VCF Identity Broker facilitates this integration, enabling administrators to enforce MFA for all interactive access, encompassing both privileged and non-privileged accounts. The OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code grant type is the compatible and recommended authentication flow for MFA, supported by both vCenter and the VCF Identity Broker. This ensures that all interactive authentication to VCF management components flows through the external provider, where MFA policies are enforced.

VCF does not include a built-in MFA mechanism; the actual MFA capability, including token generation, push notifications, or biometric verification, is provided by an external identity provider. Organizations must deploy and configure an identity provider that supports MFA policies and then federate VCF SSO to it.

Furthermore, while VCF provides the necessary infrastructure for its management plane, the organization is responsible for ensuring that MFA is implemented for *all* remote network access originating from outside the entity’s network that could access or impact the CDE, including any direct access paths to workloads or other components within the CDE that may not be solely managed through VCF's federated identity.

The use of the Password grant type is a single-factor method and is incompatible with MFA requirements, necessitating the correct configuration of the Authorization Code grant type. Non-interactive, service-to-service authentication between VCF components utilizes certificate-based solution user accounts, which operate outside the MFA flow.

Description: 8.5 Multi-factor authentication is implemented to secure access to the CDE.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
8.5.1 MFA systems are implemented as follows:
 • The MFA system is not susceptible to replay attacks.
 • MFA systems cannot be bypassed by any users, including administrative users unless specifically documented, and authorized by management on an exception basis, for a limited time period. 
 • At least two different types of authentication factors are used.
 • Success of all authentication factors is required before access is granted.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides a layered identity and access management architecture, leveraging VCF SSO as the centralized authentication service. VCF SSO supports identity federation with enterprise identity providers such as Microsoft Entra ID, ADFS, and Okta, enabling multifactor authentication and automatic user synchronization. The VCF Identity Broker mediates this integration, supporting SAML 2.0 and OpenID Connect (OIDC) protocols, with documented configuration paths for various providers. The OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code grant type is the recommended flow for MFA-capable authentication, compatible with both vCenter and the VCF Identity Broker.

VCF employs several authentication mechanisms designed for replay resistance across the platform. VCF SSO uses time-limited SAML tokens with holder-of-key artifacts, and the PasswordProtectedTransport method requires credentials over HTTPS, providing transport-layer replay protection through TLS. NSX Manager authentication mechanisms are specifically designed to be resistant to replay attacks, and NSX supports session-based authentication using session cookies. For API and webhook security, VCF utilizes hash-based message authentication codes (HMAC) to prevent tampering and replay, and the VCF Automation API uses rotating authentication tokens.

VCF does not include a built-in MFA mechanism; the MFA capability is provided by the external identity provider. Organizations must deploy and configure an identity provider that supports MFA policies and then federate VCF SSO to that provider, ensuring the use of the Authorization Code grant type for MFA enforcement. The organization is also responsible for configuring identity federation, enforcing TLS across the environment, selecting identity providers that implement appropriate replay protections, and defining policies for any authorized MFA bypass exceptions, including documentation, management authorization, and time limitations. For non-interactive access, solution user accounts authenticate between VCF components using certificates, which operate on a distinct trust model not reliant on MFA.

Not Applicable | VMware vDefend contributes to identity-aware network enforcement through its Identity Firewall, which integrates with Active Directory to apply firewall policies based on authenticated user identity. Within its management plane, vDefend's Security Services Platform and Security Intelligence implement role-based access control (RBAC) with defined roles to manage access to specific security functions. However, centralized identity and access management for organizational users, including single sign-on (SSO), multifactor authentication (MFA), and directory lifecycle management, is handled by vCenter/VCF SSO and falls outside vDefend's scope. Therefore, vDefend does not directly implement or enforce the multifactor authentication system requirements of this control.

Description: 8.6 Use of application and system accounts and associated authentication factors are strictly managed.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive capabilities for strictly managing application and system accounts and their associated authentication factors within its infrastructure platform. VCF SSO serves as the centralized identity service, enabling administrators to manage individual, group, system, and service accounts through vCenter and integrate external identity sources such as Active Directory, LDAP, or OIDC for streamlined lifecycle management.

VCF Operations extends this with robust user account management, including creation, editing, deletion, import/export, and role assignment. Each VCF component, including NSX Manager, ESX hosts (brokered via vCenter), and SDDC Manager, implements account management appropriate to its function, with SDDC Manager provider management accounts utilizing API access tokens that rotate after each use.

Role-based access control (RBAC) is consistently enforced across all VCF components, supporting the principle of least privilege through granular role definitions and custom roles that map directly to specific operations. VCF centralizes authentication to privileged interfaces, eliminating direct ESX host access and routing all administrative sessions through vCenter. Service accounts are separated from interactive user accounts and restricted to API-based access, simplifying review.

Audit logging capabilities in VCF Operations and VCF Operations for Networks provide detailed user activity reports, aiding in the identification of inactive accounts or anomalous activity. VMware vDefend further contributes by managing its own built-in local user accounts and supporting directory-managed accounts via external identity providers for its management plane, while enforcing least privilege through its Distributed Firewall for workload network access and RBAC for its administrative interfaces. VMware Live Recovery supports least privilege through granular roles and custom role creation for disaster recovery operations.

While the platform provides robust technical controls for account management, authentication, and access enforcement, the broader organizational governance of account lifecycle management across all enterprise systems remains a customer responsibility.

This includes defining organizational policies for provisioning approvals, periodic access reviews, account termination procedures, and temporary account duration limits. VCF does not natively associate accounts with business processes or owners, nor does it automatically flag orphaned accounts; organizations must implement external identity governance tools to maintain these mappings and drive comprehensive access reviews.

Furthermore, the overall organizational policies governing which privileged utilities are approved and who may use them are external requirements. vDefend's account management capabilities are limited to its own management plane, and it does not directly restrict OS-level or third-party utility programs capable of overriding system and application controls, which falls under endpoint privilege management and system hardening solutions.

Description:
8.6.1 If accounts used by systems or applications can be used for interactive login, they are managed as follows: 
 • Interactive use is prevented unless needed for an exceptional circumstance.
 • Interactive use is limited to the time needed for the exceptional circumstance.
 • Business justification for interactive use is documented.
 • Interactive use is explicitly approved by management. 
 • Individual user identity is confirmed before access to account is granted.
 • Every action taken is attributable to an individual user. 
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) centralizes authentication through VCF SSO, which supports identity federation with enterprise identity providers such as Microsoft Entra ID, ADFS, and Okta. This architecture confirms individual user identity before access is granted and enables multifactor authentication.

VCF eliminates direct access to ESX hosts, routing all administrative sessions through vCenter, and ensures privileged utilities like ESX Shell and SSH are accessible only when explicitly enabled and through the centralized identity system, thereby preventing general interactive use of system and application accounts.

The platform's audit event framework records each user action, including logins, logouts, configuration changes, and capability checks, with VCF Operations generating user activity audit reports that enable every action to be attributable to an individual user.

While VCF provides the technical foundation for individual identity confirmation, action attribution, and preventing general interactive use, it does not natively enforce time-based limits for exceptional interactive use, provide mechanisms for documenting business justifications, or manage explicit management approval workflows. Organizations must establish and enforce these policy-driven processes and utilize external identity governance tools to fully meet the control's requirements.

Not Assessed | The capabilities of VMware Private AI Foundation are not assessed against this control.

Not Applicable | VMware Live Recovery's capabilities are focused on role-based access control and least privilege for disaster recovery operations. The platform does not provide mechanisms to prevent or manage exceptional interactive login to system or application accounts, nor does it confirm individual identity or attribute actions for such specific scenarios.

Not Applicable | VMware vDefend primarily provides identity-aware network enforcement and role-based access control for its own management plane. It explicitly states that organization-wide account management for individual, group, service, guest, and temporary accounts across technology assets, as well as restricting OS-level or third-party utility programs, falls outside its scope. Therefore, vDefend does not address the requirements for managing interactive login to general system or application accounts.

Description:
8.6.2 Passwords/passphrases for any application and system accounts that can be used for interactive login are not hard coded in scripts, configuration/property files, or bespoke and custom source code. Note: stored passwords/ passphrases are required to be encrypted in accordance with PCI DSS Requirement 8.3.2.
 Applicability Notes
 Stored passwords/passphrases are required to be encrypted in accordance with PCI DSS Requirement 8.3.2.
 This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation utilizes certificate-based authentication for its internal service-to-service communications, eliminating the need for embedded passwords. VCF Single Sign-On provides centralized token-based authentication, ensuring individual platform components do not store static credentials. The platform's API SDKs and automation tools natively support SAML token authentication and session-based authentication, which are the recommended stronger methods for production environments. PowerCLI further enhances security by enforcing PKCE on authorization clients, preventing credential exposure during authorization code flows. While basic authentication remains available for specific interactions like content library publishing and certain API calls, it can be configured to leverage these more secure alternative methods.

The platform does not perform scans of customer-developed applications, scripts, or virtual machine workloads for embedded credentials. Organizations are therefore responsible for establishing and enforcing secure coding practices within their own custom code and automation to ensure that passwords and passphrases for interactive login accounts are not hardcoded.

Description:
8.6.3 Passwords/passphrases for any application and system accounts are protected against misuse as follows: 
 • Passwords/passphrases are changed periodically (at the frequency defined in the entity’s targeted risk analysis, which is performed according to all elements specified in Requirement 12.3.1) and upon suspicion or confirmation of compromise.
 • Passwords/passphrases are constructed with sufficient complexity appropriate for how frequently the entity changes the passwords/passphrases. 
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) enforces robust password complexity, length, and lifespan requirements across all platform components. VCF SSO serves as the centralized authentication service, supporting federation with enterprise identity providers to enable multifactor authentication and token-based credentials. For local accounts, components like NSX Manager and VCF Operations enforce minimum password lengths (e.g., 12 or 15 characters), require a mix of uppercase, lowercase, numeric, and special characters, and implement account lockout policies after repeated failed login attempts. NSX Edge further restricts dictionary words, palindromes, and monotonic sequences. Password expiration is configurable, with defaults typically set to 90 days, and SDDC Manager provides centralized password lifecycle management, allowing administrators to rotate passwords on demand or schedule automatic rotation.

Service-to-service authentication within VCF primarily utilizes certificate-based credentials and API access tokens that rotate after each use, with SDDC Manager managing certificate lifecycles across components.
