VMware vDefend also enforces strong password policies for its management interfaces. The Security Services Platform (SSP), vDefend's centralized console, requires local user passwords to be a minimum of 12 characters, including a mix of character types, and sets validity periods (e.g., 180 days for admin/audit users, 60 days for installer users). NSX Manager and NSX Edge, which manage vDefend firewall policies, enforce similar complexity rules, minimum lengths, and configurable expiration periods, leveraging Linux PAM modules. SSP supports integration with directory services like Active Directory over LDAPS, allowing organizations to extend their enterprise-wide password policies to vDefend administrative access.

While VCF and vDefend provide comprehensive technical controls for password strength, configurable expiration, and rotation mechanisms, the determination of the specific password change frequency based on a targeted risk analysis and the procedural response to suspicion or confirmation of compromise are organizational policy and process responsibilities that extend beyond the platform's inherent capabilities.

SCF ID: DCH-06, DCH-06.1, PES-01, PES-02, PES-02.1, PES-03
Description: 9.1 Processes and mechanisms for performing activities in Requirement 9 are defined and understood.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust technical mechanisms to protect data on storage media, both in use and at rest. vSAN offers data-at-rest encryption for the vSAN datastore, ensuring data protection even if physical devices are removed from the cluster.

This is complemented by vSphere Virtual Machine Encryption, which adds an additional layer of data-at-rest protection for virtual machine data and disks. vSAN storage policies can enforce these encryption requirements.

Furthermore, VCF includes capabilities such as an "Erase disks before use" option during vSAN encryption to wipe existing data, and device cleanup to remove residual data from used devices before they are claimed. VCF best practices also recommend securely deleting decrypted files following restore operations for SDDC Manager, vCenter, and NSX.

While VMware Cloud Foundation delivers critical encryption and data sanitization mechanisms, the organization is responsible for defining and implementing the overarching processes and procedures for media protection. This includes establishing physical control of media, securing storage facilities, and defining media sanitization procedures using approved equipment and techniques. The organization must also ensure these processes and mechanisms are clearly defined, documented, and understood by relevant personnel to meet the control's intent.

Description:
9.1.1 All security policies and operational procedures that are identified in Requirement 9 are:
 • Documented. 
 • Kept up to date. 
 • In use.
 • Known to all affected parties.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 9.1.2 Roles and responsibilities for performing activities in Requirement 9 are documented, assigned, and understood.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 9.2 Physical access controls manage entry into the cardholder data environment.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
9.2.1 Appropriate facility entry controls are in place to restrict physical access to systems in the CDE.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement does not apply to locations that are publicly accessible by consumers (cardholders).
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
9.2.1.1 Individual physical access to sensitive areas within the CDE is monitored with either video cameras or physical access control mechanisms (or both) as follows:
 • Entry and exit points to/from sensitive areas within the CDE are monitored. 
 • Monitoring devices or mechanisms are protected from tampering or disabling.
 • Collected data is reviewed and correlated with other entries.
 • Collected data is stored for at least three months, unless otherwise restricted by law.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 9.2.2 Physical and/or logical controls are implemented to restrict use of publicly accessible network jacks within the facility.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation implements robust logical controls that significantly mitigate the risks associated with publicly accessible network jacks. Data traversing internal VCF networks is encrypted in transit. vSAN data-in-transit encryption utilizes AES-256 to secure all data and metadata traffic between hosts, including witness hosts, file services, and inter-host connections, enforcing forward secrecy to prevent decryption even if keys are compromised. Encrypted vSphere vMotion protects virtual machine data during live migration, with configurable modes to enforce encryption policies.

All management-plane communication is secured using TLS, with VCF Operations providing unified TLS certificate management across all components. VCF Operations for Networks supports TLS 1.2 and 1.3, leveraging FIPS-validated cryptographic modules for both internal and external connections. Furthermore, VCF supports network traffic separation through distributed switch models, offering architectural options like Storage Separation and Workload Separation to provide physical air-gapped isolation of different traffic types onto separate physical network paths. These capabilities ensure that even if physical cabling is tapped or compromised, the encrypted and segmented traffic remains unreadable and unusable by an attacker.

While VCF provides comprehensive logical protections, the physical security of network cabling infrastructure, including conduits, locked pathways, and controlled access to wiring closets and publicly accessible network jacks, remains an organizational and facility responsibility. The customer is accountable for implementing physical access controls and establishing policies and procedures to restrict the use of such jacks.

Description: 9.2.3 Physical access to wireless access points, gateways, networking/communications hardware, and telecommunication lines within the facility is restricted.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation encrypts data in transit across its internal networks. vSAN data-in-transit encryption utilizes AES-256 to secure all data and metadata traffic between hosts, including file service traffic and inter-host connections, enforcing forward secrecy. Encrypted vSphere vMotion protects virtual machine data during live migration with configurable encryption policies. All management-plane communication within VCF uses TLS, with a unified certificate management system for centralized control and support for FIPS-validated cryptographic modules. VCF also supports network traffic separation through distributed switch models, such as Storage Separation and Workload Separation, which can provide physical air-gapped separation of network traffic, isolating different traffic types onto separate physical network paths. These capabilities ensure that even if physical cabling is tapped or compromised, the encrypted and segmented traffic is not readable or usable by an attacker.

While VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust technical controls to protect data in transit, the direct restriction of physical access to wireless access points, gateways, networking/communications hardware, and telecommunication lines within a facility remains an organizational and facility responsibility. This includes implementing physical security measures such as conduits, locked pathways, and controlled access to wiring closets.

Description: 9.2.4 Access to consoles in sensitive areas is restricted via locking when not in use.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 9.3 Physical access to the cardholder data environment for personnel and visitors is authorized and managed.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
9.3.1 Procedures are implemented for authorizing and managing physical access of personnel to the CDE, including:
 •  Identifying personnel.
 • Managing changes to an individual’s physical access requirements.
 •  Revoking or terminating personnel identification.
 •  Limiting access to the identification process or system to authorized personnel.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
9.3.1.1 Physical access to sensitive areas within the CDE for personnel is controlled as follows:
 • Access is authorized and based on individual job function.
 • Access is revoked immediately upon termination.
 • All physical access mechanisms, such as keys, access cards, etc., are returned or disabled upon termination.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
9.3.2 Procedures are implemented for authorizing and managing visitor access to the CDE, including:
 • Visitors are authorized before entering.
 • Visitors are escorted at all times.
 • Visitors are clearly identified and given a badge or other identification that expires.
 • Visitor badges or other identification visibly distinguishes visitors from personnel.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 9.3.3 Visitor badges or identification are surrendered or deactivated before visitors leave the facility or at the date of expiration.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
9.3.4 Visitor logs are used to maintain a physical record of visitor activity both within the facility and within sensitive areas, including: 
 • The visitor’s name and the organization represented.
 • The date and time of the visit.
 • The name of the personnel authorizing physical access. 
 • Retaining the log for at least three months, unless otherwise restricted by law.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 9.4 Media with cardholder data is securely stored, accessed, distributed, and destroyed.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive technical capabilities to ensure media with cardholder data is securely stored, accessed, distributed, and destroyed.

For secure storage, VCF offers multiple layers of data-at-rest encryption, including vSAN cluster-based encryption and VM Encryption, which protect data across all datastore types. Encryption keys are managed through KMIP 1.1-compatible external Key Management Servers or the vSphere Native Key Provider, utilizing a three-tier key hierarchy with automatic rotation and FIPS 140-3 validated cryptographic modules. VCF also supports guest-level encryption via vTPM 2.0 and encrypts ESX host configuration data. Access to encryption configuration is controlled through role-based separation, and vDefend's Distributed Firewall enforces microsegmentation to restrict network access to data stores, limiting lateral movement and ensuring only authorized services can reach data repositories.

For secure distribution, VCF protects data in transit through TLS for inter-component communication, encrypted vSphere vMotion for VM migrations, and vSAN data-in-transit encryption for storage replication. VMware Live Recovery further enhances this by supporting encryption of vSphere Replication traffic between sites and enforcing encryption requirements for replicated virtual machines.

For secure destruction, vSAN includes a secure disk wipe feature aligned with NIST 800-88 standards, allowing for the sanitization of storage devices. Cryptographic erasure is also supported by destroying or rotating encryption keys, rendering encrypted data unrecoverable.

While VCF provides robust technical controls for data on media, the organization remains responsible for defining and implementing physical security measures, secure storage facilities, formal media handling procedures, chain-of-custody tracking, approved transport methods, and comprehensive media disposal processes, including third-party vendor management.

Description: 9.4.1 All media with cardholder data is physically secured.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust cryptographic controls to protect data residing on storage media, rendering it unreadable even if a device is physically removed from the cluster. vSAN encryption secures data at rest on storage devices, and vSphere Virtual Machine Encryption offers per-VM granularity for encrypting virtual machine files independently of the underlying storage. Both encryption mechanisms leverage FIPS 140-3 validated cryptographic modules and integrate with external Key Management Systems (KMS) for comprehensive key lifecycle management.

VCF also includes capabilities for media sanitization, such as the "Erase disks before use" option during vSAN encryption to wipe existing data, and device cleanup to remove residual data from used devices. Best practices further recommend securely deleting decrypted files after restore operations for core VCF components.

VMware Live Site Recovery encrypts replication traffic, protecting data in transit between sites.

VMware vDefend enhances overall data protection by enforcing microsegmentation policies at the vNIC level, restricting unauthorized network access to systems storing or processing data, and identifying unencrypted data flows, thereby protecting the network paths through which data is accessed and stored.

While VCF's encryption and sanitization capabilities significantly mitigate the risk associated with physical media exposure by making data unreadable, the platform does not directly provide physical security measures. The organization remains responsible for implementing physical controls over media, establishing secure storage facilities, and defining comprehensive media handling processes and sanitization procedures using approved equipment and techniques. These organizational policies and physical safeguards are essential to fully meet the requirements for physically securing all media containing cardholder data.

Description: 9.4.1.1 Offline media backups with cardholder data are stored in a secure location.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive backup, snapshot, and replication capabilities for its management components and workload layers. SDDC Manager, NSX Manager, and vCenter support file-based and image-based backups, with options for scheduled and on-demand operations.

Changed Block Tracking (CBT) and vStorage APIs for Data Protection (VADP) enable integration with third-party backup solutions for incremental backups. vSAN Data Protection allows snapshots to be replicated and stored externally to the datastore, while vSAN stretched clusters inherently distribute data across physically separated sites.

VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) supports backup and restore for cluster management workloads, including CSI volume snapshots, with configurable target backup locations such as AWS S3 or S3-compatible storage, ensuring data can reside on separate storage platforms. Configuration backups for components like vSphere Distributed Switches can be exported and copied to secure, separate locations.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) extends these capabilities by providing disaster recovery through continuous host-based replication and point-in-time snapshots to a physically separate recovery site. This fundamental design ensures replicated copies of protected workloads are stored remotely. VLR supports array-based replication and Virtual Volumes policy-driven replication to target storage arrays at the recovery site, with placeholder datastores residing on independent storage. Bidirectional protection configurations further ensure critical information is stored at a separate facility.

VMware vDefend's Security Services Platform (SSP) offers backup and restore for its control plane, including firewall rules, microsegmentation policies, and security data. These backups are encrypted with a passphrase and transmitted to a configurable SFTP server. The backup server can be provisioned on infrastructure separate from the SSP deployment, allowing organizations to direct backups to storage infrastructure located in a separate facility or on a physically distinct storage system.

While the platforms provide robust technical mechanisms for creating backups and storing them in separate, often geographically distinct, locations, the ultimate responsibility for ensuring the physical security of these backup locations, whether they contain physical media or logical storage, rests with the customer. This includes implementing appropriate environmental controls, physical access controls, and logical access controls for the backup infrastructure. The platforms enable the separation of backup data but do not enforce the physical security measures of the chosen backup facility or the handling of physical offline media. Furthermore, the identification and classification of cardholder data within workloads, and ensuring that all relevant cardholder data is included in the backup scope and handled in accordance with PCI DSS requirements, remains a customer responsibility. vDefend's contribution is limited to its own configuration and policy data, not general workload data.

Description: 9.4.1.2 The security of the offline media backup location(s) with cardholder data is reviewed at least once every 12 months.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust capabilities for securing data on storage media through encryption and configuration management, which are foundational to protecting backup data. VCF supports data-at-rest encryption on vSAN datastores and for virtual machine disks, ensuring data protection even if storage devices are physically removed. Storage policies can enforce encryption requirements, and device cleanup options are available to securely erase residual data.

VCF also offers tools for periodic security reviews of online storage configurations, including configuration drift detection for vCenter and cluster objects, storage policy compliance monitoring for vSAN, and operational dashboards that surface capacity and health information for datastores.

VMware Live Recovery extends these capabilities by providing storage visibility and compliance mechanisms for disaster recovery data, including programmatic validation of datastores against storage policies and tagging of DR copies. These capabilities ensure the integrity and security of the digital data and the online storage infrastructure where backups and replicas reside.

However, VCF does not manage physical offline media or classify storage locations as containing sensitive or regulated data, such as cardholder data.

The organizational responsibilities for this control include identifying which specific storage locations contain cardholder data, defining the scope and schedule for security reviews of physical offline media backup locations, and implementing physical security measures for these facilities.

Furthermore, the processes for secure media handling, sanitization, and the actual execution of the annual security review, including documentation and remediation, fall outside the platform's scope and must be established and maintained by the customer.

Description: 9.4.2 All media with cardholder data is classified in accordance with the sensitivity of the data.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
9.4.3 Media with cardholder data sent outside the facility is secured as follows:
 • Media sent outside the facility is logged.
 • Media is sent by secured courier or other delivery method that can be accurately tracked.
 • Offsite tracking logs include details about media location.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust encryption capabilities that protect cardholder data on storage media and during network transport, significantly mitigating risks associated with media leaving controlled areas.

vSAN data-at-rest encryption secures data on physical storage devices, rendering it unreadable if a disk is physically removed from a cluster without the encryption keys. VM Encryption extends this protection across all datastore types by encrypting virtual disk data prior to storage.

Encrypted vSphere vMotion ensures the confidentiality, integrity, and authenticity of virtual machine data during migrations between hosts or sites, always encrypting for encrypted VMs and offering opportunistic or required encryption for unencrypted VMs. vSAN data-in-transit encryption protects storage replication traffic across the network, with data encrypted at the VM host before network transmission in vSAN Express Storage Architecture.

Furthermore, vSAN includes secure disk wipe capabilities, aligned with NIST 800-88 standards, to sanitize media safely before it leaves organizational control. VMware Live Recovery complements these protections by encrypting replication traffic and isolating network traffic during cross-site data transport, safeguarding workload data as it traverses WAN links outside a single controlled area.

While these platform capabilities secure the data itself, the organization remains responsible for establishing and enforcing physical media handling procedures, maintaining chain-of-custody tracking, selecting and utilizing approved secured transport methods, and ensuring offsite tracking logs accurately detail media location. These administrative and physical security controls are outside the scope of the platform's technical capabilities.

Description:
9.4.4 Management approves all media with cardholder data that is moved outside the facility (including when media is distributed to individuals).
 Applicability Notes
 Individuals approving media movements should have the appropriate level of management authority to grant this approval. However, it is not specifically required that such individuals have “manager” as part of their title.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 9.4.5 Inventory logs of all electronic media with cardholder data are maintained.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 9.4.5.1 Inventories of electronic media with cardholder data are conducted at least once every 12 months.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
9.4.6 Hard-copy materials with cardholder data are destroyed when no longer needed for business or legal reasons, as follows:
 • Materials are cross-cut shredded, incinerated, or pulped so that cardholder data cannot be reconstructed. 
 • Materials are stored in secure storage containers prior to destruction. 
 Applicability Notes
 These requirements for media destruction when that media is no longer needed for business or legal reasons are separate and distinct from PCI DSS Requirement 3.2.1, which is for securely deleting cardholder data when no longer needed per the entity’s cardholder data retention policies.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
9.4.7 Electronic media with cardholder data is destroyed when no longer needed for business or legal reasons via one of the following: 
 • The electronic media is destroyed.
 • The cardholder data is rendered unrecoverable so that it cannot be reconstructed. 
 Applicability Notes
 These requirements for media destruction when that media is no longer needed for business or legal reasons are separate and distinct from PCI DSS Requirement 3.2.1, which is for securely deleting cardholder data when no longer needed per the entity’s cardholder data retention policies.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust technical mechanisms for rendering cardholder data unrecoverable on electronic media.

vSAN data-at-rest encryption utilizes a two-tier key architecture, enabling cryptographic erasure where the destruction of Key Encryption Keys (KEK) through an external Key Management Server (KMS) or vSphere Native Key Provider renders encrypted data permanently unrecoverable without requiring a full disk wipe. vSAN also offers direct data wiping capabilities, including an "Erase disks before use" option during encryption configuration and a "Wipe residual data" feature for securely sanitizing storage devices. Devices added to vSAN disk groups are automatically cleaned of residual data and partition information.

Furthermore, vSphere provides API methods such as `removeKey` and `removeKeys` for programmatic cryptographic key destruction, and ESX stores its configuration data encrypted, allowing for TPM clearing during host decommissioning to render configuration unrecoverable.

VCF also enforces secure deletion practices for its own operational data, directing administrators to securely delete decrypted backup files after restore operations for SDDC Manager, vCenter, and NSX components.

Audit logs from vSAN, vCenter, and VCF Operations provide traceability of these sanitization and key destruction events, which can be aggregated and forwarded to external syslog collectors for long-term retention and analysis.

While VMware Cloud Foundation provides the technical means for data sanitization and cryptographic erasure, the organization is responsible for establishing and enforcing comprehensive policies and procedures for media disposal.

This includes defining when electronic media containing cardholder data is no longer needed for business or legal reasons, implementing physical destruction processes for media, and maintaining a formal chain-of-custody.

VCF does not include native tools for inventory tracking, media sanitization workflow management, or the generation of formal disposal documentation.

These organizational processes, along with the verification of sanitization actions and the secure handling of physical media, must be managed through enterprise asset management and data governance programs.

Description: 9.5 Point-of-interaction (POI) devices are protected from tampering and unauthorized substitution.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

SCF ID: AST-01, AST-02, AST-06, AST-07, AST-15, AST-15.1, HRS-04.2, SAT-01, SAT-02, SAT-03, SAT-03.3
Description:
9.5.1 POI devices that capture payment card data via direct physical interaction with the payment card form factor are protected from tampering and unauthorized substitution, including the following:
 • Maintaining a list of POI devices. 
 • Periodically inspecting POI devices to look for tampering or unauthorized substitution. 
 • Training personnel to be aware of suspicious behavior and to report tampering or unauthorized substitution of devices.
 Applicability Notes
 These requirements apply to deployed POI devices used in card-present transactions (that is, a payment card form factor such as a card that is swiped, tapped, or dipped). These requirements do not apply to:
 • Components used only for manual PAN key entry.
 • Commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) devices (for example, smartphones or tablets), which are mobile merchant-owned devices designed for mass-market distribution.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
9.5.1.1 An up-to-date list of POI devices is maintained, including:
 • Make and model of the device. 
 • Location of device. 
 • Device serial number or other methods of unique identification.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides a robust, real-time inventory of technology assets across compute, storage, and networking layers, serving as a foundational component for maintaining an up-to-date list of devices. vCenter maintains the authoritative inventory of all managed objects, including ESX hosts, clusters, virtual machines, datastores, and networking constructs, tracking their make, model, location within the virtual infrastructure, and unique identifiers such as VM UUIDs and host serial numbers.

VCF Operations retrieves these inventory details, logically groups discovered objects, and provides detailed lists. The Service Discovery adapter automatically discovers applications and services running on managed infrastructure, along with their associated metrics, and allows for export of this data.

VCF Automation tracks provisioned resources through its project model, linking users, catalog items, and IaaS resources, with inventory views scoped by rights and project roles.

For integration with external asset registries, VCF provides mechanisms to populate and reconcile Configuration Management Databases (CMDBs) like ServiceNow with discovered resources. vSphere Lifecycle Manager further contributes by maintaining a software depot with metadata about installed components and available updates, providing a software inventory that tracks versions across the environment.

VMware vDefend Security Intelligence and Security Services Platform (SSP) maintain an inventory of network-visible workloads, organized into asset collections spanning groups, infrastructure assets, environments, applications, and application tiers. SSP supports the import of inventory asset CSV files and allows filtering of assets by identifier for reporting and alerting purposes, providing granular tracking of application-layer assets that may function as Point of Interaction (POI) devices.

While VCF and vDefend provide comprehensive technical capabilities for asset inventory and tracking within their respective scopes, the broader IT Asset Management program responsibilities remain with the customer. This includes establishing enterprise-wide asset governance policies, defining ownership lifecycles, maintaining organizational accountability for all POI devices, and implementing enterprise asset classification and business justification tracking processes that extend beyond the VCF-managed infrastructure and network-visible workloads.

Description: 9.5.1.2 POI device surfaces are periodically inspected to detect tampering and unauthorized substitution.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust capabilities for detecting logical tampering and unauthorized modifications within the managed infrastructure. VCF Operations continuously monitors infrastructure against security baselines and custom compliance policies, identifying non-compliant objects and surfacing associated violations. Configuration drift detection compares current settings against desired-state templates, notifying administrators of any deviations that could indicate unauthorized modification. Audit event reporting records configuration changes, user actions, and authentication events across the managed infrastructure, providing detailed visibility into activity. At the hardware level, TPM event logs can be retrieved through the vCenter Trusted Infrastructure API to validate boot-time measurements, and vCenter monitors host attestation state to verify hosts have booted into known-good configurations, thereby contributing to the integrity verification of the underlying physical hosts.

VMware vDefend addresses the logical, network-layer dimension of compromise detection by continuously inspecting traffic through IDS/IPS signature matching and recording event details. Security Intelligence Suspicious Traffic events capture MITRE ATT&CK tactics and techniques associated with detected anomalous behavior, enabling analysts to identify network-level indicators of compromise across workloads. This provides a mechanism to detect anomalous behavior that could originate from a tampered or substituted device interacting with the network.

While VCF and vDefend provide extensive logical integrity checks, monitoring, and anomaly detection for the underlying infrastructure and network, the physical inspection of Point-of-Interaction (POI) device surfaces to detect physical tampering or unauthorized substitution falls outside the scope of these platforms. Customers are responsible for establishing and executing physical security programs, including periodic physical inspections of POI devices and other technology assets, to address the physical dimension of this control.

Description:
9.5.1.2.1 The frequency of periodic POI device inspections and the type of inspections performed is defined in the entity’s targeted risk analysis, which is performed according to all elements specified in Requirement 12.3.1.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) Operations provides continuous monitoring, logging, and anomaly detection capabilities, generating alerts for metric deviations and potential evidence. It records audit events for configuration changes, user actions, and authentication across the managed infrastructure, supporting detailed activity views. VCF continuously monitors infrastructure against security baselines, detecting compliance violations. ESX hosts generate detailed audit logs, and multiple VCF components support syslog forwarding to external SIEM systems.

VCF Operations for Networks monitors network flows and detects anomalous traffic patterns. Configuration drift detection identifies deviations from desired-state templates.

VMware vDefend enhances this by continuously inspecting network traffic through IDS/IPS signature matching, capturing MITRE ATT&CK tactics and techniques for detected anomalous behavior, thereby identifying network-level indicators of compromise across workloads. These capabilities provide the telemetry and change-detection foundation for detecting logical indicators of compromise within the virtualized environment.

The definition of the frequency and type of POI device inspections, including the underlying targeted risk analysis, is a customer responsibility. While VCF and vDefend provide robust logical and network-level inspection capabilities, the physical inspection of hardware and system components for signs of physical tampering on POI devices falls outside the scope of these platforms and must be addressed through customer-implemented physical security and hardware integrity controls.

Description:
9.5.1.3 Training is provided for personnel in POI environments to be aware of attempted tampering or replacement of POI devices, and includes:
 • Verifying the identity of any third-party persons claiming to be repair or maintenance personnel, before granting them access to modify or troubleshoot devices.
 • Procedures to ensure devices are not installed, replaced, or returned without verification. 
 • Being aware of suspicious behavior around devices.
 • Reporting suspicious behavior and indications of device tampering or substitution to appropriate personnel.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 10.1 Processes and mechanisms for performing activities in Requirement 10 are defined and understood.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive, enterprise-wide monitoring capabilities through integrated tools that span infrastructure health, performance metrics, log analysis, and alerting.

VCF Operations continuously monitors the operational state of all VCF components, collecting metrics across compute, storage, and networking resources, correlating them to identify trends, and enabling administrators to define alert policies based on performance metrics, log events, or custom conditions. VCF Operations for Logs centralizes log collection and analysis from vCenter, ESX hosts, and other VCF components, offering dashboards, analytics, and log-based alert definitions, with configurable TLS-encrypted log forwarding to external SIEM or syslog targets. The vSphere alarm infrastructure integrates with events and performance counters for real-time alerting, allowing administrators to set alarms on specific performance metrics. VCF Health continuously monitors component health, providing a unified interface for alerts, log analysis, and diagnostics. When integrated with VCF Automation, the Insights dashboard and Alerts tab offer real-time capacity awareness. VCF Operations also supports self-monitoring and can extend monitoring to additional systems via adapters.

VMware Live Recovery provides monitoring for disaster recovery operations, integrating with the broader VCF ecosystem through a management pack that feeds VLR health and replication status data into VCF Operations. Within its domain, VLR offers event logging and configurable alarms for replication events, including RPO violation tracking, replication error state alerting, and storage capacity alarms. It continuously monitors replication traffic connectivity, alerting on loss and restoration, and provides a consolidated view of VM replication and connectivity states.

VMware vDefend supports enterprise-wide network security monitoring via Security Intelligence, maintaining continuous visibility into network traffic flows, detecting new, unapproved, or blocked flows, and alerting administrators to unauthorized or unexpected traffic. The NDR Sensor periodically checks detection feature status to ensure sustained threat detection coverage.

Both VCF and vDefend provide capabilities to support prioritized monitoring; VCF Operations allows assigning Business Criticality levels to applications, defining tag-based policies, and configuring custom alarm expressions with impact levels (Health, Risk, Efficiency) to differentiate monitoring intensity and response based on asset classification. vDefend Security Services Platform and Security Intelligence support prioritized traffic collection and asset filtering to focus monitoring efforts.

While the platform provides robust technical mechanisms and instrumentation, the organizational assessment of monitoring needs, the definition of what to monitor, the classification of asset criticality and data sensitivity, and the formal prioritization of monitoring activities are organizational functions. The customer is responsible for defining the overarching processes, policies, and governance-level decisions for monitoring, and for ensuring these processes and mechanisms are understood across the organization.

Description:
10.1.1 All security policies and operational procedures that are identified in Requirement 10 are: 
 • Documented.
 • Kept up to date.
 • In use.
 • Known to all affected parties.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 10.1.2 Roles and responsibilities for performing activities in Requirement 10 are documented, assigned, and understood.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 10.2 Audit logs are implemented to support the detection of anomalies and suspicious activity, and the forensic analysis of events.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF), VMware Live Recovery (VLR), and VMware vDefend collectively implement comprehensive audit logging across the infrastructure stack. VCF components generate detailed event logs for access, configuration, network, and system operations, with VCF Operations for Logs providing centralized search, correlation, and log-based alert definitions.

VLR produces structured event logs for disaster recovery activities, including protection group events, replication status, and permission changes. VMware vDefend captures granular event logs from its Distributed Firewall, IDS/IPS, Malware Prevention, and Network Detection and Response components, detailing traffic actions, threat detections, file analysis verdicts, and network anomalies.

All logs include essential information elements such as event type, precise timestamps, originating source, operation outcome, and the identity of the user or service account involved. VCF Operations provides an integrated alert framework with intelligent alert correlation and outbound notification capabilities, while VLR extends vCenter alarms for DR-specific events, and vDefend actively generates alerts for detected network threats.

Consistent time synchronization across all VCF, VLR, and vDefend components is enforced and monitored, ensuring the integrity and correlatability of timestamps, which is fundamental for accurate forensic analysis and supporting the detection of anomalies and suspicious activity.

Organizations must define specific alert thresholds, configure custom log-based alerts, and integrate these platform-generated alerts and logs with their broader security information and event management (SIEM) systems and incident response workflows.

Establishing and maintaining organizational policies and procedures for log review, alert response, and forensic investigation processes, along with implementing adequate log retention policies, remains an organizational responsibility.

Description: 10.2.1 Audit logs are enabled and active for all system components and cardholder data.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation, VMware Live Recovery, and VMware vDefend collectively provide robust capabilities for enabling and activating audit logs across all system components within the VCF infrastructure.

VCF components, including VCF Operations, VCF Automation, vSphere, and NSX, generate detailed event logs that capture event types, timestamps, sources, outcomes, and user identities for various operations, configuration changes, and access events. VCF Operations audit events are categorized by type (e.g., Access, Configuration, Account_management) and include user ID, user name, and client IP for traceability. VCF Automation logs reportable actions with timestamps, user names, and project details.

VMware Live Recovery produces structured event logs for protection groups, permissions, and replication activities, including event types, timestamps, object associations, and user identities for permission changes.

VMware vDefend components, such as the Distributed Firewall, IDS/IPS, Malware Prevention Service, and Network Detection and Response, generate event logs with specific details like rule actions, signature matches, file analysis verdicts, and network threat detections, all including timestamps, event sources, and outcomes.

User identity is captured through mechanisms like Identity Firewall and NSX Manager audit logs for configuration changes. These platforms ensure that audit logs are enabled by default and actively record critical system activities, with capabilities for centralized collection and SIEM integration.

While the platform provides comprehensive audit logging for its underlying infrastructure components and user actions within that infrastructure, the responsibility for identifying specific cardholder data, configuring application-level logging for access to that data, and ensuring those application logs are actively collected and integrated into the overall logging solution rests with the customer. The platform provides the foundational logging mechanisms and infrastructure where cardholder data may reside or be processed, but the customer must define and implement policies and processes to ensure that all activities directly involving cardholder data at the application layer are also adequately logged and monitored.

Description: 10.2.1.1 Audit logs capture all individual user access to cardholder data.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive audit logging capabilities across its components, capturing individual user and service account actions. VCF Operations registers audit events for interactions including logins, logouts, capability checks, and configuration modifications, with categories such as "Data_access" and "Access_control." These events are attributed to specific users and can be filtered by User ID, User Name, and Auth Source, providing clear traceability. VCF Automation creates audit logs for reportable actions, distinguishing between user and service accounts. At the vSphere layer, authenticated user actions and privilege checks are recorded, and NSX audit logging captures network configuration changes with user identity. VCF Operations for Logs centralizes these event logs, offering a unified interface for searching, filtering, and correlating activity across the platform, and supports forwarding logs to external syslog servers for long-term retention and analysis. VMware Live Recovery (VLR) logs disaster recovery operations, permission changes, and API access, attributing these actions to individual users or service accounts. VMware vDefend's Security Services Platform logs all access events by default, and its Identity Firewall component can associate network traffic with Active Directory users and groups, enabling user-attributed network policy enforcement.

While the platform provides robust logging of user activity, administrative actions, and general data access events at the infrastructure level, the explicit identification and logging of *all individual user access to cardholder data* residing within guest operating systems or applications is a customer responsibility. Customers must configure their applications, databases, and guest operating systems to specifically log access to cardholder data, and then integrate these application-level logs with the VCF Operations for Logs platform for centralized collection and analysis.

Description: 10.2.1.2 Audit logs capture all actions taken by any individual with administrative access, including any interactive use of application or system accounts.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) and its integrated components, including VMware Live Recovery (VLR) and VMware vDefend, provide comprehensive, identity-aware audit logging for all administrative and privileged actions.

VCF Operations registers every platform interaction—from searches and logins to configuration modifications and privilege checks—as an audit event, meticulously capturing user ID, name, authentication source, session details, and client IP. VCF Automation similarly logs reportable actions with timestamps, user names, and trace IDs, distinguishing between user and service accounts.

At the vSphere layer, the AuthorizationManager API and privilege checks recorder track object access and invoked operations, while NSX audit logging captures network configuration changes with user attribution.

VLR records all disaster recovery operations, such as protection group modifications and recovery plan executions, directly linking them to the initiating user or service account, including detailed permission change events and authorization logging between VLR Server and vCenter.

VMware vDefend's Security Services Platform enforces mandatory, non-disableable audit logging for all access events, capturing privileged management actions and providing a dedicated Auditor role for review.

Across the platform, logs include timestamps, event types, sources (e.g., vNIC, VM, security policy), and outcomes (e.g., allow, drop, reject, success, failure), ensuring a detailed record of administrative activity and interactive use. VCF Operations for Logs centralizes these diverse log sources, offering advanced analytics and filtering, and supports secure syslog forwarding to external SIEMs for consolidated analysis and long-term retention.

While the platform provides the robust technical mechanisms for capturing these audit logs, customers are responsible for defining their specific administrative roles and access policies, configuring appropriate logging verbosity where not default, and establishing comprehensive processes for the regular review, analysis, and retention of these audit records. This includes developing procedures for responding to detected anomalies or suspicious activities identified through log analysis.

Description: 10.2.1.3 Audit logs capture all access to audit logs.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) Operations event auditing registers each action as an audit event, capturing interactions within the platform including searches, logins, logouts, capability checks, and configuration modifications. This ensures that administrative and programmatic access to VCF's audit logs, including viewing, searching, exporting, and forwarding, is recorded as an auditable event.

API audit logging captures REST API calls, SDK usage, and PowerCLI commands with user attribution.

VCF Operations for Networks supports exporting audit log data, and VCF Operations can forward its user activity log to an external syslog server, with these actions themselves being subject to audit.

VMware vDefend's Security Services Platform logs all access events in audit logs by default, which cannot be disabled. This provides a record linking system access to the accounts that performed actions. The dedicated Auditor role in vDefend, designed for reviewing tracked activity, further confirms that such review, which constitutes access to logs, is captured as an access event.

VMware Live Recovery's external API module logs API access at a verbose level, capturing programmatic interactions with VLR.

Authorization logging tracks privilege checks and authorization decisions for cross-component operations.

These capabilities collectively ensure that access to audit logs across the VCF ecosystem is comprehensively captured and attributed.

Description: 10.2.1.4 Audit logs capture all invalid logical access attempts.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) comprehensively captures all invalid logical access attempts within its audit logs. VCF Operations audit events explicitly record both success and failure states for operations across the platform, including user logins and logouts, privilege checks, and configuration modifications across vSphere, vSAN, and NSX resources. Each action is registered as an event with detailed user attribution, including User ID, User Name, Auth Source, Session, and Client IP. API audit logging further captures REST API calls, SDK usage, and PowerCLI commands with user identity. All events include timestamps and source identification, enabling precise correlation and analysis. VCF Operations for Logs centralizes these events, and VCF Operations can forward user activity logs to external syslog servers with TLS encryption for secure, centralized retention and analysis.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) contributes by generating error-level events when operations fail, clearly distinguishing unsuccessful outcomes from successful ones. VLR provides authorization logging between the VLR Server and vCenter, tracking privilege checks and authorization decisions for cross-component operations, and its external API module logs API access. These events include named event types, timestamps, and user identity for permission changes and recovery plan executions.

VMware vDefend's Security Services Platform logs all access events by default, which cannot be disabled, ensuring that privileged management actions performed within the platform are captured in audit logs.

NSX Manager audit logs capture the identity of administrators making configuration changes to firewall rules and security policies, and the Identity Firewall component associates network traffic with Active Directory users and groups. These logs include timestamps, event source, and user identity.

The integrated logging across VCF and its components ensures that all invalid logical access attempts are captured with the necessary contextual information.

Description:
10.2.1.5 Audit logs capture all changes to identification and authentication credentials including, but not limited to:
 • Creation of new accounts.
 • Elevation of privileges.
 • All changes, additions, or deletions to accounts with administrative access.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation, VMware Live Recovery, and VMware vDefend collectively ensure that audit logs capture all required changes to identification and authentication credentials.

VCF Operations registers audit events for account management, access control, and configuration modifications, enabling traceability of user actions, including the creation of new accounts and changes to existing ones. The vCenter Server privilege checks recorder specifically monitors and records privilege elevations and modifications to administrative access, detailing the principal, privilege checked, and session identifier. VCF Automation and NSX Manager audit logs further capture user-attributed changes to platform configurations and network security policies, including those made by service accounts.

All VCF components support syslog forwarding for centralized collection and review of these detailed logs.

VMware Live Recovery provides specific audit events for permission changes, such as the addition, update, or deletion of roles, directly addressing modifications to administrative access and privilege management within the disaster recovery environment. These events record the user identity, the role assigned, and the affected entity.

vDefend's Security Services Platform maintains default, non-disableable audit logs for all access events, linking system access to individual accounts and capturing privileged management actions performed within the network security domain. The platform's comprehensive logging capabilities ensure that all specified changes to identification and authentication credentials, including account creation, privilege elevation, and administrative account modifications, are captured with user attribution and timestamps.

Description:
10.2.1.6 Audit logs capture the following: 
 • All initialization of new audit logs, and 
 • All starting, stopping, or pausing of the existing audit logs.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust audit logging capabilities that capture configuration modifications and administrative actions across its components. VCF Operations event auditing registers each action as an audit event, capturing interactions within the platform, including configuration modifications. The VCF Operations Audit Events framework captures platform interactions, including configuration modifications across vSphere, vSAN, and NSX resources. VCF Operations for Networks captures administrative actions, including CRUD operations. These mechanisms ensure that any administrative action to initialize new audit logs, or to start, stop, or pause existing audit logs, is recorded as a configuration modification or administrative action within the platform's audit logs.

VMware vDefend's Security Services Platform logs all access events by default, meaning privileged management actions performed within the platform are captured in audit logs. This includes any actions taken to manage the lifecycle of vDefend's audit logs, such as their initialization, starting, stopping, or pausing. The platform's inherent logging of configuration and management actions directly addresses the requirement for audit logs to capture their own lifecycle events.

Description: 10.2.1.7 Audit logs capture all creation and deletion of system-level objects.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation, through its integrated components, captures the creation and deletion of system-level objects.

VCF Operations provides an audit events framework that records configuration modifications across vSphere, vSAN, and NSX resources. VCF Automation creates audit logs for actions such as deploying blueprints, performing Day 2 operations, and creating, deleting, or updating property groups.

NSX audit logging captures network configuration changes, including logical switch operations, firewall rules, and security policies, with user attribution.

VMware Live Recovery logs specific disaster recovery operations, such as the creation and removal of protection groups and consistency groups, as well as all permission changes.

VMware vDefend's Security Services Platform logs all access events and configuration changes related to network security policies and firewall rules.

These logs consistently include event types, timestamps, event sources, outcomes, and user identities, providing a comprehensive record of system-level object lifecycle management.

The customer is responsible for configuring appropriate log retention policies and integrating these logs with a centralized security information and event management (SIEM) system for ongoing monitoring and analysis.

Description:
10.2.2 Audit logs record the following details for each auditable event:
 • User identification.
 • Type of event.
 • Date and time.
 • Success and failure indication.
 • Origination of event.
 • Identity or name of affected data, system component, resource, or service (for example, name and protocol).
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation, VMware vDefend, and VMware Live Recovery collectively provide the technical mechanisms to record audit log details for auditable events, including user identification, event type, date and time, origination, and the identity of affected components.

However, VMware Live Recovery does not consistently provide an explicit success or failure indication for all informational event types.

Organizations must establish policies and processes to review the specific event types and their attributes across all components to ensure that the level of detail for success/failure indication meets their specific audit requirements, particularly for informational events within VMware Live Recovery.

Description: 10.3 Audit logs are protected from destruction and unauthorized modifications.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation, VMware Live Recovery, and VMware vDefend collectively provide robust capabilities for generating and protecting audit logs from unauthorized access and destruction.

VMware Cloud Foundation components generate security-relevant audit logs, which are aggregated by VCF Operations for Logs. Access to these logs is strictly governed by role-based access controls, restricting who can view and manage log data. Log forwarding to external syslog servers is supported with TLS encryption, ensuring protection in transit. VCF Operations orchestrator enforces role-based access control, and user permission events are sourced from vCenter logs, providing visibility into access changes.

Similarly, VMware Live Recovery protects event logs through its role-based access controls, with specific roles governing the generation, retrieval, and deletion of log bundles. VLR generates audit log entries for permission changes and supports syslog forwarding to centralized log management platforms.

VMware vDefend's Security Services Platform ensures audit logs are enabled by default and cannot be disabled, preventing suppression or deletion through configuration. It generates structured security event logs from its Distributed Firewall, Gateway Firewall, IDS/IPS, and Malware Prevention components. vDefend further restricts access to audit log functions through role-based access control, including a dedicated Auditor role that provides read-only access, separating log review from administrative functions.

While these platforms provide extensive technical mechanisms for log generation, access control, and secure forwarding, they do not inherently offer immutable or tamper-evident log storage. Protection against log modification or deletion at the storage layer requires integration with an external SIEM, a write-once-read-many (WORM) log repository, or an equivalent tamper-resistant storage solution. Organizations must forward logs to such external systems to fully satisfy the intent of protecting logs from destruction and unauthorized modification at rest.

Furthermore, the organizational responsibility for defining which users have a business need for log access, establishing and enforcing least-privilege policies, and managing access policies for log management functions across the broader environment remains with the customer.

Description: 10.3.1 Read access to audit logs files is limited to those with a job-related need.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust role-based access controls across its management components, including VCF Operations and VCF Operations for Logs, to restrict who can view and manage event logs. Granular object-level access is enforced through role assignments, allowing administrators to limit users to specific navigation areas, dashboards, and configuration sections based on group membership and job function. VCF Operations for Logs manages its own access controls, granting permissions to SSO users and groups to govern viewing, searching, and managing collected log data. VCF Operations orchestrator enforces RBAC, restricting users to specific permissions based on assigned roles.

Similarly, VMware Live Recovery (VLR) protects event logs through its privilege model, with the vSphere Replication VRM diagnostics role specifically controlling access to log bundles and the VRM Administrator role allowing for custom, restricted permissions. VLR audits permission changes, providing visibility into modifications of log access controls.

VMware vDefend's Security Services Platform restricts access to audit log and event log functions via role-based access control, featuring a dedicated Auditor role that provides read-only access to logged activity, thereby separating log review from administrative functions. Role assignments can be scoped to local accounts or directory-managed remote users and groups, aligning log access with specific business needs. All platforms support forwarding logs to centralized collection points, with VCF components supporting TLS encryption for logs in transit.

While the platforms provide the technical mechanisms for restricting and auditing log access, the organizational component of defining which users have a business need for log access and enforcing least-privilege policies requires administrative process and governance outside the scope of VCF, VLR, or vDefend. The organization must define and manage these access policies.

Furthermore, VCF and VLR do not inherently provide immutable or tamper-evident log storage. Protection against log modification or deletion at the storage layer, as well as broader RBAC enforcement for log management functions outside the Security Services Platform, depends on integration with an external SIEM, a write-once-read-many (WORM) log repository, or equivalent tamper-resistant storage.

Description: 10.3.2 Audit log files are protected to prevent modifications by individuals.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation, VMware Live Recovery, and VMware vDefend collectively provide robust technical mechanisms to protect audit log files from unauthorized modification. VCF components, VLR, and vDefend's Security Services Platform generate comprehensive audit logs for security-relevant events, with vDefend explicitly preventing the disabling of these logs to ensure continuous recording.

Log forwarding from VCF components and VLR to centralized syslog servers supports TLS encryption, safeguarding log data in transit.

Access to log management functions across these platforms is rigorously controlled through role-based access controls. VCF Operations for Logs, for instance, governs permissions for viewing and managing collected log data, while VLR's vSphere Replication VRM diagnostics role restricts who can generate, retrieve, and delete log bundles. Similarly, vDefend's Security Services Platform implements an Auditor role, providing read-only access to logged activity, thereby separating log review from administrative functions. VLR further enhances protection by generating audit log entries for all permission changes, creating an immutable record of access control modifications.

Despite these capabilities, the platforms do not inherently provide immutable or tamper-evident log storage.

Protection against log modification or deletion at the storage layer requires integration with an external Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) system, a write-once-read-many (WORM) log repository, or equivalent tamper-resistant storage. Organizations must forward logs to such external systems to fully satisfy the control's intent for long-term, tamper-proof retention.

Furthermore, while the platforms offer the technical mechanisms for restricting log access, the organizational responsibility for defining business needs for log access, establishing least-privilege policies, and managing these access policies remains with the customer.

Description: 10.3.3 Audit log files, including those for external-facing technologies, are promptly backed up to a secure, central, internal log server(s) or other media that is difficult to modify.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive capabilities for the prompt, secure, and centralized backup of audit log files to media that is difficult to modify. VCF Operations for Logs functions as the dedicated, centralized log collection and analysis platform for the entire VCF environment, gathering security-related event logs from vCenter, ESX hosts, NSX, VCF Operations, and VCF Operations for Networks. This platform operates on its own cluster of nodes, ensuring physical separation from the log-generating components, thereby serving as a distinct and secure system for log backup.

All VCF components support native syslog forwarding, including TLS encryption for data in transit, enabling logs to be promptly directed to VCF Operations for Logs or external SIEM systems. VCF Operations captures a wide array of audit events, including user logins, configuration modifications, API calls, and authentication events, registering them as audit records for security and compliance. VMware Live Recovery (VLR) and VMware vDefend components generate extensive security event logs and support forwarding these to VCF Operations for Logs or external SIEMs via syslog, extending the centralized collection to disaster recovery and network security domains.

The VCF platform provides the necessary technical infrastructure for log collection, secure transmission, and centralized storage. While VCF Operations for Logs inherently offers a more secure and controlled environment for logs, customers are responsible for configuring the retention policies within VCF Operations for Logs and ensuring that any external SIEMs or log archives utilized are configured with appropriate access controls, immutability features (such as WORM storage), and backup strategies to fully satisfy the "difficult to modify" requirement for long-term archival.

Description: 10.3.4 File integrity monitoring or change-detection mechanisms is used on audit logs to ensure that existing log data cannot be changed without generating alerts.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust integrity and change-detection mechanisms for its underlying infrastructure components.

VCF Operations Configuration Drifts continuously monitors vCenter and vSphere cluster settings against defined configuration templates, alerting administrators to deviations from the desired state. vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) performs compliance checks of ESX hosts against a specified image, remediating any drift in the hypervisor software stack. For boot-time integrity, ESX hosts support Secure Boot and Trusted Platform Module (TPM) attestation, with vCenter retrieving TPM event logs for validation of firmware and boot components. VCF Operations also maintains an audit trail for configuration files, recording modifications and verifying settings against authorized changes.

These capabilities ensure the integrity of the VCF platform's configuration and operational state.

However, VMware Cloud Foundation does not include a native file integrity monitor (FIM) that watches for unauthorized changes to individual files on disk, specifically audit logs within guest operating systems or arbitrary files on VCF management appliances. To address the requirement for file-level integrity monitoring of audit logs, organizations must deploy a dedicated FIM product. ESX Agent Manager can be utilized to facilitate the deployment and lifecycle management of such third-party security agents within the virtualized environment.

Description: 10.4 Audit logs are reviewed to identify anomalies or suspicious activity.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides a comprehensive platform for centralized log collection, analysis, and anomaly detection across the entire infrastructure stack. VCF Operations for Logs aggregates logs from all VCF components, including vCenter, ESX, NSX, vSAN, and VCF Automation, offering pre-built dashboards, queries, and content packs for immediate visibility into security-relevant events such as authentication failures and configuration changes. The platform continuously monitors compute, storage, and networking resources, correlating metrics and log events over time to identify trends and anomalies, and administrators can define alert policies based on these conditions. Intelligent Alerts group related alerts to reduce noise and assist in root cause analysis. VCF also supports forwarding all audit logs to external SIEM or syslog targets with TLS encryption for centralized security operations. VMware Live Recovery (VLR) extends these capabilities by generating audit events and alarms for disaster recovery operations, including protection group modifications and site connectivity issues, which integrate with the vCenter alarm framework and can be forwarded to centralized monitoring tools. VMware vDefend further enhances security monitoring by generating detailed event logs from Distributed Firewall, Gateway Firewall, and IDS/IPS components. Its Security Intelligence feature automatically analyzes network traffic flows to surface anomalies and policy violations, while Network Detection and Response (NDR) provides real-time analysis of security event data, with direct integration to external SIEM systems.

While the platform provides robust technical tools for log collection, automated anomaly detection, and alerting, the ongoing organizational process of reviewing audit logs, correlating events across all sources, and making definitive judgments to identify suspicious activity, along with defining and executing incident escalation procedures, remains the customer's responsibility. A dedicated Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) solution or equivalent organizational processes are required to fully correlate events from all sources, manage the lifecycle of identified incidents, and ensure timely human review and response to potential threats.

Description:
10.4.1 The following audit logs are reviewed at least once daily:
 • All security events. 
 • Logs of all system components that store, process, or transmit CHD and/or SAD.
 • Logs of all critical system components.
 • Logs of all servers and system components that perform security functions (for example, network security controls, intrusion-detection systems/intrusion-prevention systems (IDS/IPS), authentication servers).
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides a robust, integrated framework for centralized log collection, analysis, and alerting across the entire infrastructure stack. VCF Operations for Logs aggregates security-relevant events from vCenter, ESX hosts, NSX, vSAN, VCF Automation, VCF Identity Broker, and VCF Operations for Networks. This includes comprehensive audit events for platform interactions, user logins/logouts, configuration changes, API calls, Kubernetes API calls, and authentication events from all identity sources. VMware vDefend further enriches this with detailed security event logs from Distributed Firewall, Gateway Firewall, and IDS/IPS components, including network traffic flows, anomalies, and policy violations, with direct integration for forwarding to SIEM systems. VMware Live Recovery (VLR) contributes by generating audit events for disaster recovery operations, protection group modifications, and replication lifecycle changes, which are integrated into the vCenter event framework and forwarded to centralized logging. All components support native syslog forwarding to external SIEM systems, ensuring comprehensive collection of security events, logs from critical system components, and logs from components performing security functions.

VCF Operations for Logs offers pre-built dashboards, alerts, and queries for immediate visibility into security-relevant events, supporting search, filtering, and custom alert definitions based on log patterns. Intelligent Alerts correlate related events across the topology, reducing noise and aiding root cause analysis. vDefend provides domain-specific analysis tools such as Firewall Insights for real-time traffic visualization, Security Intelligence for automated anomaly detection, and Security Services Platform Rule Analysis for policy health reports. VLR integrates with the vCenter alarm framework, generating alarms for DR-specific events like connectivity issues or error states. The VCF platform provides a comprehensive alert generation, correlation, and response framework, supporting outbound notifications via email, SNMP, webhooks, and ServiceNow, enabling timely incident awareness and escalation.

While the VCF platform provides the essential technical capabilities for log collection, analysis, and alerting, the control's requirement for logs to be "reviewed at least once daily" necessitates defined organizational processes and human accountability. Customers are responsible for establishing and enforcing these daily review procedures, defining alert policies and thresholds, and implementing incident response workflows. This includes identifying specific system components that store, process, or transmit CHD/SAD, as well as defining critical system components, and ensuring their logs are actively monitored and reviewed according to organizational policy. The platform facilitates these activities but does not automate the human review and decision-making process.

Description:
10.4.1.1 Automated mechanisms are used to perform audit log reviews.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive automated mechanisms for audit log reviews across the entire infrastructure stack. VCF Operations for Logs centralizes log aggregation from all VCF components, including vCenter, ESX, NSX, vSAN, VCF Automation, VCF Identity Broker, and VCF Operations for Networks.

This platform offers pre-built dashboards, alerts, and queries through content packs, enabling near real-time analysis, correlation of metrics, events, and logs, and identification of trends and anomalies.

Alert definitions can be based on performance metrics, log events, or custom conditions, with Intelligent Alerts grouping related alerts to reduce noise and facilitate root cause analysis.

VCF supports forwarding all collected logs to external SIEM or syslog targets using secure protocols like TLS, ensuring confidentiality in transit.

The VCF Operations audit events framework captures platform interactions, user logins/logouts, capability checks, and configuration changes, providing detailed activity tracking for security and compliance.

VMware Live Recovery generates security-relevant event logs and alarms for disaster recovery operations, including protection group modifications, host configuration changes, and site connectivity status. These events are integrated with vCenter's alarm framework and flow through its syslog forwarding infrastructure, making them available to centralized monitoring platforms.

VMware vDefend provides extensive security event logging across its Distributed Firewall, Gateway Firewall, and IDS/IPS components.

Network Detection and Response (NDR) collects and analyzes this security event data, correlating detection events into campaigns to build a broader picture of threat activity. Security Intelligence offers centralized analysis of network traffic flows, identifying anomalies and generating policy recommendations.

vDefend components support direct forwarding of these security event logs to external SIEM systems or VCF Operations for Logs, serving as a critical source of network security telemetry.

While the platform provides robust automated tools for log collection, analysis, alerting, and domain-specific correlation, the comprehensive "review" of audit logs, including full enterprise-wide correlation across both technical and non-technical data sources, requires a dedicated Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) solution. Organizations must establish and implement defined policies, processes, and human accountability for ongoing log review, incident escalation, and response procedures to fully meet the intent of this control.

Description:
10.4.2 Logs of all other system components (those not specified in Requirement 10.4.1) are reviewed periodically.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement is applicable to all other in-scope system components not included in Requirement 10.4.1.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation Operations for Logs centralizes and continuously monitors event logs from vCenter, ESX hosts, NSX, vSAN, and other VCF components, providing comprehensive visibility across the infrastructure stack. Administrators utilize search, filtering, pre-built content packs, and dashboards to review log events, identify security-relevant activities such as authentication failures, privilege changes, and configuration modifications. The platform includes an audit events framework that captures user interactions and configuration changes across vSphere, vSAN, and NSX resources, registering each action for review. Alert definitions can be configured against log events and metrics, triggering notifications via email, SNMP traps, webhooks, ServiceNow, or syslog forwarding to external SIEM platforms, supporting robust incident escalation. VMware Live Recovery further generates audit events for disaster recovery operations, including protection group modifications and host configuration changes, with corresponding alarms for event escalation. VMware vDefend contributes by generating security event logs from its Distributed Firewall, Gateway Firewall, and IDS/IPS components, offering real-time monitoring and analysis through a dedicated Security dashboard for ongoing visibility into network security events.

While the platform provides extensive technical capabilities for log generation, collection, centralization, monitoring, and tools for review and incident notification, the organization remains responsible for defining and implementing the specific policies and procedures for the periodic review of these logs. This includes establishing review frequencies, assigning accountability for log review, defining incident escalation timelines, and integrating platform-generated alerts and events into a comprehensive organizational incident response plan.

Description:
10.4.2.1 The frequency of periodic log reviews for all other system components (not defined in Requirement 10.4.1) is defined in the entity’s targeted risk analysis, which is performed according to all elements specified in Requirement 12.3.1.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
Response:
