Additionally, customers must define and implement comprehensive incident response procedures to address both network compromise indicators identified by vDefend and any direct unauthorized changes detected on payment pages.

Description:
11.6.1 A change- and tamper-detection mechanism is deployed as follows:
 • To alert personnel to unauthorized modification (including indicators of compromise, changes, additions, and deletions) to the security-impacting HTTP headers and the script contents of payment pages as received by the consumer browser.
 • The mechanism is configured to evaluate the received HTTP header and payment pages. 
 • The mechanism functions are performed as follows:
  - At least once weeky,
  OR
  - Periodically (at the frequency defined in the entity’s targeted risk analysis, which is performed according to all elements specified in Requirement 12.3.1). 
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement also applies to entities with a webpage(s) that includes a TPSP’s/payment processor’s embedded payment page/form (for example, one or more inline frames or iframes.)
 This requirement does not apply to an entity for scripts in a TPSP’s/payment processor’s embedded payment page/form (for example, one or more iframes), where the entity includes a TPSP’s/payment processor’s payment page/form on its webpage. 
 Scripts in the TPSP’s/payment processor’s embedded payment page/form are the responsibility of the TPSP/payment processor to manage in accordance with this requirement.
 The intention of this requirement is not that an entity installs software in the systems or browsers of its consumers, but rather that the entity uses techniques such as those described under Examples in the PCI DSS Guidance to prevent and detect unexpected script activities.
 This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 12.1 A comprehensive information security policy that governs and provides direction for protection of the entity’s information assets is known and current.
Response:
VMware vDefend's Security Intelligence capabilities categorize detected suspicious traffic events by impact type, including Critical, High, Medium, and Low, which enables security teams to prioritize incident triage based on severity.

Network Detection and Response (NDR) assigns impact scores to campaigns, with higher scores indicating higher priority for triage and response. Detection events are further classified into impact levels, including Informational for scores ranging from 1 to 24.

IDS/IPS and Malware Prevention alerts provide additional contextual signals that contribute to incident classification, directly supporting the protection of the entity's information assets as directed by an overarching security policy.

The establishment, maintenance, and communication of a comprehensive information security policy, ensuring it is known and current, remains an organizational responsibility. While vDefend provides critical technical mechanisms for asset protection and incident classification, the broader incident management and categorization processes are governed by organizational procedures defined and managed by the customer.

Description:
12.1.1 An overall information security policy is: 
 • Established.
 • Published.
 • Maintained.
 • Disseminated to all relevant personnel, as well as to relevant vendors and business partners.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
12.1.2 The information security policy is:
 • Reviewed at least once every 12 months.
 • Updated as needed to reflect changes to business objectives or risks to the environment.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

SCF ID: GOV-02, GOV-04, HRS-03, HRS-03.1, HRS-05, HRS-05.1
Description: 12.1.3 The security policy clearly defines information security roles and responsibilities for all personnel, and all personnel are aware and acknowledge their information security responsibilities.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 12.1.4 Responsibility for information security is formally assigned to a Chief Information Security Officer or other information security knowledgeable member of executive management.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 12.2 Acceptable use policies for end-user technologies are defined and implemented.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
12.2.1 Acceptable use policies for end-user technologies are documented and implemented, including:
 • Explicit approval by authorized parties.
 • Acceptable uses of the technology.
 • List of products approved by the company for employee use, including hardware and software.
 Applicability Notes
 Examples of end-user technologies for which acceptable use policies are expected, include but are not limited to, remote access and wireless technologies, laptops, tablets, mobile phones, and removable electronic media, email usage, and Internet usage.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 12.3 Targeted risks to the cardholder data environment are formally identified, evaluated, and managed.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
12.3.1 For each PCI DSS requirement that specifies completion of a targeted risk analysis, the analysis is documented and includes:
 • Identification of the assets being protected. 
 • Identification of the threat(s) that the requirement is protecting against.
 • Identification of factors that contribute to the likelihood and/or impact of a threat being realized. 
 • Resulting analysis that determines, and includes justification for, how the frequency or processes defined by the entity to meet the requirement minimize the likelihood and/or impact of the threat being realized.
 • Review of each targeted risk analysis at least once every 12 months to determine whether the results are still valid or if an updated risk analysis is needed
 • Performance of updated risk analyses when needed, as determined by the annual review.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides foundational capabilities that generate critical data for targeted risk analyses. Its configuration drift detection, continuous compliance monitoring against industry benchmarks (including vSphere, vSAN, and NSX Security Configuration Guides), and vSphere Lifecycle Manager's desired state enforcement offer objective insights into the security posture of protected assets. These mechanisms identify potential vulnerabilities and deviations from secure baselines, which are essential factors for assessing threats and their likelihood and impact.

VMware vDefend further contributes by analyzing network traffic flows to recommend optimized security policies, identifying unnecessary or overly permissive firewall rules through its Rule Analysis capability, and monitoring infrastructure services and traffic trends. This data helps in identifying assets, understanding specific threats, and evaluating the effectiveness of existing controls.

Moreover, vDefend directly implements technical compensating countermeasures such as microsegmentation, IDS/IPS, malware prevention, URL filtering, and TLS inspection, which are direct technical responses designed to minimize the likelihood and impact of threats, forming a critical part of the justification for control effectiveness within a risk analysis.

Despite these robust technical contributions, the comprehensive requirements of Control 12.3.1, which mandate the formal documentation of targeted risk analyses—including the explicit identification of assets, threats, contributing factors to likelihood and impact, and a justified analysis of how processes minimize risk—are not fully automated by the platforms.

The annual review of these documented analyses and the performance of updated analyses when needed also fall outside the scope of VCF and vDefend's direct capabilities. These aspects require customer-defined policies, established processes, and human oversight to perform the qualitative and quantitative risk assessments, generate the formal documentation, and conduct the periodic reviews necessary to meet the control's intent.

Description:
12.3.2 A targeted risk analysis is performed for each PCI DSS requirement that the entity meets with the customized approach, to include:
 • Documented evidence detailing each element specified in Appendix B: Guidance and Instructions for Using Customized Approach (including, at a minimum, a controls matrix and risk analysis).
 • Approval of documented evidence by senior management.
 • Performance of the targeted analysis of risk at least once every 12 months.
 
 Applicability Notes
 This only applies to entities using a Customized Approach.
Response:
VMware vDefend directly implements technical compensating countermeasures at the network layer through Distributed Firewall microsegmentation, IDS/IPS, malware prevention, URL filtering, and TLS inspection. These capabilities inherently reduce risk exposure and limit the impact of threats within the environment. Intelligent Assist further enhances this by recommending and enabling the creation of targeted or comprehensive remediation policies, such as preventing lateral propagation, which function as compensating controls when primary preventive measures are insufficient. These remediation policies are manually enabled after administrator due diligence and verification, ensuring human oversight of compensating control activation.

The formal performance of a targeted risk analysis for PCI DSS requirements met with a customized approach, the generation of documented evidence detailing each element specified in Appendix B (including a controls matrix and risk analysis), and the approval of this documented evidence by senior management remain organizational responsibilities. VMware solutions do not encompass the organizational risk governance processes or the formal documentation required for customized approach assessments, nor do they perform the targeted analysis of risk at least once every 12 months.

Description:
12.3.3 Cryptographic cipher suites and protocols in use are documented and reviewed at least once every 12 months, including at least the following:
 • An up-to-date inventory of all cryptographic cipher suites and protocols in use, including purpose and where used.
 • Active monitoring of industry trends regarding continued viability of all cryptographic cipher suites and protocols in use.
 • Documentation of a plan to respond to anticipated changes in cryptographic vulnerabilities.
 Applicability Notes
 The requirement applies to all cryptographic cipher suites and protocols used to meet PCI DSS requirements, including, but not limited to, those used to render PAN unreadable in storage and transmission, to protect passwords, and as part of authenticating access. 
 This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation, VMware Live Recovery, and VMware vDefend provide comprehensive technical capabilities for managing cryptographic cipher suites and protocols. VMware Cloud Foundation exposes TLS configurations through APIs, enabling administrators to inventory supported cipher suites, their enabled/disabled status, and adjust them to align with organizational cryptographic policy. It defaults to TLS 1.3 for internal communications and actively updates its cipher suite inventory with each release, removing known vulnerable options. VMware Live Recovery documents its use of TLS 1.2 for replication traffic and provides mechanisms to verify and configure minimum TLS versions. VMware vDefend documents its fixed TLS 1.2 and TLS 1.3 cipher suites for internal component communication, ensuring a consistent and auditable cryptographic posture.

All platforms leverage FIPS 140-3 validated cryptographic modules and provide features such as automated certificate lifecycle management, key rotation, and secure boot, which are critical for maintaining cryptographic hygiene.

While the platform provides the technical foundation and tools for cryptographic management, the organization is responsible for establishing and documenting a comprehensive, up-to-date inventory of *all* cryptographic cipher suites and protocols in use across their entire environment, including their purpose and where used. The organization must also implement an active process for monitoring industry trends regarding the continued viability of these cryptographic elements and document a formal plan to respond to anticipated changes in cryptographic vulnerabilities. These are governance and procedural requirements that extend beyond the platform's technical scope.

Description:
12.3.4 Hardware and software technologies in use are reviewed at least once every 12 months, including at least the following:
 • Analysis that the technologies continue to receive security fixes from vendors promptly.
 • Analysis that the technologies continue to support (and do not preclude) the entity’s PCI DSS compliance.
 • Documentation of any industry announcements or trends related to a technology, such as when a vendor has announced “end of life” plans for a technology.
 • Documentation of a plan, approved by senior management, to remediate outdated technologies, including those for which vendors have announced “end of life” plans.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive capabilities for the periodic review and lifecycle management of hardware and software technologies. VCF Operations includes a Configuration Drift feature that performs scheduled drift detection for vCenter instances and vSphere clusters, comparing current states against desired configuration templates and reporting deviations. For security-specific configuration review, VCF continuously monitors infrastructure against compliance benchmarks, such as the VMware SDDC Benchmark, which assesses adherence to vSphere, vSAN, and NSX Security Configuration Guides. vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) further ensures that ESX hosts conform to a desired software image, including base images, firmware, and third-party components, and can remediate non-compliant hosts. VCF's integrated lifecycle management, including the upgrade planner and SDDC Manager, coordinates updates and upgrades across core components (vCenter, NSX, ESX, vSAN) and Kubernetes workloads (VKS), ensuring technologies receive security fixes and remain current. VMware vDefend enhances this by continuously analyzing network traffic to generate policy recommendations, identifying overly permissive or unnecessary firewall rules, and providing tools like Rule Analysis and the Security Overview Dashboard to facilitate periodic review of network security configurations. These capabilities ensure that technologies continue to support and do not preclude the entity’s PCI DSS compliance by maintaining secure configurations and current software versions.

While the platform provides the technical mechanisms for review, remediation, and maintaining current software versions, the entity is responsible for documenting industry announcements or trends related to a technology, such as vendor "end of life" plans. Furthermore, the entity must document a plan, approved by senior management, to remediate outdated technologies, including those for which vendors have announced "end of life" plans. The platform facilitates the execution of such plans but does not generate or approve the policy-level documentation.

Description: 12.4 PCI DSS compliance is managed.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
12.4.1 Additional requirement for service providers only: Responsibility is established by executive management for the protection of cardholder data and a PCI DSS compliance program to include: 
 • Overall accountability for maintaining PCI DSS compliance.
 • Defining a charter for a PCI DSS compliance program and communication to executive management.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement applies only when the entity being assessed is a service provider.
 Executive management may include C-level positions, board of directors, or equivalent. The specific titles will depend on the particular organizational structure.
 Responsibility for the PCI DSS compliance program may be assigned to individual roles and/or to business units within the organization.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
12.4.2 Additional requirement for service providers only: Reviews are performed at least once every three months to confirm personnel are performing their tasks in accordance with all security policies and all operational procedures. Reviews are performed by personnel other than those responsible for performing the given task and include, but not limited to, the following tasks: 
 • Daily log reviews. 
 • Configuration reviews for network security controls. 
 • Applying configuration standards to new systems. 
 • Responding to security alerts. 
 •  Change-management processes.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement applies only when the entity being assessed is a service provider.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides comprehensive technical capabilities that support the review of personnel performance across critical operational tasks. VCF Operations for Logs centralizes log data from all VCF components, offering dashboards and alerts for security-relevant events such as authentication failures, privilege changes, and configuration modifications, which are essential for reviewing daily log activities.

VCF Operations also includes Configuration Drifts, which monitors configuration changes against desired states, and compliance management that continuously assesses infrastructure against security benchmarks like the VCF 9 Security Baseline, directly supporting configuration reviews for network security controls and the application of configuration standards to new systems.

VCF Automation includes approval policies for catalog instances and integrates with ITSM platforms, while vSphere Lifecycle Manager (vLCM) enforces desired-state configuration and detects drift, providing robust technical support for change management processes.

For responding to security alerts, VCF Operations generates alerts with resolution recommendations and supports multiple outbound notification methods, including email, SNMP, and ServiceNow integration. Role-based access control (RBAC) across VCF components restricts who can make configuration changes, and audit logging captures all change activity for review.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) further contributes by generating audit events for disaster recovery operations, including protection group modifications and host configuration changes for replication.

VLR defines granular privilege levels that separate change authority across DR functions, such as Site Recovery Manager Administrator and Recovery Plans Administrator, supporting the principle of least privilege for personnel performing tasks. Alarms can be configured to escalate events through email notifications or SNMP traps, aiding in the review of security alert responses.

VLR also provides orchestrator workflows and a scripting API to integrate DR change activities with existing change management processes.

VMware vDefend provides configuration change control mechanisms within its network security policy domain, including a deployment wizard that validates changes, staged policy enforcement, and monitoring of changes to input entities.

Security Services Platform includes a Security Segmentation score and report generation capability, along with policy recommendation analysis that compares recommended rules against current NSX inventory, directly supporting configuration reviews for network security controls. The Threat Event Monitoring dashboard provides visibility into detected threats and policy violations, aiding in the review of security alert responses.

vDefend's Rule Analysis capability and Security Intelligence recommendations assist in identifying unnecessary or outdated firewall policies and rules, contributing to the review of configuration standards.

While VCF, VLR, and vDefend provide the essential technical evidence, audit trails, and tools to enable these reviews, the control's core requirement for reviews to be performed by personnel *other than those responsible for performing the given task* at least once every three months, and the confirmation of personnel adherence to security policies and operational procedures, remains an organizational process. The platforms provide the data and mechanisms for an organization to conduct these independent reviews, but they do not automate the human review itself, nor do they define or enforce the organizational policies, procedures, or the specific frequency of the human review process.

Description:
12.4.2.1 Additional requirement for service providers only: Reviews conducted in accordance with Requirement 12.4.2 are documented to include: 
 • Results of the reviews. 
 • Documented remediation actions taken for any tasks that were found to not be performed at Requirement 12.4.2.
 • Review and sign-off of results by personnel assigned responsibility for the PCI DSS compliance program.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement applies only when the entity being assessed is a service provider.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 12.5 PCI DSS scope is documented and validated.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 12.5.1 An inventory of system components that are in scope for PCI DSS, including a description of function/use, is maintained and kept current.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides a comprehensive inventory of its managed infrastructure objects, including virtual machines, hosts, datastores, clusters, network segments, and containers. VCF Operations extends this with scalable tagging and categorization capabilities, allowing assets to be labeled by regulatory jurisdiction, business unit, or compliance scope, thereby facilitating the description of their function and use.

NSX maintains its own inventory with tag-based filtering, enabling organization of network objects and linking classification to policy enforcement. VMware Live Recovery further contributes by maintaining a detailed inventory of protected virtual machines and datastores, tracking their replication status and configurations, which is critical for demonstrating business continuity and data resilience.

VMware vDefend, through its Security Intelligence and Security Services Platform, offers structured asset inventory via auto-discovery or import, supporting hierarchical categorization by region, zone, environment, application, and tier. This platform tracks tags applied to virtual machines, providing an auditable link between asset classification and security policy enforcement, while VCF Operations and NSX Manager continuously monitor infrastructure and verify inventory accuracy to ensure the inventory remains current.

While these capabilities provide the technical foundation for maintaining a compliance-scoped asset inventory, the organizational determination of which specific system components fall within PCI DSS scope, and which statutory, regulatory, or contractual obligations apply to them, remains a customer governance decision. Organizations must leverage VCF's robust categorization and tagging infrastructure to define and apply their specific PCI DSS scope and asset classifications.

Description:
12.5.2 PCI DSS scope is documented and confirmed by the entity at least once every 12 months and upon significant change to the in-scope environment. At a minimum, the scoping validation includes:
 • Identifying all data flows for the various payment stages (for example, authorization, capture settlement, chargebacks, and refunds) and acceptance channels (for example, card-present, card-not-present, and e-commerce).
 • Updating all data-flow diagrams per Requirement 1.2.4.
 • Identifying all locations where account data is stored, processed, and transmitted, including but not limited to: 1) any locations outside of the currently defined CDE, 2) applications that process CHD, 3) transmissions between systems and networks, and 4) file backups.
 • Identifying all system components in the CDE, connected to the CDE, or that could impact security of the CDE.
 • Identifying all segmentation controls in use and the environment(s) from which the CDE is segmented, including justification for environments being out of scope.
 • Identifying all connections from third-party entities with access to the CDE.
 • Confirming that all identified data flows, account data, system components, segmentation controls, and connections from third parties with access to the CDE are included in scope.
 Applicability Notes
 This annual confirmation of PCI DSS scope is an activity expected to be performed by the entity under assessment, and is not the same, nor is it intended to be replaced by, the scoping confirmation performed by the entity’s assessor during the annual assessment.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) and VMware vDefend provide robust capabilities that significantly aid in the identification of elements critical for PCI DSS scope validation. VCF Operations for Networks supports segmentation planning by analyzing traffic flows and grouping them by entities such as VLAN, security group, application tier, subnet, cluster, or individual virtual machine. The NSX Management Pack and NSX Segment Inventory Dashboard offer visibility into logical switches and their dependencies, simplifying the tracking and management of the segmentation scheme. VCF also restricts information processing and storage locations through DRS affinity/anti-affinity rules, Storage Policy Based Management (SPBM) for VM data placement, and workload domains for infrastructure-level separation.

VMware vDefend's Security Intelligence continuously analyzes network traffic flows, generates policy recommendations, and provides segmentation planning workflows to discover traffic flows, identify infrastructure services, and group assets by environment. The Plan and Troubleshoot feature allows review of traffic flow trends over configurable intervals. vDefend's Distributed Firewall enforces microsegmentation at the vNIC level, while the Gateway Firewall provides stateful North-South traffic inspection, including VPN capabilities for external connections. Segmentation Monitoring continuously watches for new servers or services and detects unauthorized traffic, and the Security Segmentation Report quantifies segmentation progress. These platform capabilities provide the foundational data and visibility required to identify data flows, account data locations, system components, and segmentation controls.

However, the control explicitly requires the entity to document the PCI DSS scope, update data-flow diagrams per Requirement 1.2.4, identify all connections from third-party entities with access to the CDE, provide justification for environments being out of scope, and formally confirm that all identified elements are included in scope. These activities involve organizational processes, policy definition, and human accountability for documentation and formal confirmation, which are outside the direct technical automation capabilities of VCF and vDefend.

Description:
12.5.2.1 Additional requirement for service providers only: PCI DSS scope is documented and confirmed by the entity at least once every six months and after significant changes. At a minimum, the scoping validation includes all the elements specified in Requirement 12.5.2.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement applies only when the entity being assessed is a service provider.
 This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive capabilities for graphically representing infrastructure boundaries and identifying compliance-scoped assets. Its Topology Graph Widget, NSX topology visualization, and network configuration workflow visualizations illustrate logical, physical, and network connections, helping to delineate infrastructure components and network segments. VCF maintains a detailed inventory of managed objects, including virtual machines, hosts, and network segments, which can be categorized and tagged at scale to align with regulatory jurisdictions or business units. VCF Operations continuously monitors infrastructure against compliance benchmarks, identifying non-compliant objects and deviations from security baselines through features like Configuration Drift detection and vSphere Lifecycle Manager compliance checks.

VMware vDefend further enhances boundary representation through Security Intelligence's interactive graphical views of network security posture, traffic flows, and segmentation boundaries, using color-coded indicators to distinguish security states. Its Security Services Platform maintains a structured asset inventory with hierarchical categorization and tag-based classification, supporting the mapping of assets to compliance scope boundaries. vDefend's Security Intelligence and Rule Analysis capabilities continuously analyze network traffic and existing firewall rules, generating recommendations to identify and remove unnecessary or overly permissive functions, ports, protocols, and services, thereby supporting periodic review of security configurations.

VMware Live Recovery contributes by maintaining a detailed inventory of protected virtual machines and datastores, organizing assets into protection groups, and using storage policies and tags to identify DR-protected assets, which is directly relevant for demonstrating compliance with business continuity and data resilience obligations.

While the platform provides robust technical tools for visualizing boundaries, identifying assets, and reviewing configurations, the formal determination of PCI DSS scope, its mapping to organizational governance boundaries, and the overarching process of confirming this scope at least once every six months and after significant changes remain organizational responsibilities. The entity must define which statutory, regulatory, or contractual obligations apply to specific assets and establish the formal processes for documenting and validating the overall compliance scope, leveraging the technical data and capabilities provided by VMware Cloud Foundation, vDefend, and Live Recovery.

Description:
12.5.3 Additional requirement for service providers only: Significant changes to organizational structure result in a documented (internal) review of the impact to PCI DSS scope and applicability of controls, with results communicated to executive management.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement applies only when the entity being assessed is a service provider.
 This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 12.6 Security awareness education is an ongoing activity
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 12.6.1 A formal security awareness program is implemented to make all personnel aware of the entity’s information security policy and procedures and their role in protecting the cardholder data.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
12.6.2 The security awareness program is: 
 • Reviewed at least once every 12 months, and 
 • Updated as needed to address any new threats and vulnerabilities that may impact the security of the entity’s cardholder data and/or sensitive authentication data, or the information provided to personnel about their role in protecting cardholder data.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
12.6.3 Personnel receive security awareness training as follows: 
 • Upon hire and at least once every 12 months.
 • Multiple methods of communication are used.
 • Personnel acknowledge at least once every 12 months that they have read and understood the information security policy and procedures.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
12.6.3.1 Security awareness training includes awareness of threats and vulnerabilities that could impact the security of cardholder data and/or sensitive authentication data, including but not limited to:
 • Phishing and related attacks. 
 • Social engineering.
 Applicability Notes
 See Requirement 5.4.1 for guidance on the difference between technical and automated controls to detect and protect users from phishing attacks, and this requirement for providing users security awareness training about phishing and social engineering. These are two separate and distinct requirements, and one is not met by implementing controls required by the other one.
 This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
12.6.3.2 Security awareness training includes awareness about the acceptable use of end-user technologies in accordance with Requirement 12.2.1.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 12.7 Personnel are screened to reduce risks from insider threats.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
12.7.1 Potential personnel who will have access to the CDE are screened, within the constraints of local laws, prior to hire to minimize the risk of attacks from internal sources. 
 Applicability Notes
 For those potential personnel to be hired for positions such as store cashiers, who only have access to one card number at a time when facilitating a transaction, this requirement is a recommendation only.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 12.8 Risk to information assets associated with third-party service provider (TPSP) relationships is managed.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
12.8.1 A list of all third-party service providers (TPSPs) with which account data is shared or that could affect the security of account data is maintained, including a description for each of the services provided.
 Applicability Notes
 The use of a PCI DSS compliant TPSP does not make an entity PCI DSS compliant, nor does it remove the entity’s responsibility for its own PCI DSS compliance.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) establishes a robust security and operational governance framework essential for managing the security posture of cloud instances.

VCF Operations provides comprehensive security and compliance capabilities, including infrastructure configuration measurement against built-in benchmarks such as the VCF Security Baseline and DISA STIGs. Compliance scanning identifies violations, highlights risk areas, and offers remediation recommendations, with support for custom benchmarks to align with specific organizational standards. The VCF Operations security dashboard centralizes security posture information.

Role-based access control (RBAC) is enforced across all VCF components, including NSX, which restricts management access through granular permissions and supports custom role creation. VCF Operations also implements its own RBAC model for monitoring and management functions.

Configuration changes across all infrastructure components are meticulously tracked by VCF Operations, with drift detection identifying deviations from baselines and each change attributed to an authenticated user with a timestamp. Audit events capture all platform interactions, including user logins, capability checks, and configuration modifications across vSphere, vSAN, and NSX resources.

All VCF components support syslog forwarding to external SIEM systems for centralized security monitoring and log retention, with VCF Operations for Logs offering pre-built content packs for security use cases.

VMware vDefend enhances network security within VCF by enforcing microsegmentation policies at the vNIC level via the Distributed Firewall, limiting lateral movement and enabling zero-trust segmentation. The Gateway Firewall extends this protection to north-south traffic at Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) perimeters.

vDefend IDS/IPS provides signature-based and behavioral intrusion detection and prevention, supporting custom signatures and third-party threat feeds, along with virtual patching capabilities.

Malware Prevention through Advanced Threat Prevention (ATP) inspects files traversing network paths, detecting malicious payloads without requiring agents.

The Security Services Platform (SSP) hosts Security Intelligence, which correlates network traffic data to discover services, servers, and communication patterns, categorizing hosts and VMs for security monitoring. Network Detection and Response (NDR) within SSP provides behavioral analysis and anomaly detection across east-west traffic flows.

These capabilities collectively provide the necessary visibility and enforcement to secure the environment, including interactions with third-party services.

While VCF and vDefend provide critical technical controls for securing the environment and monitoring activities within it, the core responsibility for identifying, listing, and describing all third-party service providers (TPSPs) that share account data or affect security remains with the organization.

The platforms do not automate the discovery of external vendor relationships, nor do they maintain a centralized inventory of these entities or their contractual service descriptions. Organizations must establish and execute formal vendor management processes, including due diligence, contractual agreements, and ongoing oversight, to ensure a comprehensive and accurate TPSP list is maintained.

The technical controls within VCF and vDefend, such as RBAC, audit logging, and network segmentation, are instrumental in governing and monitoring the *access and activities* of approved TPSPs within the environment, but they do not fulfill the administrative requirement of creating and maintaining the TPSP inventory itself.

Description:
12.8.2 Written agreements with TPSPs are maintained as follows: 
 • Written agreements are maintained with all TPSPs with which account data is shared or that could affect the security of the CDE.
 • Written agreements include acknowledgments from TPSPs that TPSPs are responsible for the security of account data the TPSPs possess or otherwise store, process, or transmit on behalf of the entity, or to the extent that the TPSP could impact the security of the entity’s cardholder data and/or sensitive authentication data.
 Applicability Notes
 The exact wording of an agreement will depend on the details of the service being provided, and the responsibilities assigned to each party. The agreement does not have to include the exact wording provided in this requirement.
 The TPSP’s written acknowledgment is a confirmation that states the TPSP is responsible for the security of the account data it may store, process, or transmit on behalf of the customer or to the extent the TPSP may impact the security of a customer’s cardholder data and/or sensitive authentication data. 
 Evidence that a TPSP is meeting PCI DSS requirements is not the same as a written acknowledgment specified in this requirement. For example, a PCI DSS Attestation of Compliance (AOC), a declaration on a company’s website, a policy statement, a responsibility matrix, or other evidence not included in a written agreement is not a written acknowledgment.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 12.8.3 An established process is implemented for engaging TPSPs, including proper due diligence prior to engagement.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
12.8.4 A program is implemented to monitor TPSPs’ PCI DSS compliance status at least once every 12 months. 
 Applicability Notes
 Where an entity has an agreement with a TPSP for meeting PCI DSS requirements on behalf of the entity (for example, via a firewall service), the entity must work with the TPSP to make sure the applicable PCI DSS requirements are met. If the TPSP does not meet those applicable PCI DSS requirements, then those requirements are also “not in place” for the entity.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 12.8.5 Information is maintained about which PCI DSS requirements are managed by each TPSP, which are managed by the entity, and any that are shared between the TPSP and the entity.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 12.9 Third-party service providers (TPSPs) support their customers’ PCI DSS compliance.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust access control and network isolation capabilities, enabling organizations to manage risks associated with third-party access.

The platform integrates with federated identity providers through standards-based protocols, extending existing identity governance to the infrastructure. Role-based access control, enforced through the CloudAdmin role model, restricts platform-level operations and prevents unauthorized changes, while vSphere Namespaces scope third-party access to specific workload boundaries.

VCF Automation Organizations facilitate tenant isolation with separate quotas, permissions, and network boundaries, allowing service providers and external partners to operate within defined resource limits. Administrators control which services, roles, and UI plug-ins are published to each tenant, restricting third-party visibility to only what is needed.

Network isolation between tenants is enforced at the NSX segment level, and data-in-transit communications default to TLS 1.3 encryption. VCF Operations monitors resource usage and policy compliance across the environment, providing visibility into third-party activity, and Service Discovery identifies active services and listening ports to detect unauthorized third-party introductions.

VMware vDefend further contributes to mitigating risks from third-party access through network-level access controls and threat detection.

The Distributed Firewall enforces microsegmentation policies at the vNIC level, allowing administrators to define granular network access rules that restrict third-party workloads or user sessions to only the specific application tiers and services required. Context-aware rules can incorporate Active Directory group membership, allowing policies that restrict traffic based on user or workload identity. The Gateway Firewall provides north-south traffic filtering at the perimeter of logical network segments, adding another enforcement point for controlling inbound and outbound access from third-party networks, with URL filtering and TLS inspection capabilities.

vDefend's IDS/IPS monitors east-west and north-south traffic for malicious activity, detecting exploitation attempts from compromised third-party connections. Security Intelligence identifies risky protocols in use across the environment, flagging third-party workloads that transport data in clear text, and the Security Services Platform measures the proportion of workloads not at risk from obsolete operating systems and risky protocols, providing a quantitative view of third-party workload security posture. At-risk workload identification highlights workloads that can expose connected neighbors through unprotected traffic, helping administrators understand and contain the blast radius of a potential third-party compromise.

While the platform provides comprehensive technical controls for managing and securing third-party access within the environment, the ultimate responsibility for ensuring Third-Party Service Providers (TPSPs) support their customers’ PCI DSS compliance rests with the customer. This requires establishing formal third-party management policies, defining contractual agreements that explicitly mandate PCI DSS compliance support, conducting due diligence, and continuously monitoring the TPSP's adherence to these agreements and their own compliance posture.

The platform provides the technical enforcement and visibility tools, but the overarching governance, contractual obligations, and human accountability for TPSP compliance support must be defined and managed by the customer.

Description:
12.9.1 Additional requirement for service providers only: TPSPs provide written agreements to customers that include acknowledgments that TPSPs are responsible for the security of account data the TPSP possesses or otherwise stores, processes, or transmits on behalf of the customer, or to the extent that the TPSP could impact the security of the customer’s cardholder data and/or sensitive authentication data.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement applies only when the entity being assessed is a service provider.
 The exact wording of an agreement will depend on the details of the service being provided, and the responsibilities assigned to each party. The agreement does not have to include the exact wording provided in this requirement.
 The TPSP’s written acknowledgment is a confirmation that states the TPSP is responsible for the security of the account data it may store, process, or transmit on behalf of the customer or to the extent that the TPSP could impact the security of a customer’s cardholder data and/or sensitive authentication data. 
 Evidence that a TPSP is meeting PCI DSS requirements is not the same as a written agreement specified in this requirement. For example, a PCI DSS Attestation of Compliance (AOC), a declaration on a company’s website, a policy statement, a responsibility matrix, or other evidence not included in a written agreement is not a written acknowledgment.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
12.9.2 Additional requirement for service providers only: TPSPs support their customers’ requests for information to meet Requirements 12.8.4 and 12.8.5 by providing the following upon customer request:
 • PCI DSS compliance status information (Requirement 12.8.4).
 • Information about which PCI DSS requirements are the responsibility of the TPSP and which are the responsibility of the customer, including any shared responsibilities (Requirement 12.8.5), for any service the TPSP provides that meets a PCI DSS requirement(s) on behalf of customers or that can impact security of customers’ cardholder data and/or sensitive authentication data.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement applies only when the entity being assessed is a service provider.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 12.1 A comprehensive information security policy that governs and provides direction for protection of the entity’s information assets is known and current.
Response:
VMware vDefend provides security intelligence capabilities that categorize detected suspicious traffic events by impact type, including Critical, High, Medium, and Low, enabling security teams to prioritize incident triage based on severity. Its Network Detection and Response (NDR) assigns impact scores to campaigns, with higher scores indicating higher priority for triage and response, and classifies detection events into impact levels, including Informational for scores ranging from 1 to 24. IDS/IPS and Malware Prevention alerts deliver additional contextual signals that contribute to incident classification, directly supporting the operational protection of information assets.

The establishment, comprehensiveness, currency, and communication of the overarching information security policy that governs and provides direction for the protection of information assets remain the responsibility of the customer. While vDefend's functions are integral to the operational implementation of security measures, the broader incident management and categorization processes, along with the governance and management of the policy itself, are defined and maintained through organizational procedures outside of vDefend.

Description:
12.10.1 An incident response plan exists and is ready to be activated in the event of a suspected or confirmed security incident. The plan includes, but is not limited to: 
 • Roles, responsibilities, and communication and contact strategies in the event of a suspected or confirmed security incident, including notification of payment brands and acquirers, at a minimum.
 • Incident response procedures with specific containment and mitigation activities for different types of incidents.
 • Business recovery and continuity procedures.
 • Data backup processes.
 • Analysis of legal requirements for reporting compromises.
 • Coverage and responses of all critical system components.
 • Reference or inclusion of incident response procedures from the payment brands.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust backup, snapshot, and replication capabilities across its management and workload layers, directly supporting the establishment of Recovery Time Objectives (RTOs) and Recovery Point Objectives (RPOs).

Scheduled backups are configurable for all VCF management components, including SDDC Manager, NSX Manager, and VMware vCenter, utilizing both file-based and image-based methods to ensure consistent restoration of the management plane. On-demand manual backups are also supported.

For workload-level data protection, Changed Block Tracking (CBT) enables incremental backups, and VMware vStorage APIs for Data Protection (VADP) facilitate integration with third-party backup providers. vSAN Data Protection allows external replication of snapshots with automated scheduling and retention, while vSAN Express Storage Architecture (ESA) delivers high-performance, metadata-based snapshots for point-in-time recovery. VMware Kubernetes Service (VKS) supports backup and restore for cluster management workloads, including CSI volume snapshots.

VCF directly provides the technical mechanisms for recurring backups, integrity verification through restore testing, and meeting RTO/RPO requirements.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) extends these capabilities with data replication and snapshot features.

VMware vSphere Replication maintains current replicas of protected virtual machines at a recovery site through continuous host-based replication with configurable RPO intervals, and point-in-time snapshots allow recovery to specific points.

VMware Live Site Recovery further enhances this through vSAN Data Protection protection groups, enabling scheduled management of vSAN snapshots for multiple virtual machines and retaining numerous recovery points. Array-based replication is also supported.

VLR's own configuration data is protected via backup retention settings, and recovery plan testing verifies data recoverability and integrity.

VMware vDefend's Security Services Platform (SSP) provides dedicated backup and restore capabilities for its control plane, encompassing firewall rules, microsegmentation policies, IDS/IPS configurations, and all associated security data.

SSP supports recurring backup schedules, encrypts backup data for transmission to an SFTP server, and enables file-based restoration of the vDefend management plane from a known good state.

This ensures the protection of the environment's security posture configuration.

While the platform provides comprehensive technical tools for data backup, recovery, and business continuity, the organization is solely responsible for the creation, documentation, and activation of a complete incident response plan.

This includes defining roles, responsibilities, communication and contact strategies, specific containment and mitigation activities for different incident types, and overarching business recovery and continuity procedures.

The organization must also conduct an analysis of legal requirements for reporting compromises, ensure the plan covers all critical system components beyond the platform's direct scope, and incorporate incident response procedures from payment brands as mandated.

The platform provides the essential technical foundation for recovery, but the strategic planning, procedural definition, and human accountability remain customer obligations.

Description:
12.10.2 At least once every 12 months, the security incident response plan is: 
 • Reviewed and the content is updated as needed.
 • Tested, including all elements listed in Requirement 12.10.1.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 12.10.3 Specific personnel are designated to be available on a 24/7 basis to respond to suspected or confirmed security incidents.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: 12.10.4 Personnel responsible for responding to suspected and confirmed security incidents are appropriately and periodically trained on their incident response responsibilities.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
12.10.4.1 The frequency of periodic training for incident response personnel is defined in the entity’s targeted risk analysis which is performed according to all elements specified in Requirement 12.3.1.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
12.10.5 The security incident response plan includes monitoring and responding to alerts from security monitoring systems, including but not limited to: 
 • Intrusion-detection and intrusion-prevention systems. 
 • Network security controls.
 • Change-detection mechanisms for critical files.
 • The change-and tamper-detection mechanism for payment pages. This bullet is a best practice until its effective date; refer to Applicability Notes below for details. 
 • Detection of unauthorized wireless access points.
 Applicability Notes
 The bullet above (for monitoring and responding to alerts from a change- and tamper-detection mechanism for payment pages) is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required as part of Requirement 12.10.5 and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) Operations provides comprehensive monitoring and automated correlation of metrics, events, and logs across the VCF infrastructure stack, including vCenter, ESX hosts, vSAN, and NSX. It supports multi-condition alert definitions by correlating signals from various sources and offers centralized log collection and analysis with log-based alerts. VCF supports syslog forwarding to external SIEM platforms for enterprise-wide correlation and integrates with third-party IT service management platforms like ServiceNow for consolidated status and alerts. VCF provides technical capabilities for automated event detection through faults and vCenter Server alarms, analysis via a Troubleshooting Incidents page, Diagnostic Findings, and Log Assist, and containment through VM power state changes and network reconfiguration.

VMware vDefend, extending VCF's networking foundation, correlates network detection events into campaigns, providing Security Intelligence and IDS/IPS for intrusion detection and prevention. vDefend supports targeted containment through quarantine policies and automated remediation via Intelligent Assist.

VMware Live Recovery (VLR) further supports incident handling for disaster recovery scenarios through structured recovery plans, automated failover orchestration, and recovery plan testing to validate response procedures.

While VCF, vDefend, and VLR provide robust technical capabilities for monitoring, alerting, and supporting various phases of incident handling, the organization is responsible for defining and implementing the overarching security incident response plan. This includes establishing formal response procedures, assigning human accountability for monitoring and responding to alerts, and integrating the technical outputs from VCF and vDefend into a comprehensive enterprise-wide incident management process.

Full enterprise-wide correlation across both technical and non-technical data sources requires a SIEM or similar platform beyond vDefend's scope. Additionally, specific mechanisms for change and tamper detection on payment pages (a best practice until March 2025) and the detection of unauthorized wireless access points are not directly provided by VCF or vDefend and would require complementary tooling or services.

Description: 12.10.6 The security incident response plan is modified and evolved according to lessons learned and to incorporate industry developments.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
12.10.7 Incident response procedures are in place, to be initiated upon the detection of stored PAN anywhere it is not expected, and include:
 • Determining what to do if PAN is discovered outside the CDE, including its retrieval, secure deletion, and/or migration into the currently defined CDE, as applicable.
 • Identifying whether sensitive authentication data is stored with PAN. 
 • Determining where the account data came from and how it ended up where it was not expected.
 • Remediating data leaks or process gaps that resulted in the account data being where it was not expected. 
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust technical capabilities that support an organization's incident response to the detection of unexpected stored PAN. VCF Operations centralizes event data and compliance monitoring across the environment, aggregating log data from infrastructure components and forwarding events to enterprise SIEM platforms, which is critical for correlating events during spill investigation workflows. NSX network segments and security groups offer containment mechanisms at the virtualization layer, enabling the isolation of affected workloads from the broader network. VCF Automation supports creating network profiles with isolation by subnet, VLAN transport zone, or security group, which can be applied to quarantine contaminated systems. VM snapshots capture the memory, disk, and settings state of a virtual machine, preserving evidence for forensic analysis while corrective actions are underway. For data protection and recovery during remediation, vSAN native snapshots provide local recovery points, and vSphere HA and vSphere Fault Tolerance support workload continuity when contaminated infrastructure needs to be taken offline. VMware vDefend further contributes through Security Intelligence, which monitors traffic flows and detects leaked flows not covered by existing Distributed Firewall rules, enabling administrators to identify unauthorized data movement between environments. The Policy Recommendations engine analyzes these leaked flows and generates modified firewall rules to contain the spill by restricting communication for affected workloads, guiding administrators to monitor traffic leakage, establish exceptions, and implement lockdown protections as part of the remediation workflow.

The establishment of formal incident response procedures, including the specific steps for retrieving, securely deleting, or migrating PAN, remains an organizational responsibility. Determining the origin of unexpected account data, identifying whether sensitive authentication data is stored with PAN, and remediating underlying data leaks or process gaps are procedural and policy-driven activities. Broader organizational processes such as legal notification, evidence chain-of-custody, data classification reassessment, user communication, forensic investigation, and regulatory reporting are outside the scope of the platform's technical capabilities.

Description: A1.1 Multi-tenant service providers protect and segregate all customer environments and data.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust multi-tenant isolation across compute, storage, networking, and management layers.

At the hypervisor level, vSphere segregates virtual machines with dedicated virtual CPU, memory, and storage resources, enforcing isolation through CPU scheduler controls, memory reservation policies, and restricted port-level configurations.

Network segmentation is achieved via vSphere Distributed Switch capabilities like VLAN tagging and Private VLANs, which prevent lateral movement. NSX extends this with Projects and Virtual Private Clouds, offering isolated security and networking configurations with tenant-level access control and self-contained network topologies. VMware vDefend Distributed Firewall further enhances this with microsegmentation at the vNIC level, enabling granular east-west traffic isolation between tenants.

Platform management is multi-tenant aware through VCF Automation organizations, which provide infrastructure isolation, resource quotas, and API token management per tenant. Workload domain isolation, fault domains, and DRS VM-VM anti-affinity rules ensure physical and logical separation.

For containerized workloads, vSphere Namespaces provide resource-bounded, isolated environments with RBAC, encryption, and network segmentation integrated with NSX and PVLANs.

Storage multi-tenancy is supported through vSAN storage policies, datastore-level permissions, I/O limits, and cryptographic key separation.

VMware vDefend also contributes through tenant-aware IDS/IPS, allowing isolation of security and networking configurations across tenants, with logging that provides visibility into per-tenant security operations. Its Security Services Platform offers planning tools for designing microsegmentation strategies that isolate different environments and tenants.

While the platform provides comprehensive technical mechanisms for multi-tenant protection and segregation, the customer is responsible for defining and implementing specific security policies, configuring access controls, and managing tenant environments.

For disaster recovery, VMware Live Recovery offers access segmentation in shared recovery site configurations through granular permission management on vCenter inventory objects like VM folders, resource pools, datastores, and networks. However, administrators must understand that a shared recovery site model assumes a degree of trust between vSphere sites, and complete isolation between all users in such a configuration is not achievable; dedicated infrastructure is required for absolute separation. The customer must also ensure consistent role assignments across protected and recovery sites and configure automatic protection accounts appropriately for multiple VLR deployments.

The effectiveness of these capabilities relies on the customer's architectural decisions, ongoing configuration, and operational management to enforce the desired level of tenant segregation and data protection.

Description:
A1.1.1 Logical separation is implemented as follows:
 • The provider cannot access its customers’ environments without authorization.
 • Customers cannot access the provider’s environment without authorization.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) implements comprehensive logical separation across compute, storage, networking, and management layers, ensuring that environments are isolated. At the hypervisor level, vSphere segregates virtual machines with dedicated virtual CPU, memory, and storage resources, enforcing isolation through CPU scheduler controls, memory reservation policies, and restricted port-level configurations.

Network segmentation is robustly provided by vSphere Distributed Switch, supporting VLAN tagging, Private VLANs (PVLANs) for isolation within the same VLAN, and NSX Projects, which deliver isolated security and networking configurations with tenant-level access control. VMware vDefend Distributed Firewall further enhances this with microsegmentation, applying granular rules at the vNIC level for east-west traffic isolation between tenants, and allowing policy scoping to define enforcement boundaries per tenant.

VCF Automation supports multi-tenancy through organizations, providing infrastructure isolation, resource quotas, and API token management per tenant. Workload domains offer complete infrastructure separation, and DRS VM-VM anti-affinity rules ensure physical isolation across hosts.

For containerized workloads, vSphere Namespaces provide resource-bounded, isolated environments with role-based access control and network segmentation enforced via NSX and PVLANs.

Storage multi-tenancy is achieved through vSAN storage policies, datastore-level permissions, and cryptographic key management for tenant separation.

VMware Live Recovery contributes to access segmentation in multi-tenant disaster recovery environments by enabling vCenter administrators to manage permissions, assign dedicated resources (VM folders, resource pools, datastores, networks) per user, and restrict permission propagation, thereby preventing unauthorized access to other organizations' resources. VMware vDefend further strengthens this by providing tenant-aware IDS/IPS with logging of tenant context for security configuration changes, and segmentation planning tools that support environment isolation.

While the platform provides the technical mechanisms for robust logical separation and the enforcement of authorization, the customer is responsible for defining and implementing specific authorization policies, managing user identities and access controls, and ensuring that operational processes align with the principle of least privilege. This includes configuring roles and permissions within vCenter, NSX, and VCF Automation, and establishing procedures for granting, reviewing, and revoking access to customer and provider environments.

Description: A1.1.2 Controls are implemented such that each customer only has permission to access its own cardholder data and CDE.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive multi-tenant isolation across compute, storage, networking, and management layers.

At the hypervisor level, vSphere segregates virtual machines with dedicated virtual CPU, memory, and storage resources, enforcing isolation through CPU scheduler controls, memory reservation policies, and restricted port-level configurations.

Network segmentation is achieved via vSphere Distributed Switch with VLANs, Private VLANs, and NSX Projects, which provide isolated security and networking configurations with tenant-level access control. NSX Virtual Private Clouds further offer self-contained networks with their own permission models.

VCF Automation supports multi-tenancy through organizations, providing infrastructure isolation, resource quotas, and API token management per tenant. Workload domains, fault domains, and DRS anti-affinity rules ensure infrastructure and physical separation.

For containerized workloads, vSphere Namespaces offer resource-bounded environments with RBAC, encryption, and network segmentation, acting as isolated sandboxes.

Storage multi-tenancy is enforced through vSAN storage policies, datastore-level permissions, I/O limits, and cryptographic separation using different key managers.

VMware vDefend enhances this with microsegmentation via the Distributed Firewall at the vNIC level, enabling granular east-west traffic isolation between tenants with policy scoping. vDefend IDS/IPS supports multi-tenancy, isolating security configurations and logging tenant context for auditability.

VMware Live Recovery provides access segmentation mechanisms for multi-tenant disaster recovery environments, allowing vCenter administrators to manage permissions for separate VM folders, resource pools, datastores, and networks per user on shared recovery sites, with consistent role assignments across paired sites.

While the platform delivers robust technical capabilities for multi-tenant isolation and access control, the customer is responsible for defining and implementing specific access policies and permissions for each tenant. This includes configuring the granular firewall rules, assigning appropriate roles and privileges within vCenter and NSX, managing API tokens, and establishing operational processes to ensure that each customer's access is strictly limited to their own cardholder data and CDE. For complete isolation in disaster recovery scenarios, the customer must also consider dedicated infrastructure rather than relying solely on shared recovery site configurations.

Description: A1.1.3 Controls are implemented such that each customer can only access resources allocated to them.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides comprehensive multi-tenant isolation across all layers of the infrastructure. At the hypervisor level, vSphere segregates virtual machines with dedicated virtual CPU, memory, and storage resources, enforced through CPU scheduler isolation, memory reservation policies, and restricted port-level configurations. Workload domains, fault domains, and DRS VM-VM anti-affinity rules further enhance physical and logical isolation.

For containerized workloads, vSphere Namespaces provide resource-bounded environments with RBAC, encryption, and network segmentation, ensuring teams cannot exceed allocated resources or access other namespaces. Network segmentation is robustly supported by vSphere Distributed Switch with VLAN tagging, Private VLANs (PVLANs), and NSX Projects, which provide isolated security and networking configurations per tenant. NSX Virtual Private Clouds offer self-contained networks with their own permission models.

Storage multi-tenancy is achieved through vSAN storage policies, datastore-level permissions, I/O limits, and cryptographic key managers for tenant separation.

At the platform management layer, VCF Automation organizations provide infrastructure isolation, resource quotas, and API token management per tenant. VMware vDefend enhances this with microsegmentation via Distributed Firewall at the vNIC level, granular policy scoping using the "Applied To" field, and tenant-aware IDS/IPS for east-west traffic isolation and threat detection.

VMware Live Recovery provides access segmentation mechanisms for multi-tenant disaster recovery environments, enabling vCenter administrators to manage permissions for separate VM folders, resource pools, datastores, and networks, ensuring users can only configure and operate DR for their allocated resources.

While the platform provides extensive technical capabilities for resource isolation and access control, the customer is responsible for the correct implementation and ongoing management of these controls. This includes defining and applying appropriate resource allocations, configuring access policies, setting up network segmentation rules, and establishing disaster recovery configurations. For environments demanding absolute separation between all users in a shared recovery site, the customer must implement dedicated infrastructure rather than relying on shared VMware Live Recovery deployments.

Description:
A1.1.4 The effectiveness of logical separation controls used to separate customer environments is confirmed at least once every six months via penetration testing. 
 Applicability Notes
 The testing of adequate separation between customers in a multi-tenant service provider environment is in addition to the penetration tests specified in Requirement 11.4.6.
 This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation establishes robust logical separation across compute, storage, networking, and management layers within multi-tenant environments. vSphere segregates virtual machines with dedicated resources, enforcing CPU scheduler and memory isolation. Network segmentation is achieved through vSphere Distributed Switch capabilities like VLAN tagging and Private VLANs, further extended by NSX Projects and Virtual Private Clouds which provide isolated security and networking configurations.

VCF Automation supports multi-tenancy through organizations, offering infrastructure isolation, resource quotas, and API token management. Workload domains and DRS anti-affinity rules ensure physical and infrastructure separation, while vSAN storage policies and cryptographic key management provide storage-level multi-tenancy.

For containerized workloads, VMware Kubernetes Service integrates vSphere Namespaces for resource-bounded, isolated environments with RBAC and network segmentation.

VMware Live Recovery provides access segmentation mechanisms for multi-tenant disaster recovery, ensuring users have appropriate privileges for their own protected sites and resources through granular permission management on virtual machine folders, resource pools, datastores, and networks.

VMware vDefend enhances this with microsegmentation via the Distributed Firewall at the vNIC level, enforcing granular east-west traffic isolation between tenants. It provides tenant-aware IDS/IPS for threat detection and policy scoping capabilities to define enforcement boundaries per tenant. Security Services Platform offers segmentation planning and monitoring tools, including Network Detection and Response for correlating attack campaigns across network segments.

While these platforms provide the comprehensive technical controls for logical separation, the explicit requirement to confirm their effectiveness via penetration testing at least once every six months is an organizational process. The platforms do not natively perform or manage these penetration tests. Customers are responsible for engaging qualified personnel or third parties to conduct these penetration tests against the deployed environment and for establishing the associated policies and procedures to ensure this recurring confirmation.

Description: A1.2 Multi-tenant service providers facilitate logging and incident response for all customers.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation establishes the foundational logging infrastructure, collecting audit events from vSphere, vSAN, NSX, and other VCF components, including third-party elements like Kafka and Kubernetes. VCF Operations for Logs centralizes this collection and supports forwarding to external syslog destinations with TLS encryption, providing the technical basis for tenant-level security event visibility and export to external log management or SIEM systems.

NSX Projects and VCF Automation organizations ensure tenant-level isolation for security and networking objects, enabling audit trails and user activity tracking to be scoped precisely to individual tenants.

VCF also provides robust alerting and monitoring, generating risk and critical alerts with remediation guidance, and integrates with ServiceNow for automated incident creation. For incident response, VCF supports immutable snapshots for tamper-resistant evidence preservation and provides a management pack for ServiceNow to integrate alerts into existing ticketing workflows.

VMware vDefend extends these capabilities by generating detailed network security events from Distributed and Gateway Firewalls, Distributed IDS/IPS, and Malware Prevention Service, all with tenant context. It supports remote logging to centralized systems and publishes Network Detection and Response (NDR) events to SIEMs.

vDefend's Security Intelligence analyzes network traffic for suspicious patterns, providing a network-layer forensic timeline, while its Auditor role supports separation of duties during investigations. Intelligent Assist capabilities within vDefend enable rapid remediation actions, including emergency firewall policies.

VMware Live Recovery further contributes by generating tenant-attributable audit events for disaster recovery operations, including permission changes and recovery plan executions, with RBAC ensuring per-tenant isolation and support for forwarding DR event data to external log management systems.

While the platform provides comprehensive technical tools for multi-tenant logging, event generation, forensic evidence collection, and incident response acceleration, the service provider remains responsible for defining and implementing specific per-tenant log retention policies, mapping logged events to statutory, regulatory, or contractual obligations, establishing formal forensic investigation procedures, maintaining chain-of-custody documentation, and developing comprehensive incident communication and customer notification processes.

Description:
A1.2.1 Audit log capability is enabled for each customer’s environment that is consistent with PCI DSS Requirement 10, including:
 • Logs are enabled for common third-party applications.
 • Logs are active by default.
 • Logs are available for review only by the owning customer.
 • Log locations are clearly communicated to the owning customer.
 • Log data and availability is consistent with PCI DSS Requirement 10
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides the foundational logging infrastructure, enabling tenant-level security event visibility. It captures platform interactions, including authentication, configuration changes, API calls, and storage or network modifications across vSphere, vSAN, and NSX resources.
