VCF Operations for Logs integrates with vCenter Servers and other VCF components for centralized log collection, including logs from common third-party applications such as Kafka, PostgreSQL, RabbitMQ, and Kubernetes. NSX Projects and VCF Automation organizations establish tenant-level isolation for security and networking objects, ensuring that logging and audit trails can be scoped to individual tenants, thereby supporting the requirement for logs to be available for review only by the owning customer. VCF supports forwarding logs to external syslog destinations with TLS encryption. VMware Live Recovery further contributes by generating tenant-attributable audit events for permission changes, recovery plan execution, and replication status, with RBAC ensuring per-user isolation. VMware vDefend enhances this by providing network security event generation from Distributed and Gateway Firewalls, logging tenant context for IDS/IPS, and enabling NDR and Malware Prevention Service logs to capture tenant IDs, facilitating per-tenant attribution of network security events. These capabilities provide the technical foundation for collecting and forwarding comprehensive, tenant-scoped audit data.

While the platform provides robust technical capabilities for log generation, collection, and forwarding, the service provider is responsible for defining and enforcing per-tenant log retention policies, mapping logged events to specific statutory, regulatory, or contractual obligations such as the full scope of PCI DSS Requirement 10, and implementing log access controls for tenant self-service. Ensuring logs are active by default across all components and communicating log locations to the owning customer are also operational responsibilities. These aspects require organizational processes, customer configuration, and integration with external log management or SIEM systems to achieve full compliance with the control's intent.

Description: A1.2.2 Processes or mechanisms are implemented to support and/or facilitate prompt forensic investigations in the event of a suspected or confirmed security incident for any customer.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) implements robust mechanisms to support and facilitate prompt forensic investigations for multi-tenant environments.

VCF Operations audit events capture platform interactions, including authentication, configuration changes, and security-related activities across the infrastructure, providing critical input for forensic timelines. VCF Operations for Logs centralizes log collection from vCenter, NSX, and other VCF components, offering a single point for retrieving log-based evidence.

For evidence preservation, VM snapshots capture the memory, disk, and settings state, with quiescing options for filesystem consistency. vSAN's Snapshot Service extends this with policy-based scheduling and retention, including immutable snapshots that create tamper-resistant, read-only copies for forensic integrity.

NSX Projects and VCF Automation organizations enable multi-tenancy by isolating security, networking objects, and ownership tracking, ensuring audit trails and log data can be scoped to specific tenant environments without exposing other tenants' data.

VCF also supports forwarding tenant-scoped telemetry and audit data to external SIEM or forensic analysis platforms via syslog integration for deeper investigation.

VMware vDefend further enhances multi-tenant forensic capabilities by providing network-level threat detection, traffic analysis, and event correlation.

Security Intelligence analyzes network traffic flows, detecting suspicious patterns and classifying them using MITRE ATT&CK tactics and techniques. This allows investigators to review participating VMs, examine event sequences like command-and-control activity followed by data exfiltration, and trace lateral movement, thereby constructing a network-layer forensic timeline.

The Security Services Platform tracks file inspection activity, including malicious file detections and trends, and correlates exfiltration events with preceding infection-type events to reconstruct attack chains.

The Distributed Firewall's audit logging records rule evaluations and connection attempts, providing evidence of network communications during an investigation.

The vDefend Auditor role supports separation of duties by allowing forensic personnel to view security information without modifying configurations.

While the platform provides extensive technical capabilities for evidence collection, preservation, and analysis across host and network layers, establishing formal forensic investigation procedures, chain-of-custody documentation, and coordination with law enforcement are organizational responsibilities that remain with the customer.

Description:
A1.2.3 Processes or mechanisms are implemented for reporting and addressing suspected or confirmed security incidents and vulnerabilities, including:
 • Customers can securely report security incidents and vulnerabilities to the provider. 
 • The provider addresses and remediates suspected or confirmed security incidents and vulnerabilities according to Requirement 6.3.1.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) provides robust capabilities for detecting, monitoring, and integrating security incident and vulnerability management. VCF Operations generates risk and critical alerts, complete with remediation steps, enabling prompt investigation and actionable guidance. Platform operators can configure alerts, perform comprehensive log analysis, and run infrastructure diagnostics across the VCF environment.

For streamlined incident management, VCF Operations includes a Management Pack for ServiceNow, which automatically creates and tracks incidents from VCF alerts, associating them with configuration items in the CMDB.

NSX Projects and VCF Automation ensure tenant-level isolation, allowing incident investigation and response to be precisely scoped to affected tenants without cross-tenant exposure. VCF Automation's ownership tracking further aids in identifying affected tenants.

Furthermore, VCF supports forwarding logs and alerts to external SIEM platforms via syslog, integrating VCF telemetry into centralized incident response and orchestration systems.

VMware vDefend complements these capabilities by supporting multi-tenant IDS/IPS deployments and offering Intelligent Assist features. These features empower security teams to select and execute targeted or comprehensive remediation strategies in response to detected security incidents, including the ability to create emergency firewall policies for non-IDS/IPS events, thereby accelerating incident response within the vDefend platform across tenant environments.

While VCF and vDefend provide the technical mechanisms for detecting, investigating, and remediating security incidents and vulnerabilities, the secure channels for customers to report these incidents to the provider, along with the overarching organizational processes for incident communication, customer notification, and vulnerability disclosure timelines, are outside the technical scope of these platforms. These aspects require the service provider to define and implement specific policies, procedures, and human accountability.

Description: A2.1 POI terminals using SSL and/or early TLS are confirmed as not susceptible to known SSL/TLS exploits.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF), VMware Live Recovery (VLR), and VMware vDefend components are engineered to operate exclusively with modern TLS protocols, specifically TLS 1.3 as the default for VCF internal communications with TLS 1.2 as a compatibility fallback, and TLS 1.2 for VLR replication traffic. These platforms do not utilize SSL or early TLS versions, inherently ensuring their own components are not susceptible to known exploits associated with these deprecated protocols.

Cryptographic operations across VCF leverage FIPS 140-3 validated modules, and VCF Operations for Networks can enforce FIPS mode for both internal and external connections, further strengthening the cryptographic posture. VCF provides comprehensive data-in-transit encryption for management, compute, and storage networks, including vSAN DIT encryption, encrypted vSphere vMotion, and VM Encryption, all relying on robust TLS implementations and FIPS-validated cryptography. VLR mandates TLS 1.2 for replication traffic and secures all management communications via configurable TLS connections, with certificate verification enforced during site pairing.

For customer-deployed applications and services, which may function as Point-of-Interaction (POI) terminals within the VCF infrastructure, vDefend provides critical capabilities to ensure adherence to secure cryptographic standards. vDefend's TLS inspection and enforcement features allow for the decryption, inspection, and re-encryption of HTTPS traffic at the network perimeter, enabling policy application, URL filtering, and threat detection on encrypted sessions. Furthermore, vDefend identifies and flags risky cleartext application protocols such as HTTP, Telnet, and FTP, providing visibility into unencrypted traffic flows and at-risk workloads. This capability allows security teams to detect and block any attempts by customer applications to use insecure protocols like SSL or early TLS, or to transmit sensitive data in cleartext. While the platform provides the robust technical foundation and tools to prevent and detect the use of insecure protocols, the customer is responsible for configuring and enforcing network policies within NSX and leveraging vDefend's monitoring and inspection capabilities to ensure that all deployed POI terminals adhere to secure TLS standards and are confirmed not susceptible to known SSL/TLS exploits.

Description:
A2.1.1 Where POS POI terminals at the merchant or payment acceptance location use SSL and/or early TLS, the entity confirms the devices are not susceptible to any known exploits for those protocols.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement is intended to apply to the entity with the POS POI terminal, such as a merchant. This requirement is not intended for service providers who serve as the termination or connection point to those POS POI terminals. Requirements A2.1.2 and A2.1.3 apply to POS POI service providers.
 The allowance for POS POI terminals that are not currently susceptible to exploits is based on currently known risks. If new exploits are introduced to which POS POI terminals are susceptible, the POS POI terminals will need to be updated immediately.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
A2.1.2 Additional requirement for service providers only: All service providers with existing connection points to POS POI terminals that use SSL and/or early TLS as defined in A2.1 have a formal Risk Mitigation and Migration Plan in place that includes:
 • Description of usage, including what data is being transmitted, types and number of systems that use and/or support SSL/early TLS, and type of environment. 
 • Risk-assessment results and risk-reduction controls in place.
 • Description of processes to monitor for new vulnerabilities associated with SSL/early TLS.
 • Description of change control processes that are implemented to ensure SSL/early TLS is not implemented into new environments.
 • Overview of migration project plan to replace SSL/early TLS at a future date.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement applies only when the entity being assessed is a service provider.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) components inherently utilize modern cryptographic protocols, with TLS 1.3 as the default for internal communications and TLS 1.2 available for compatibility. All management interfaces, including SDDC Manager, vCenter, NSX Manager, and VCF Operations, are served exclusively over HTTPS. Cryptographic operations leverage FIPS 140-3 validated modules, and VCF Operations for Networks can enforce FIPS mode. Data-in-transit encryption is extensively applied across the platform, including vSAN data-in-transit encryption, encrypted vSphere vMotion, and VM Encryption, which protects data before it reaches the storage layer. VMware Live Recovery (VLR) secures all communications with TLS, with configurable minimum TLS versions and mandatory TLS 1.2 encryption for replication traffic. VMware vDefend contributes by providing TLS inspection capabilities to enforce policies on encrypted traffic and identify risky cleartext application protocols, thereby surfacing unprotected traffic flows and enabling the identification of gaps in encryption coverage for workloads running on the infrastructure.

While the platform provides a robust foundation that avoids the use of SSL and early TLS for its native components, the creation and maintenance of a formal Risk Mitigation and Migration Plan remain the responsibility of the service provider. This plan must encompass a description of usage for any existing SSL/early TLS connections to POS POI terminals, including data transmitted and systems involved, along with risk assessment results and reduction controls. The service provider is also accountable for defining processes to monitor for new vulnerabilities, implementing change control to prevent new SSL/early TLS deployments, and developing a comprehensive migration project plan to replace any remaining SSL/early TLS usage at a future date.

Description:
A2.1.3 Additional requirement for service providers only: All service providers provide a secure service offering.
 Applicability Notes
 This requirement applies only when the entity being assessed is a service provider.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: A3.1 A PCI DSS compliance program is implemented.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

A3.1.1 Responsibility is established by executive
management for the protection of account data and
a PCI DSS compliance program that includes:
• Overall accountability for maintaining PCI DSS
compliance.
• Defining a charter for a PCI DSS compliance
program.
• Providing updates to executive management
and board of directors on PCI DSS compliance
initiatives and issues, including remediation
activities, at least once every 12 months.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
A3.1.2 A formal PCI DSS compliance program is in
place that includes:
• Definition of activities for maintaining and
monitoring overall PCI DSS compliance,
including business-as-usual activities.
• Annual PCI DSS assessment processes.
• Processes for the continuous validation of PCI
DSS requirements (for example, daily, weekly,
every three months, as applicable per the
requirement).
• A process for performing business-impact
analysis to determine potential PCI DSS impacts
for strategic business decisions.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
A3.1.3 PCI DSS compliance roles and
responsibilities are specifically defined and formally
assigned to one or more personnel, including:
• Managing PCI DSS business-as-usual activities.
• Managing annual PCI DSS assessments.
• Managing continuous validation of PCI DSS
requirements (for example, daily, weekly, every

Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

A3.1.4 Up-to-date PCI DSS and/or information
security training is provided at least once every 12
months to personnel with PCI DSS compliance
responsibilities (as identified in A3.1.3).
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description: A3.2 PCI DSS scope is documented and validated.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
A3.2.1 PCI DSS scope is documented and
confirmed for accuracy at least once every three

months and upon significant changes to the in-
scope environment. At a minimum, the scoping

validation includes:
• Identifying all data flows for the various payment
stages (for example, authorization, capture,
settlement, chargebacks, and refunds) and

acceptance channels (for example, card-
present, card-not-present, and e-commerce).

• Updating all data-flow diagrams per
Requirement 1.2.4.
• Identifying all locations where account data is
stored, processed, and transmitted, including but
not limited to 1) any locations outside of the
currently defined CDE, 2) applications that
process CHD, 3) transmissions between
systems and networks, and 4) file backups.
• For any account data found outside of the
currently defined CDE, either 1) securely delete
it, 2) migrate it into the currently defined CDE, or
3) expand the currently defined CDE to include
it.
• Identifying all system components in the CDE,
connected to the CDE, or that could impact
security of the CDE.
Identifying all segmentation controls in use and
the environment(s) from which the CDE is
segmented, including justification for
environments being out of scope
• Identifying all connections to third-party entities
with access to the CDE.
• Confirming that all identified data flows, account
data, system components, segmentation
controls, and connections from third parties with
access to the CDE are included in scope.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) and VMware vDefend provide robust capabilities that are foundational for defining and maintaining the Cardholder Data Environment (CDE) scope, particularly concerning network segmentation and traffic visibility.

VCF establishes network segmentation through VLANs, vSphere Distributed Switches, and NSX software-defined networking, allowing for the isolation of management, vMotion, storage, and workload traffic. NSX segments and Virtual Private Clouds (VPCs) enable dynamic creation of isolated Layer 2 networks, while Network I/O Control prioritizes bandwidth. VCF Operations for Networks supports segmentation planning by analyzing traffic flows and provides visibility into logical switches and their dependencies. NSX Edge nodes provide the north-south boundary for DMZ architectures, allowing for distinct network zones.

VMware vDefend extends these capabilities with fine-grained microsegmentation via the Distributed Firewall, applying security policies at the vNIC level of every virtual machine and container, independent of network placement. The vDefend Gateway Firewall provides stateful North-South traffic inspection at the network edge, including FQDN analysis and logging of traffic crossing zone boundaries. Security Intelligence within vDefend offers segmentation planning and monitoring tools, including traffic flow analysis, policy recommendations based on observed patterns, and continuous monitoring for new servers or services. vDefend IDS/IPS inspects traffic for known attack signatures, and Network Detection and Response (NDR) correlates detection events to identify multi-stage attack campaigns, providing critical visibility into traffic within and across CDE segments and DMZ zones.

While VCF and vDefend provide essential technical controls for network segmentation, traffic monitoring, and CDE boundary enforcement, the comprehensive scope validation required by this control remains an organizational responsibility.

The platform does not automatically identify all data flows for various payment stages and acceptance channels, nor does it inherently identify all locations where account data is stored, processed, and transmitted within applications, databases, or file backups.

Updating data-flow diagrams, securely deleting or migrating account data found outside the CDE, identifying all system components (beyond network infrastructure), and identifying all connections to third-party entities with CDE access are processes that require customer-defined policies, procedures, and often additional tools or manual verification.

The overarching documentation and confirmation of scope accuracy at least once every three months and upon significant changes are also customer-driven processes.

Description:
A3.2.2 PCI DSS scope impact for all changes to
systems or networks is determined, including
additions of new systems and new network
connections. Processes include:
• Performing a formal PCI DSS impact
assessment.
• Identifying applicable PCI DSS requirements to
the system or network.
• Updating PCI DSS scope as appropriate.
• Documented sign-off of the results of the impact
assessment by responsible personnel (as
defined in A3.1.3).
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
A3.2.2.1 Upon completion of a change, all relevant
PCI DSS requirements are confirmed to be
implemented on all new or changed systems and
networks, and documentation is updated as
applicable.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation, VMware Live Recovery, and VMware vDefend collectively provide robust capabilities for verifying the implementation of relevant security and data protection controls following a change.

VMware Cloud Foundation offers configuration drift detection, vSphere Configuration Profiles, and vSphere Lifecycle Manager compliance checks to ensure infrastructure components align with desired states and compliance benchmarks. VMware Live Recovery enables comprehensive testing of recovery plans and continuous monitoring of replication status, confirming data protection mechanisms function as intended post-change. VMware vDefend provides a draft-based workflow for firewall policy changes, allowing for review and validation before enforcement, and includes features like Rule Analysis and change monitoring to verify the correctness and effectiveness of network security controls after modifications. Across all platforms, audit logging, detailed reports, and exportable configurations provide essential evidence to support documentation updates.

While these platforms offer extensive technical verification and audit trails, the ultimate responsibility for confirming the functional effectiveness of all PCI DSS requirements, particularly through comprehensive functional testing beyond configuration state validation, remains with the organization. Establishing formal non-production environments, defining comprehensive test procedures, and formally updating organizational documentation, policies, and broader change management procedures are also customer responsibilities.

Description:
A3.2.3 Changes to organizational structure result in
a formal (internal) review of the impact to PCI DSS
scope and applicability of controls.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
A3.2.4 If segmentation is used, PCI DSS scope is
confirmed as follows:
• Per the entity’s methodology defined at
Requirement 11.4.1.
• Penetration testing is performed on
segmentation controls at least once every six
months and after any changes to segmentation
controls/methods.
• The penetration testing covers all segmentation
controls/methods in use.
• The penetration testing verifies that
segmentation controls/methods are operational

and effective, and isolate the CDE from all out-
of-scope systems.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) establishes robust network segmentation as a core design principle, leveraging VLANs, vSphere Distributed Switches, and NSX software-defined networking to create multiple layers of isolation. NSX provides dynamically configurable overlay-backed segments, Virtual Private Clouds (VPCs), and NSX Edge nodes for defining distinct network zones and north-south boundaries.

VMware vDefend enhances this with comprehensive microsegmentation capabilities through the Distributed Firewall, enforcing security policies at the vNIC level for individual workloads, independent of network placement. The vDefend Gateway Firewall provides stateful inspection, Network Address Translation (NAT), and VPN services at the network edge, ensuring secure traffic flow between segments and external networks. Together, these capabilities enable granular control over both East-West and North-South traffic, effectively isolating technology assets, applications, and services.

VCF Operations for Networks and vDefend Security Intelligence provide advanced tools for segmentation planning, including traffic flow analysis, asset grouping, and the generation of policy recommendations based on observed patterns. The Security Segmentation Report offers a quantitative measure of segmentation progress, while continuous Segmentation Monitoring detects new services and allows for the enforcement of lockdown rules.

Network health checks verify connectivity and configuration consistency across the infrastructure. Furthermore, vDefend's Distributed Deep Packet Inspection Engine, IDS/IPS, and Network Detection and Response (NDR) capabilities provide real-time traffic analysis, threat detection, and correlation of attack campaigns, offering continuous assurance of segmentation effectiveness and isolation of the CDE from out-of-scope systems.

While the platform provides the foundational segmentation controls and extensive monitoring and verification tools, the organization remains responsible for defining and documenting its segmentation methodology as required by PCI DSS 11.4.1. The customer must also procure, schedule, and execute penetration testing on these segmentation controls at least once every six months and after any significant changes, ensuring the testing scope comprehensively covers all segmentation methods in use. The formal confirmation that segmentation controls are operational, effective, and successfully isolate the CDE from out-of-scope systems, based on the penetration test results, is an organizational process.

Description:
A3.2.5 A data-discovery methodology is
implemented that:
• Confirms PCI DSS scope.
• Locates all sources and locations of cleartext
PAN at least once every three months and
upon significant changes to the CDE or
processes.
• Addresses the potential for cleartext PAN to
reside on systems and networks outside the
currently defined CDE.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides a comprehensive inventory of its managed infrastructure objects, supporting custom tagging and categorization to classify assets by regulatory jurisdiction, business unit, or compliance scope. Its visualization capabilities, including the Topology Graph Widget and NSX network topology views, graphically represent infrastructure boundaries, network segments, and object relationships, aiding in the understanding of the technical environment. VCF Operations continuously monitors infrastructure against industry standards and custom policies, identifying non-compliant objects. VMware vDefend, through Security Intelligence and Security Services Platform, offers structured asset inventory, hierarchical categorization, and tag-based classification, which can be aligned with compliance scope boundaries. Its visualization canvas interactively displays network security posture, traffic flows, and segmentation boundaries, delineating security zones and policy applicability. VMware Live Recovery maintains a detailed inventory of protected virtual machines and datastores, organizing them into protection groups and using storage policies and tags to identify assets under disaster recovery protection, contributing to a comprehensive asset register relevant for compliance.

While these platforms provide robust capabilities for asset inventory, categorization, and the visualization of infrastructure and network boundaries, they do not natively implement a data-discovery methodology for locating cleartext PAN. The platforms do not perform content inspection or sensitive data scanning to identify cleartext PAN on systems or networks, whether inside or outside the CDE. Organizations must define and implement a formal data-discovery methodology, including the selection and deployment of specialized tools capable of scanning for cleartext PAN. This methodology must confirm PCI DSS scope, execute cleartext PAN discovery at least once every three months and upon significant changes, and explicitly address the potential for cleartext PAN to reside on systems and networks beyond the currently defined CDE. The mapping of technical boundaries and asset classifications to formal compliance obligations and governance decisions remains an organizational responsibility.

Description:
A3.2.5.1 Data discovery methods are confirmed as
follows:
• Effectiveness of methods is tested.
• Methods are able to discover cleartext PAN on
all types of system components and file formats
in use.
• The effectiveness of data-discovery methods is
confirmed at least once every 12 months.
Response: End user activity or process, design decision, or activity out of scope.

Description:
A3.2.5.2 Response procedures are implemented to
be initiated upon the detection of cleartext PAN
outside the CDE to include:
• Determining what to do if cleartext PAN is
discovered outside the CDE, including its
retrieval, secure deletion, and/or migration into
the currently defined CDE, as applicable.
• Determining how the data ended up outside the
CDE.
• Remediating data leaks or process gaps that
resulted in the data being outside the CDE.
• Identifying the source of the data.
• Identifying whether any track data is stored with
the PANs.
Response:
VMware Cloud Foundation provides robust technical capabilities that support an organization's response to the detection of cleartext PAN outside the CDE. VCF offers containment mechanisms at the virtualization layer, including VM snapshots to preserve evidence for forensic analysis and NSX network segments and security groups to isolate affected workloads, thereby limiting further data exposure.

VCF Automation can create network profiles with isolation by subnet, VLAN transport zone, or security group, which are applicable for quarantining contaminated systems.

For monitoring, VCF Operations centralizes event data and compliance monitoring, aggregating log data from infrastructure components and forwarding events to enterprise SIEM platforms for correlation with spill investigation workflows. VCF Operations compliance management continuously monitors infrastructure against configured policies, detecting deviations that may indicate where a spill occurred or what systems were affected.

Data protection and recovery during spill remediation are supported by vSAN native snapshots for local recovery points and vSphere HA and vSphere Fault Tolerance for workload continuity when contaminated infrastructure requires remediation.

VMware vDefend further contributes to sensitive data spill response through its Security Intelligence, which monitors traffic flows and detects leaked flows not covered by existing Distributed Firewall rules, enabling administrators to identify unauthorized data movement between environments.

The Policy Recommendations engine analyzes these leaked flows and generates modified firewall rules to contain the spill by restricting communication for affected workloads, guiding administrators through monitoring traffic leakage, establishing exceptions, and implementing lockdown protections as part of the remediation workflow.

While VCF and vDefend provide essential technical tools for detection, containment, and recovery, the overarching response procedures, including determining specific actions for retrieval, secure deletion, or migration of cleartext PAN, remain organizational responsibilities.

The organization must define and implement policies and processes for determining how data ended up outside the CDE, remediating data leaks or process gaps, identifying the source of the data, and identifying whether any track data is stored with the PANs.

Broader data spill response activities, such as legal notification, evidence chain-of-custody procedures, data classification reassessment, forensic investigation beyond initial evidence preservation, and user communication, are outside the scope of these platforms and must be addressed through separate organizational policies and procedures.
